meta.title: Pink Oregon Trail
meta.amendment_v3_3: Strategy and pedagogy depth amendment. Adds 4-track scoring (Steward / Provider / Scholar / Pathfinder), Push-On Tax (consec_push_on counter with 4 thresholds), Smart-Stop bypass penalties (forts / rivers / landmarks), DYK exposure quotas, residual probabilities (3-15% on protections and shocks), and per-member personality vectors (5 hidden traits). The v3.3.0 ship is partial; content expansion (items 5-7, 9, 10), pedagogy UI (items 12-14), expandable map (item 15), and physical board redesign (item 16) ship in v3.3.x patches.

calendar.historical_note: The real Oregon Trail had brutal time pressure. Wagon trains that didn't reach Oregon by mid-October risked being trapped by mountain snow. The Donner Party in 1846 left too late — they were trapped in the Sierra Nevada and only half survived.

per_event_day_costs.philosophy: Real Oregon Trail events took TIME. A river crossing wasn't a 1-day affair — caulking and ferrying could take 2-3 days. Fort visits meant overnight stays. Landmark photos and journal entries meant the wagon stopped. Adding small day costs to events makes the calendar feel honest and the winter clock feel real.
per_event_day_costs.applies_when: After the event resolves, before the next Push On
per_event_day_costs.narrative_hook: Append to event resolution callouts: 'It cost us a day. The trail does not give time freely.'

difficulty_notes.philosophy: The game should feel real. Players should sometimes lose. Resources should feel scarce. The winter clock should create real urgency. A game where everyone always wins teaches nothing.

meta.notes: v3.1.1 quick-fix patch: (1) Day/night cycle stuck on dark — diagnose and force midday for finish scenes, (2) race_to_fort intervention choice should not appear when wagon is already at a fort, (3) regenerate the two paintable PDFs to fill the full page (current PDFs render in top-left corner only). v3.2 multiplayer (PeerJS room codes) follows as separate build.

stochastic_system.philosophy: Real pioneers did not see probability tables before making choices. They knew the river was high, the team was tired, the trail looked rough — and they decided. The game models this. Choice screens show qualitative risk and team advantages, never exact numbers. Outcomes sample from hidden distributions. Sometimes the dice fall well. Sometimes they don't. Profession bonuses shift the distribution but rarely eliminate risk entirely.

profession_residual_risk.philosophy: Even with the right profession on the team, real pioneers sometimes lost. Doctor cannot save every cholera victim. Carpenter cannot patch every breakdown.

simulation_system.philosophy: Resources are not independent counters. They form a coupled dynamical system where each resource affects the others, member states, and the wagon's effective capabilities. The historical 1-in-10 death rate emerges from system feedback, not from scripted scarcity.

healthy.description: Full strength. Profession modifier active. Normal food/water consumption.

weakened.description: Tired, slow, but still functional. Profession modifier still active but with -20% effectiveness.

sick.description: Coughing, fever. Profession modifier disabled. Doubled water consumption. Slows the wagon.

injured.description: Wounded. Profession modifier disabled. Slows the wagon. Heals slowly with rest or Doctor.

dying.description: Critical. High chance of death per turn unless healed.

state_transitions.philosophy: Members transition between states based on resource conditions and event outcomes. Transitions check at the end of every turn.

rules.note: Without basic supplies, life on the trail breaks down

rules.trigger: Doctor on team treats sick member
Doctor on team treats injured member
Doctor on team treats dying member
Rest action with adequate food + water
Rest action with adequate food + water
natural recovery (fed and watered for 3+ turns)

resource_consumption_per_turn.note: Total food consumed per turn = (alive members * 0.5 * ration_multiplier * sum_of_state_multipliers). With 7 healthy members on Meager rations: 3.5 food. On Bare Bones: 0 food (intentional starvation). On Filling: 7 food (burns fast).

resource_threshold_effects.philosophy: Beyond zero is not the only critical level. Approaching empty has consequences too.

food.below_3: Members may shift to weakened state at start of next turn (10% chance per healthy member)
food.below_0: Cannot go below 0 — clamp to 0 and apply starvation rules

water.below_2: Members may shift to weakened (15% chance per healthy member)
water.note: Water effects are stronger than food because dehydration kills faster than starvation in real life.

morale.below_3: 10% chance per turn that one member abandons the wagon (treated as a 'lost' member with special narrative)
morale.below_1: Argument event: 25% chance per turn the wagon delays a day (camp infighting)
morale.above_8: +1 movement on Push On (high morale, the team pushes hard)

health.philosophy: Health is now the WAGON's collective health, NOT an alive/dead binary. Used as a status indicator that affects pace and event severity.
health.below_3: Wagon takes -1 movement modifier (slow and battered)
health.at_0: Catastrophic failure: 50% chance to lose 1 random member outright, restore to 4. This is now a fallback only — most deaths come through state transitions, not health-zero.

death_rate_targets.philosophy: These are the targets for a balanced game across many plays. Tune the constants above if observed rates drift far from these.
death_rate_targets.note: On a 7-member team, losing 1-3 members is the historical norm. Losing zero is rare. Losing all 7 is rarer still.

path_divergence.philosophy: Two teams playing the same game with different professions and choices should end up in visibly different states. The system should reward divergent strategies.
divergence_drivers: Pace+Ration combinations create different resource trajectories
Profession choices create different protective coverage (some events save you, some don't)
Member states cascade — losing your Doctor early changes what subsequent events do to you
Stochastic outcomes mean identical events have different consequences
Surprise events fire rarely and add path-unique stories
Quiz performance at the bandit ambush creates win/loss divergence at the climax
anti_convergence_rules: No mid-game 'rubber-banding' that helps losing teams catch up
No automatic resource floor (resources can hit 0 and stay there)
Forts are the ONLY major recovery point — and they cost money/days
Doctor recovery is probabilistic, not guaranteed — sick members may die

professions.name: Carpenter
skills: reduce time loss when smth breaks

professions.name: Blacksmith
skills: Has better supplies for fixing stuff

professions.name: Scout
skills: Finds the way on the trail

professions.name: Language Teacher
skills: Can speak to the Native Americans

professions.name: Banker
skills: Gives small amounts of money to the crew

professions.name: Food Critic
skills: Detects if the food is spoiled before eaten

events.name: Cholera Outbreak
events.flavor: Cholera killed thousands along rivers
events.intro: A creeping sickness sweeps through camp before dawn.

narratives.default: By dawn, fever burns. By noon, {lost_member} the {lost_profession} cannot stand. By dusk, they are gone. We dig a grave at the side of the trail and carve their name into a stone. The wagon train continues — it must — but a part of us stays here.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} works through the dark hours — boiling water, mixing tinctures, holding cool cloths to fevered foreheads. By morning, the worst has passed. We lose a day to nursing the sick. But we are all here. {Doctor} gave us back to ourselves.
narratives.Cook: {Cook} smells it before anyone falls ill — the bad water from the last camp. They burn every soiled cloth, boil every drop, and forbid anyone from drinking the river. The fever comes but does not spread. We are weakened, but whole.

events.name: River Crossing Disaster
events.flavor: The wagon tipped and took half our supplies
events.intro: The river runs faster than it looked.

narratives.default: The oxen panic mid-current. The wagon LURCHES sideways. We watch helplessly as bundles of supplies tumble into the brown water and float away downstream. {lost_member} the {lost_profession} is swept off the wagon. We fish them out cold and white but breathing.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} sealed every seam with tar before we tried the crossing. The wagon rides high in the current — our supplies stay dry. We come up the far bank with everything we started with and one good story.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} reads the river like a map — pointing out the safest line, the slack water below the bend, the gravel bar that breaks the current. We cross slow but we cross whole. No one is lost.
narratives.Merchant: {Merchant} hails a passing wagon train with two ferries. After some haggling, our coins buy our way across — dry, safe, and slightly poorer. Better than the alternative.

events.name: Broken Wagon Wheel
events.flavor: A wheel broke-we are stopped
events.intro: Half a mile after a stretch of rocky trail, an alarming CRACK.

narratives.default: The right rear wheel splinters. The wagon jerks to a halt at an angle. Without a fix, we go nowhere. We make camp in the dust and wait for morning, hoping someone in our party can sort it out.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} steps down without a word, takes one look at the wheel, and reaches for the toolbox. By dusk, three new spokes are set, the rim is reseated, and the wheel turns true. We lose two hours, not two days. {Carpenter} is the reason we move tomorrow.
narratives.Blacksmith: {Blacksmith} examines the iron tire band, which has split. Builds a small forge from rocks and embers. Hammers the band whole, fits it back to a fresh wheel from our spares. Slow work, but solid work. The wagon will hold to Oregon.

events.name: Oxen Collapse
events.flavor: Our ox fell.We cannot move fast now.
events.intro: The lead ox staggers. Slows. And falls.

narratives.default: The ox is finished. We unhitch the others and try to move on, but the wagon drags. The whole train slows. We've lost real time today and we feel it.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} crouches by the ox, runs a hand along its neck, says something quiet. Lifts the yoke off, brings water in a hat. The ox stands shakily but it stands. {Cowboy} keeps it on its feet and us on our trail.

events.name: Food spoilage
events.flavor: The food has turned bad.
events.intro: The food has turned bad.

narratives.default: Maggots in the salt pork. The flour is sour. We lose three pounds of provisions to the dirt and watch the wagon master's face grow tight as he counts what's left.
narratives.Food Critic: {Food_Critic} smells trouble at supper and stops everyone before a bite is taken. We lose only what was already gone. The rest is sealed in fresh wax and packed deeper in the wagon.
narratives.Cook: {Cook} salvages most of it — pulls the good from the bad, salts what can be salted, dries strips of meat over the fire. We lose only one pound. The rest will hold to Fort Laramie.

events.name: Successful Hunt
events.flavor: We brought down a buffalo-meat for days.
events.intro: A herd of buffalo, dark and slow, on the prairie.

narratives.default: Someone in our party manages a shot. A young calf falls. Not the feast we hoped for, but enough meat for two days of cold dinners. Better than nothing.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} crawls through the grass for an hour, picks the wind, and drops a full-grown buffalo with one shot. The whole train eats hot meat tonight. There are songs around the fire. Even the children stop fidgeting.

events.name: Failed hunt
events.flavor: Nothing today. We go hungry.
events.intro: Hours pass with the rifle. Nothing.

narratives.default: Whoever drew the hunting straw came back empty-handed. The team grumbles. Stomachs growl. Tomorrow we eat less, walk farther, and hope.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} hunts all day and sees only one rabbit at sundown — a lean kill, but a kill. We will not eat well tonight, but we will eat. {Hunter}'s patience saved us from going hungry.

events.name: Severe storm
events.flavor: The storm tore through camp.
events.intro: The clouds turn black before noon. The wind comes hard.

narratives.default: Lightning splits the sky. The canvas tears. Two barrels are blown clean off the wagon and roll across the plain — gone. The team huddles soaked and shaken until the worst passes.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} reads the sky an hour before the rain — points to a hollow under the bluff. We make camp early and the worst of the storm passes us by. Wet, but whole. The bluff saved us.

events.name: Snake bite
events.flavor: He was bitten-swelling fast.
events.intro: A scream from the brush. {random_member} is struck.

narratives.default: We lay {lost_member} the {lost_profession} on the ground. The leg swells black. By dusk they are unconscious. By midnight they are gone. The wagon train pauses for a brief funeral and continues — it must.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} sees the bite, sees the snake's marks, knows it's not the worst kind. Cuts, sucks, ties off above the wound. By morning the swelling drops. The bitten member walks slow for a week but walks.

events.name: Thief in camp
events.flavor: We woke to missing supplies.

events.name: Contaminated water
events.flavor: The water made us ill.
events.intro: Half the camp drinks from the still pool by the trail.

narratives.default: By midnight, six are sick. We lose a day waiting for them to recover. The whole team weakens.
narratives.Cook: {Cook} dips a finger, smells, refuses to draw water from the still pool — points to the running creek a quarter mile up. No one drinks the bad water. No one falls sick.

events.name: Lost trail
events.flavor: We cannot find the path.
events.intro: The wagon ruts vanish in the rocks ahead.

narratives.default: We backtrack three times. We argue. We light the lantern early and make a cold camp where we shouldn't. Two days lost.
narratives.Guide: {Guide} reads the land like a book — points to a worn passage between two outcrops we'd missed. We're back on the right line within an hour. No time lost.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} climbs the high rock and sees the trail re-emerging in the valley below. We descend a steep slope, find the ruts again, and continue. Half a day saved.

events.name: Stampede
events.flavor: The animals ran wild.
events.intro: A noise like thunder. The earth shakes. Buffalo are running.

narratives.default: The herd splits around our wagons but two oxen panic and bolt. We catch them by nightfall but two crates were trampled flat. Salt and flour scattered to the wind.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} is on the lead ox before anyone realizes what's happening — shouting, slapping reins, holding the team steady. Not a thing is lost. The herd parts and is gone.

events.name: Injury accident
events.flavor: He fell beneath the wagon.
events.intro: {random_member} falls beneath the wagon as it lurches over a rut.

narratives.default: A leg is crushed. We splint it as best we can and lose three days while it heals. The team is grim.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} sets the leg cleanly, splints it tight, and has the patient walking on crutches by sundown. The wagon doesn't even slow.

events.name: Wagon fire
events.flavor: Fire took what we had.
events.intro: A spark from the cooking fire catches the canvas.

narratives.default: Flames take the cover before we can throw water. We lose the canvas, four blankets, and a barrel of flour. The night sky is too cold for what's left of our bedding.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} is up the wagon side with wet sacks before any of us understand what's happening. The fire is out before it spreads past the rear bow. We lose a corner of canvas and one blanket. Nothing more.

events.name: Mountain pass snow in
events.flavor: Snow blocks the way.
events.intro: We crest the pass at dawn and find it choked with snow.

narratives.default: We turn back and seek a lower route — two days of doubling back. The team is exhausted and hungry by the time we find a way through.
narratives.Guide: {Guide} knows another pass — lower, longer, but open. We add half a day instead of two. We sleep cold but we keep moving.

events.name: Starvation
events.flavor: We ration the last crumbs.
events.intro: We ration the last crumbs.

narratives.default: Each morning the wagon feels lighter and our legs feel heavier. The children stop asking for second helpings. By the third day, no one talks at all.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} disappears at first light and returns at dusk, two days running, with rabbits and a small deer. We stretch the meat with wild greens. We are weakened but we live.
narratives.Farmer: {Farmer} forages along every creek bed — wild onions, lamb's quarter, sour berries, even the inner bark of cottonwood. Not feasts, but food. We make it through.

events.name: Fort arrival
events.flavor: A fort at last-supplies and rest.
events.intro: The fort rises from the plain — log walls, a flag, smoke from chimneys.

narratives.default: We resupply at the trader's prices. The team gets a hot meal and a soft place to sleep. Hope returns to the camp.
narratives.Merchant: {Merchant} bargains with the trader for an hour. We come away with twice the supplies for the same coin. The trader shakes their head as we pull out — they've never seen anyone haggle like that.

events.name: Trade opportunity
events.flavor: They offer goods for trade.
events.intro: A wagon train heading east stops alongside ours. Bartering begins.

narratives.default: We trade what we don't need for what we do. Both sides feel slightly cheated, which is the sign of a fair trade.
narratives.Merchant: {Merchant} runs the negotiation like a riverboat captain. We come away with everything we wanted and a small bag of coffee they hadn't planned to part with. The other train's leader laughs and shakes their head.

events.name: Native American Aid
events.flavor: They showed us the way.
events.intro: A Pawnee family has made camp ahead of us. They wave us over.

narratives.default: They share dried buffalo and corn cakes. The children stare at our wagon and ours stare back. We part ways with full bellies and gentle hearts. The myths we were told back east were wrong.
narratives.Language Teacher: {Language_Teacher} speaks a few halting words of Pawnee — enough to make the family laugh. They feed us, fill our water barrels, and draw us a map showing a route around bad ground. {Language_Teacher} just changed our journey.

events.name: Heat wave
events.flavor: Heat drains us.
events.intro: The sun comes up and does not go down.

narratives.default: By noon the canvas is too hot to touch. The water in the barrels turns warm and sour. We rest under the wagons and lose half a day.
narratives.Cook: {Cook} rigs a cloth shade over the water barrels and digs a shallow pit to keep the supplies cool. We lose less than we feared.

events.name: Cold night
events.flavor: Frost bites hard.
events.intro: Frost on the canvas at dawn.

narratives.default: The cold cuts through every blanket. By morning two of the children are coughing. The team's spirits are low.
narratives.Seamstress: {Seamstress} works late by lamp-light, patching the worst of the canvas tears and stitching extra wool inside the children's coats. We sleep warm enough.

events.name: Broken axle
events.intro: A loud snap as we cross a deep rut.

narratives.default: The rear axle has split. We make camp on the spot, chop saplings, and try to fashion a replacement. We lose two days.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} unfastens the axle, finds straight oak in the spare lumber, and has a new axle shaped and mounted by sundown. We lose half a day.

events.name: Wagon stuck in mud
events.intro: The wheels sink to the hub.

narratives.default: We unload everything, lay branches under the wheels, and the whole train pushes. We free the wagon by dusk, exhausted and muddy.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} unhitches, repositions the team to pull from a different angle, slaps the lead ox, and shouts the right word. The wagon comes free in twenty minutes. We barely lose an hour.

events.name: Food contamination
events.intro: Rats in the flour. Mold in the bacon.

narratives.default: We cull what's spoiled and lose a fair share. The team rations carefully for the next week.
narratives.Food Critic: {Food_Critic} catches the spoilage at the first whiff. We lose only the worst, save the rest, and re-pack everything in tight wraps.

events.name: Hunting injury
events.intro: A bear, surprised at the creek, charges our hunter.

narratives.default: {lost_member} the {lost_profession} is mauled badly. The bear runs. We carry our wounded back to camp but they do not survive the night.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} cleans the wounds, stitches what can be stitched, drips willow bark tea past their lips through the night. By morning the fever breaks. They will scar but they will live.

events.name: Bandit encounter
events.intro: Three riders, faces wrapped, approach the camp at dusk.

narratives.default: They take what coin we have and leave. No one is hurt but the team's morale is broken for days.
narratives.Politician: {Politician} steps forward, hands open, voice calm. Talks for ten long minutes. The bandits leave with two cups of coffee and an apple, no coin. {Politician} has a gift the rest of us don't.

events.name: River shortcut success
events.intro: A narrow ford no one else has tried.

narratives.default: We make it across in forty minutes. The trail beyond is dry and clear. Two days saved, somehow.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} found this crossing — read the water, judged the depth, and led us through without a foot getting wet. {Scout} just earned their keep for the whole journey.

events.name: River shortcut failure
events.intro: The shortcut looked promising. The current was deeper than it looked.

narratives.default: We lose two crates of supplies in the rapids. The team makes the far bank wet, cold, and humbled.
narratives.Guide: {Guide} pulls us back from the worst of it just in time — we ford a quarter mile downstream where the river spreads thin. We lose nothing. {Guide} just saved this wagon.

events.name: Lost supplies
events.intro: We unpack at noon and find a barrel missing.

narratives.default: It must have fallen from the wagon hours back. By the time we walk back, it's gone — picked clean by who-knows-what. We lose what's lost.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} backtracks for an hour and finds the barrel right where it bounced off — no harm done. We lose only an hour.

events.name: Camp illness
events.intro: Half the camp wakes coughing.

narratives.default: We rest a day. The cough lingers a week. The team is weakened and slow.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} dispenses willow bark tea, brews chest poultices, and orders the worst-off into the wagon to ride for three days. By the end of the week the cough is gone.

events.name: Entertainment night
events.intro: A clear night, a warm fire, and tired bones.

narratives.default: Someone in our party tells a story. It's not great but it's a story, and after weeks on the trail, that's enough. The fire dies low. We sleep deep.
narratives.Musician: {Musician} plays the fiddle long into the night. Kids dance around the fire. Even the dour ones smile. The whole camp wakes the next morning lighter than they have in weeks.
narratives.Actor: {Actor} acts out the story of our journey so far — every disaster, every triumph, every grumpy oxen — and the camp laughs until the fire is embers. There's a place for foolery on the trail. {Actor} has it.

events.name: Argument in camp
events.intro: Two voices rise around the cook fire. Then five. Then everyone.

narratives.default: The fight is about nothing — rationing, pace, who took whose blanket. By morning no one is speaking. The team moves slow and silent for days.
narratives.Influencer: {Influencer} steps into the middle, makes a joke at exactly the right moment, somehow gets everyone laughing. The fight ends in friendship. The trail is long. We need each other.
narratives.Magician: {Magician} does a card trick — a little stage business with a coin and a kerchief. The crowd that gathers is the same crowd that was fighting. By the time the trick is over, the argument is forgotten.

events.name: Rain damage
events.flavor: None
events.intro: Three days of rain. The canvas leaks.

narratives.default: Two sacks of flour are ruined. The bedding is soaked. The whole camp smells like wet wool for a week.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} re-tars the seams between rains, re-pitches the canvas tight. Our supplies stay dry. Our bedding gets damp but never soaked. We come through the storm whole.

events.name: Trail shortcut found
events.intro: A faint track leading off through the sage.

narratives.default: We try it. It works. Two days saved, somehow. The wagon master gives quiet thanks.
narratives.Guide: {Guide} confirms the shortcut goes through — has heard of it from a returning party at the last fort. We take it without doubt and save two solid days. {Guide}'s knowledge is gold.

events.name: Final push energy
events.intro: The valley is in sight. Oregon is close.

narratives.default: Everyone walks faster. Even the oxen seem to feel it. We make extra miles today. We make extra miles tomorrow. We are almost there.
narratives.Musician: {Musician} plays a marching tune. The team sings along, ragged but real. The miles fly. Oregon is RIGHT THERE.

events.name: Buffalo Herd Encounter
events.intro: A vast brown sea of bison stretches to the horizon. Dust rises like smoke. The earth hums.
events.flavor: Buffalo herds once stretched to the horizon — millions strong. A single bull could feed a wagon for two weeks.

narratives.default: We take what we can — one calf, three sacks of meat hauled back to camp. The whole train eats well for a week.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} crawls a half-mile through grass with the wind in their face, picks the largest bull, and drops it with a single shot. Four hundred pounds of meat. The whole train eats hot and full for two weeks.
narratives.Trapper: {Trapper} fans out alongside, picks a young one, brings it down clean. Two animals, twice the meat. We're set until the next fort.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} rides into the herd at the gallop, cuts a young bull from the edge, and holds it for the rifle. The whole camp watches and cheers.

events.name: Childbirth on the Trail
events.intro: A cry in the night. {random_member} is in labor.
events.flavor: Many children were born along the Oregon Trail. Some pioneer families had two more by the time they reached Oregon than when they left.

modifiers.effect: no delay, mother and baby healthy

narratives.default: The whole camp stays up. By dawn there is a baby — small, red, wailing. We lose a day to rest. The team is tired but joyful. A new pioneer was born under prairie stars.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} stays with the laboring mother through the long night — calm, certain, sure. Mother and baby come through strong. We lose only a few hours of travel. The camp celebrates with a feast.
narratives.Seamstress: {Seamstress} brings clean cloths, warm water, and patient hands. The birth goes well. Mother and child are sleeping by midnight.

events.name: Wolves at the Camp
events.intro: Yellow eyes in the dark, just past the firelight. A low growl. Then another.
events.flavor: Wolves were a real fear at night camps. They rarely attacked people but could panic livestock and steal supplies.

narratives.default: Two oxen are bitten. One does not survive the night. The morning camp is shaken — wolves are not the storybook monsters, but they are not nothing.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} lights torches, fires warning shots, and rides the perimeter for hours. The wolves circle once and slink off into the dark. Not a single ox is touched.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} takes the lead wolf with a long shot from the firelight. The pack scatters. Camp morale is high — there's even talk of a wolf pelt.
narratives.Trapper: {Trapper} sets snares around the perimeter at dusk. The wolves catch the scent and move on. Quiet, professional, no fuss.

events.name: Pawnee Family Shares Camp
events.intro: Four families have made camp ahead of you. They wave you over and set out food.
events.flavor: The Pawnee, Shoshone, Cayuse, and other tribes routinely shared food and routes with emigrants. The myth of constant conflict is just that — a myth.

narratives.default: They share dried buffalo and corn cakes. The children stare at our wagon and ours stare back. We trade a tin of sugar for a bundle of pemmican. We part ways with full bellies. The myths we were told were wrong.
narratives.Language Teacher: {Language_Teacher} speaks a few halting words of Pawnee — enough to make the family laugh. They feed us, fill our water barrels, and draw a map showing a route around bad ground we'd have hit in two days. {Language_Teacher} just changed our journey.
narratives.Politician: {Politician} sits with the elder for an hour, speaking through gestures and patience. Real warmth grows between the camps. We leave with extra dried meat, fresh water, and a friend.

events.name: Lightning Strike
events.intro: A flash, a deafening crack — a tree splits in half a hundred feet from camp.
events.flavor: Plains thunderstorms were terrifying. Lightning killed pioneers and panicked livestock more than once.

narratives.default: The sky split open right above camp. One barrel struck and shattered. The team is shaken — the youngest will not stop crying.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} reads the storm an hour before it hits and moves the wagons under a low ridge. The lightning passes overhead but does no harm. The team sleeps shaken but whole.
narratives.Priest: {Priest} gathers the camp and prays through the storm in a low, steady voice. By the time the thunder fades, the children are quiet. Faith holds the team.

events.name: Wild Berry Patch
events.intro: A bend in the creek. Brambles thick with dark, ripe berries.
events.flavor: Wild berries were one of the great gifts of the trail — a sweet break from salt pork and hardtack.

narratives.default: We pick what we can carry. The kids' faces and hands are stained purple by sundown. Tonight there's berry cobbler. Small joys.
narratives.Farmer: {Farmer} knows which berries are good, which leaves are worth picking, which roots can be dried for later. We come away with twice what anyone else would have gathered.
narratives.Food Critic: {Food_Critic} taste-tests every variety, throws out the bad, dries the good. Tonight: cobbler. Tomorrow: berry preserves. The whole team eats like royalty.

events.name: Stranded Wagon
events.intro: A wagon by the trail, tilted, abandoned. Or so it seems. A small face peeks from the canvas.
events.flavor: Wagon trains often helped each other — and sometimes saved each other.

narratives.default: We give them food and a blanket and continue. Doing the right thing costs us — but the team rests easier knowing we didn't pass them by.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} unhitches, gets under the wagon, fixes the broken axle in two hours. The stranded family weeps with gratitude. We share a meal and continue. Both wagons west.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} treats their sick child through the night. By morning the fever has broken. We share supplies, share stories, and ride on together for two days. We didn't lose anything. We gained a friendship.

events.name: Dust Storm
events.intro: The horizon turns brown. A wall of dust, a hundred feet tall, rolling toward the camp.
events.flavor: Plains dust storms could blot out the sun for hours and bury supplies in inches of grit.

narratives.default: We hunker under the wagons. Sand in our teeth, in our eyes, in everything. By the time it passes, the trail is gone under drifts of dust. We lose half a day.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} sees it coming and rides the team into a shallow draw. The storm howls past us. We're back on the trail within an hour. {Scout} just saved a day.
narratives.Cook: {Cook} seals the water barrels with wax and cloth before the dust arrives. Not a drop is lost — and the team has clean water through the storm and after.

events.name: Sunday Service
events.intro: The seventh day. The wagon master calls a halt.
events.flavor: Most pioneer wagon trains rested and worshipped on Sundays — both for faith and for the oxen.

narratives.default: We rest. We mend clothes, water the oxen, write in our journals. The pause does us good. The week ahead feels possible again.
narratives.Priest: {Priest} reads scripture under the open sky, leads the camp in hymns. Even the doubters bow their heads. Something quiet and good settles over the whole camp. The week ahead doesn't feel quite so heavy.
narratives.Musician: {Musician} leads the hymns — voice and fiddle together, the kind of harmony you remember years later. Even the gruffest pioneer is wiping their eyes by the end.

events.name: Lost Child
events.intro: The youngest is missing. The whole camp is searching as the light fails.
events.flavor: Children sometimes wandered off at noon stops. Most were found. Some weren't.

narratives.default: We search until the moon is high. The child is found at last in a thicket, scratched and crying but alive. We hold them tight all night. We move slow the next morning.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} reads the tracks like a book — small footprints leading off into the grass. Finds the child asleep in a thicket within twenty minutes. The whole camp breathes again.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} knows the land like an animal — circles back to where the child was last seen, traces the wandering, finds them by a creek bed inside an hour. Crisis averted.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} mounts up immediately, rides a wide arc around camp at full gallop, and spots the child at the edge of a draw. Home before sundown.

events.name: Oxen Exhaustion
events.intro: The team is failing. Heads down, sides heaving, every step a struggle.
events.flavor: Oxen could only walk so many miles a day. Push them too hard and they died — and a wagon without oxen was a tomb.

narratives.default: We have to halt and let them rest two days. We lose ground we can't get back. The trail master grows quiet — he's seen this before.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} adjusts the yoke, redistributes the load, rotates the lead pair. The team perks up within hours. We barely lose half a day.
narratives.Farmer: {Farmer} forages along the trail edge — bringing back armloads of fresh grass, clover, wild oats. The oxen eat better than they have in weeks. They're plodding strong by tomorrow.

events.name: Gold Rush Rumors
events.intro: A rider comes from the south with wild eyes. Gold. California. Riches for the taking.
events.flavor: The 1848 California Gold Rush pulled many Oregon-bound emigrants south. Some struck rich. Most did not.

modifiers.effect: no morale loss, team unified on Oregon
no morale loss, team trusts the math

narratives.default: Half the camp wants to head south. Arguments rage past midnight. By morning we continue toward Oregon, but the team is fractured. Some will desert at the next trading post.
narratives.Politician: {Politician} gathers everyone under the night sky and gives a speech that nobody quite remembers but everyone feels. Oregon is the destination. The team holds together. The trail master grins.
narratives.Priest: {Priest} reminds the camp why we left — not for gold, but for land, family, a new start. The fever cools. By morning, no one wants to turn south. Faith is heavier than rumor.
narratives.Banker: {Banker} runs the numbers out loud — the cost of detour, the odds of striking gold, the tax on shipped bullion. By the end nobody believes the rumors anymore. Oregon it is.

events.name: Letter from Home
events.intro: A letter, somehow, miraculously, finds the wagon at the trading post.
events.flavor: Mail on the trail was rare and precious. A letter from home could change a pioneer's whole month.

narratives.default: Someone sits by the fire reading it aloud. There's news of a niece, a wedding, a death, a new church. We laugh and we cry. The wagon train continues, lighter.

events.name: Wagon Race Challenge
events.intro: Another wagon train pulls alongside ours. Their leader rides over with a grin. "Race you to the next ridge?"
events.flavor: Wagon trains sometimes raced for fun, for honor, for a shared meal. Sometimes the racing broke things.

narratives.default: We win by a hair. The other wagon master buys us coffee at the next fire. We lose a barrel that bounced off in the chase, but we gained a story we'll tell our grandchildren.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} drives the team like a man possessed but without losing control. We pull ahead by a half-mile and lose nothing. The other wagon master doffs his hat.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} re-strapped every barrel before the race. We tear over the ridge whole and intact. The other wagon master wants {Carpenter} for his own train.

events.name: Snake in the Wagon
events.intro: A scream from the wagon at first light. A rattler, coiled in the bedding.
events.flavor: Rattlesnakes routinely sheltered in pioneer wagons overnight. Most encounters ended without injury — but not all.

modifiers.effect: no death even if bite happens

narratives.default: {lost_member} the {lost_profession} is bitten before anyone fully wakes. The leg swells. By dusk, they are gone. We bury them by the trail.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} acts before the swelling spreads — cuts, draws, ties off, applies poultice. The team member sweats through the night but wakes alive. They walk slow for a week but they walk.
narratives.Trapper: {Trapper} hooks the snake with a forked stick, walks it off into the brush, releases it. No one is bitten. The whole camp is wide awake but safe.
narratives.Hunter: {Hunter} comes in with a heavy stick, dispatches the snake in a single swift motion. The wagon is searched twice over. Safe.

events.name: Toothache Becomes Infected
events.intro: {random_member} has been hiding the toothache for days. Now their face is swollen.
events.flavor: A simple toothache, untreated on the trail, could become deadly within a week.

modifiers.effect: tooth pulled, no delay, no death

narratives.default: There's nothing we can do but wait. Three days the suffering continues. They lose strength. By the fourth day, the worst happens. We bury them quietly.
narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} pulls the tooth with steady hands and the right tools. There's a brief, awful moment, and then relief. By morning the patient is sleeping comfortably. Crisis past.
narratives.Blacksmith: {Blacksmith} has pulled teeth before — back home, for cousins, for friends. The pliers are not delicate but they work. The patient recovers slowly but recovers.

events.name: Found Cache of Supplies
events.intro: An abandoned wagon by the trail, its load scattered.
events.flavor: Pioneers often had to lighten their wagons by abandoning supplies on the trail. Other emigrants found gold in their leavings.

modifiers.effect: extra food found in good condition

narratives.default: We sift through what's left — a sack of flour, a bolt of canvas, three good pots, a child's wooden toy. We take what's useful and continue. A small windfall on a hard road.
narratives.Merchant: {Merchant} sees value where others see junk. Rebraids the abandoned canvas, salvages every ounce of usable goods. We come away significantly richer than we expected.
narratives.Food Critic: {Food_Critic} sniffs every barrel and bag. Throws out the spoiled. Salvages the rest. We find a sealed cask of dried apples that's perfectly good. A real prize.

events.name: Homesickness
events.intro: The team is quiet around the fire. No one looks at anyone else. Eyes are wet.
events.flavor: Homesickness was a real and serious affliction on the trail. Some emigrants turned back. Some despaired. The team's spirit was as critical as its supplies.

narratives.default: The team grows distant. People stop talking at meals. Two of the children ask, in quiet voices, when they can go home. We keep going. There is no going back.
narratives.Musician: {Musician} starts a quiet tune by the fire — a song from back east, the kind everyone knows. One by one the team joins in. By the end of the song, eyes are dry and the camp feels like home again.
narratives.Actor: {Actor} tells stories — about home, about strangers we'll meet, about the houses we'll build. Stories that give the future a shape. By morning, the team is looking forward instead of back.
narratives.Priest: {Priest} reminds us that home is not a place but the people we love. We pray together. We weep together. By dawn, the sadness has turned to resolve. The trail goes on.
narratives.Influencer: {Influencer} tells the funniest, brightest stories anyone has ever heard around a fire. The team laughs until tears stream — different tears than the homesick ones. Morale rebounds.

events.title: A Wagon in Trouble
events.intro: We see a wagon stopped at the side of the trail. A family stands beside it, looking lost.
events.flavor: Their axle has snapped. They cannot continue without help.

narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} examines the broken axle and gets to work. By dusk, the wagon is moving again. The family presses three silver coins into our hands.
narratives.Blacksmith: {Blacksmith} has the iron and the strength to forge a brace. The family pays us in silver.
narratives.default: We offer what help we can but we have neither the tools nor the skill. The family thanks us and we move on.

events.title: A Buried Cache
events.intro: Something glints under the dirt at the side of the trail.
events.flavor: An emigrant must have buried valuables here, planning to return. They never did.

narratives.Scout: {Scout} spots the disturbed earth before anyone else. We dig and find a small leather pouch of coins.
narratives.default: We dig carefully. A small leather pouch of coins, left by some pioneer who came before.

events.title: A Sick Child in Another Wagon
events.intro: A man rides up at a gallop. His child is sick. He has heard there is a doctor with our train.
events.flavor: We could keep moving. We could stop. The child is small and pale.

narratives.Doctor: {Doctor} works through the afternoon. The fever breaks by sundown. The grateful father insists on paying — three silver coins, and a sack of flour.
narratives.default: We have no doctor among us. We share what we know of fevers and pray for the child. We continue without payment.

events.title: Music for the Camp
events.intro: A larger wagon train is camped nearby. They invite us to share their fire.
events.flavor: Our team's musician could play for them tonight.

narratives.Musician: {Musician} fiddles into the night. The other emigrants laugh, dance, and pass a hat. We earn two silver coins.
narratives.Actor: {Actor} tells stories around the fire — Shakespeare and tall tales mixed. Coins pressed into our hands.
narratives.default: We share their fire and their food. A pleasant evening. No payment, but no hardship either.

events.title: An Eastbound Trader
events.intro: A lone trader rides east on a tired horse. He stops to talk.
events.flavor: He is heading back to Independence and offers to buy whatever surplus we have.

narratives.Merchant: {Merchant} drives a hard bargain. We sell two units of supplies for four silver coins.
narratives.Politician: {Politician} talks the trader's price up. We sell two units of supplies for three silver coins.
narratives.default: We sell two units of supplies for two silver coins. Fair is fair.

events.title: A Lost Dog
events.intro: A small brown dog trots up to our wagon, wagging its tail.
events.flavor: There's a brass tag on its collar with a name and a fort address.

narratives.default: We feed the dog and bring it along. At the next fort, the owner — overjoyed — pays a reward of two silver coins.

events.title: A Bride's Dress
events.intro: We meet a small wedding party. The bride's dress has been torn on a thornbush.
events.flavor: They are desperate for someone who can mend it before the ceremony.

narratives.Seamstress: {Seamstress} mends the dress beautifully — better than new. The grateful family pays three silver coins.
narratives.Fashion Designer: {Fashion Designer} doesn't just mend it — they redesign the bodice. The bride weeps with joy. Five silver coins.
narratives.default: We try our best with our limited skill. The dress is wearable. The family thanks us with bread and a small coin.

events.title: Hire the Teacher
events.intro: A wealthy family in another wagon has children who need lessons.
events.flavor: They will pay good money for an afternoon of proper schooling.

narratives.Language Teacher: {Language Teacher} spends the afternoon teaching reading and Latin. The family pays four silver coins.
narratives.Priest: {Priest} teaches scripture and letters. The family pays three silver coins.
narratives.default: We have no one suited for proper teaching. We move on.

events.title: A Portrait Commission
events.intro: A wealthy emigrant family wants a portrait painted before they reach Oregon.
events.flavor: They have heard our team includes an artist.

narratives.Artist: {Artist} paints them through the afternoon, capturing their hopes and weariness both. They pay six silver coins — a small fortune.
narratives.default: We have no artist among us. The family is disappointed.

events.title: An Evening of Wonder
events.intro: Several wagons are camped together at a watering hole. The mood is somber.
events.flavor: Children are crying. People are tired.

narratives.Magician: {Magician} performs by firelight — coins from ears, scarves from sleeves, a card trick that fools even the trail-hardened. By the end the children are laughing. Three silver coins.
narratives.Influencer: {Influencer} gathers everyone and tells stories of the great wide world. Two silver coins for the show.
narratives.default: We share their somber camp. Tomorrow comes with dawn.

events.title: Feeding the Train
events.intro: A wagon train of two dozen has run out of trail cooks.
events.flavor: They will pay for someone who can feed them well.

narratives.Cook: {Cook} works miracles with limited supplies — biscuits, bean stew, fried sage. Four silver coins.
narratives.Food Critic: {Food Critic} doesn't cook, but advises and tastes. The meal turns out wonderful. Two silver coins.
narratives.default: We help where we can. Bread and beans. They pay one silver coin and a sack of flour.

events.title: Horses Need Shoeing
events.intro: A traveling cattle drover meets us on the trail. His horses need new shoes badly.
events.flavor: He has coin and is desperate.

narratives.Blacksmith: {Blacksmith} sets up the forge. By sundown, six horses are shod. Five silver coins and a hot meal.
narratives.default: We point him toward the next fort and continue on.

resources.name: Food
resources.description: Used to feed the team each turn
resources.notes: v3.2 — units are pounds. Cap raised to 999 because the calibrated economy starts you at 140-240 lbs and a Hunt nets 25 lbs per success; the old cap of 20 made forage gains and hunts no-op.

resources.name: Water
resources.notes: v3.2 — units are gallons. Cap raised to 99 because the calibrated economy starts you at 16-30 gallons and forage/Scout finds add up; the old cap of 20 silently truncated all subsequent water gains.

resources.name: Supplies
resources.notes: v3.2 — cap 15->20 for parity with V32_STARTING easy preset (8 supplies). Used to avoid delays or losses.

resources.name: Hides
resources.description: Animal skins from successful hunts. Sell at forts for cash.
resources.notes: Pure trade good — has no consumption effect.

resources.name: Medicine
resources.description: Doses for treating illness on the trail. One dose treats one minor illness; severe illness takes two doses (one with Doctor).
resources.loss_sources: Treating sick or weakened members; severe illness events
resources.notes: v3.2 — required for the new sickness/cholera shock handlers. Without medicine, sick members may not recover.

resources.name: Money
resources.description: Used for trading and ferry crossing

resources.name: Morale
resources.notes: Low morale increases risk in events

resources.name: Movement
resources.notes: Usually 1 per space, can be boosted

short.name: Independence, Missouri
short.info: April, 1848. The 'jumping-off place' is bursting with thousands of wagons and oxen and dreams. The whole country smells of mud, livestock, and fresh-cut wood. Today you sign your name in the ledger and turn your wagon west. There is no road. There is only the trail others have made before you. Behind you is everything you have ever known. Ahead lies 2,000 miles of America.

short.name: Kansas Prairie
short.info: The grass is shoulder-high in places. The wagon train stretches behind you to the horizon.

short.name: Kansas River Crossing
short.info: Your first real river. The Kansas runs wide and brown, swirling with spring melt. Some wagons line up for the ferry. Others — those with thinner pockets — wade in and pray. Will you ford the current, seal your wagon and float, or pay coin for safe passage?

short.name: Big Blue River
short.info: No ferry here. No choice but to cross under your own power. The Big Blue is named for its color but today it runs muddy and fast. You can ford it or seal your wagon and float. Choose wrong and supplies — or people — go downstream.

short.name: Fort Kearney
short.info: The first fort. A square of log walls in a sea of grass, built in 1848 to protect the trail. Soldiers in blue. A blacksmith's hammer ringing. A trader with bolts of cloth and barrels of flour. This may be your last chance to mail a letter home for a year. Maybe forever.

short.name: Platte River Valley
short.info: The Platte River runs beside the trail for hundreds of miles. Pioneers said it was 'a mile wide and an inch deep' — too thick to drink, too thin to plow.

short.name: The Great Plains
short.info: Endless grass in every direction. No tree in sight. The sky is bigger here than you have ever seen the sky.

short.name: Chimney Rock
short.info: After weeks of flat prairie, a stone spire rises from the earth — 325 feet of pale rock pointing at the sky. Pioneers wrote about Chimney Rock in their journals more than any other landmark on the trail. Seeing it means you have come a long way. It also means the easy part is ending.

short.name: Scotts Bluff
short.info: Towering bluffs that look like the ramparts of a buried castle. The Lakota people held this place sacred long before any wagon train passed by. The trail must wind around them — straight through is impossible.

short.name: Fort Laramie
short.info: The biggest fort on the trail. Once a fur-trading post, now an army outpost — a real town in the wilderness with a sutler's store, a bakery, and other travelers heading every direction. Repair your wagon. Trade for fresh oxen. Listen for news of the trail ahead — there are always rumors.

short.name: Mountain Foothills
short.info: The land starts to rise. The air gets thinner. The oxen breathe harder.

short.name: Independence Rock
short.info: The 'Great Register of the Desert.' A granite dome the size of a football field, covered in carved names. Five thousand pioneers have signed this rock — and you might too. Reach it by July 4th and you are on schedule for Oregon. Reach it later, and the mountains will catch you in the snow.

short.name: Devil's Gate
short.info: A narrow gorge carved 370 feet deep through solid rock by the Sweetwater River. The wagon trail must go around — no wagon can fit through. But you can climb up and look. It is one of the most beautiful and frightening places on the trail.

short.name: South Pass
short.info: You have crossed the Continental Divide. From this moment forward, every river flows toward the Pacific. The pass is so gentle and gradual that you almost miss it — yet you have just walked over the spine of America. You are in Oregon Country now.

short.name: Green River Crossing
short.info: Swift, deep, and dangerous. Three to five ferries operate here in peak season because so many people drown trying to ford. The river runs cold from mountain snow. The choice is yours, but think hard.

short.name: Soda Springs
short.info: Naturally carbonated mineral water, bubbling up from the ground. Pioneers wrote about it with wonder — water that fizzed in your cup like nature's own soda pop. The team's spirits lift. You are going to make it.

short.name: Fort Hall
short.info: A British trading post built by an American. Last major resupply before the Snake River country, the most punishing stretch of trail. Buy what you need now. There will be no more chances for a long, hard while.

short.name: Snake River Crossing
short.info: The most treacherous river on the trail. The Snake runs cold and fast through deep canyons. Many wagons have been lost here. Shoshone guides know the safe places to cross — they will help, usually in exchange for trade goods. Choose carefully.

short.name: Snake River Plain
short.info: Dry, hot, dusty country. The river runs in a canyon hundreds of feet below — you can hear it but you cannot reach it. Water is the problem now, not food.

short.name: Fort Boise
short.info: Last fort before the Blue Mountains. Hudson's Bay Company runs this one. The traders here have seen wagons arrive in every condition — and some not arrive at all. Stock up. The mountains are next.

short.name: Blue Mountains
short.info: Steep, forested mountains. The trail climbs and falls and climbs again. Wagons sometimes have to be lowered down rock faces with ropes. The oxen stagger. Every step uses everything they have.

short.name: Whitman Mission
short.info: Marcus and Narcissa Whitman built this mission in 1836 to serve emigrants and the Cayuse people. A waypoint near journey's end — a chance to rest before the final push. The garden here grows real vegetables. The first you have seen in months.

short.name: The Dalles
short.info: Where the trail meets the Columbia River. From here, you can raft your wagon down the river — fast but very dangerous — or pay the toll for the Barlow Road around Mount Hood. There is no easy way to Oregon City. There never has been.

short.name: Columbia River
short.info: The final water. Your wagon, lashed to a raft, on a wild river — or the Barlow Road, slow and steep but safer. The end is in sight. Don't lose it now.

short.name: Oregon City
short.info: You made it. The Willamette Valley stretches before you — green hills, fresh streams, soil so rich you could grow anything. The journey is over. The new life begins. You came 2,000 miles to stand here. Take a breath. You will never forget this day.

extended.name: Independence, Missouri
extended.info: Starting point. April 1848. Your team is ready, your wagon loaded with 2,000 pounds of supplies.

extended.name: Shawnee Mission
extended.info: A school and trading post run by Methodist missionaries serving the Shawnee. Last sight of any town for hundreds of miles.

extended.name: Kansas River Crossing
extended.info: Your first major river. Will you ford, float, or pay the ferry?

extended.name: Alcove Spring
extended.info: A picturesque camping site with sweet, cold water. Tempting to linger — but the season is short.

extended.name: Big Blue River
extended.info: No ferry available. You must ford or caulk and float.

extended.name: Fort Kearney
extended.info: First major fort on the trail (1848). Resupply, repair, send letters home.

extended.name: Buffalo Country
extended.info: Vast herds once stretched to the horizon. A successful hunt could feed your team for weeks.

extended.name: South Platte Crossing
extended.info: Wide and shallow but with quicksand bottoms. Cross carefully.

extended.name: Ash Hollow
extended.info: A green oasis in dry country. Many emigrants buried their dead here.

extended.name: Courthouse and Jail Rocks
extended.info: Towering rock formations that look like a courthouse and a jail. Travelers used them to gauge progress.

extended.name: Chimney Rock
extended.info: A 325-foot natural spire — the most-mentioned landmark in pioneer journals.

extended.name: Scotts Bluff
extended.info: Massive bluffs marking the Wyoming border. Sacred to the Lakota.

extended.name: Fort Laramie
extended.info: Major fur-trade post turned army fort. Trade, repair, news.

extended.name: Register Cliff
extended.info: Where emigrants carved their names into soft sandstone. Some signatures from 1843 are still visible.

extended.name: Independence Rock
extended.info: The Great Register of the Desert. If you reach this by July 4th, you'll make Oregon before winter.

extended.name: Devil's Gate
extended.info: A dramatic 370-foot gorge carved by the Sweetwater River.

extended.name: Sweetwater River
extended.info: You'll cross this river up to nine times. Calm water, good camping.

extended.name: Ice Slough
extended.info: A peat bog where ice could be found just below the surface even in summer.

extended.name: South Pass
extended.info: The Continental Divide. So gentle most travelers didn't notice. Now you're in Oregon Country.

extended.name: Pacific Springs
extended.info: The first spring where water flows west toward the Pacific.

extended.name: Big Sandy Creek
extended.info: A short crossing but a vital one — leads to the Green River.

extended.name: Green River Crossing
extended.info: Treacherous in summer. Three to five ferries operated here.

extended.name: Fort Bridger
extended.info: Trading post built by mountain man Jim Bridger in 1843. Trail splits here for California.

extended.name: Soda Springs
extended.info: Naturally carbonated mineral springs. A wonder of the trail.

extended.name: Fort Hall
extended.info: Built 1834. Last major resupply before the Snake River country.

extended.name: Snake River Country
extended.info: Hot, dry, and difficult. Water is scarce despite the river running through deep canyons.

extended.name: Three Island Crossing
extended.info: Considered the safest Snake River crossing — three islands break the current.

extended.name: Hot Springs
extended.info: Natural hot springs near the trail. A welcome rest.

extended.name: Snake River Crossing
extended.info: Shoshone guides may help cross in exchange for trade goods.

extended.name: Fort Boise
extended.info: Last fort before the Blue Mountains. Hudson's Bay Company post.

extended.name: Burnt River Canyon
extended.info: Steep and rocky. Hard on wagons and oxen alike.

extended.name: Grande Ronde Valley
extended.info: A beautiful green valley — a brief reward before the Blue Mountains.

extended.name: Blue Mountains
extended.info: Steep forested climbs. Wagons sometimes had to be lowered by ropes.

extended.name: Whitman Mission
extended.info: Mission of Marcus and Narcissa Whitman. A waypoint near journey's end.

extended.name: The Dalles
extended.info: Where the trail meets the Columbia. Decision time: raft the river, or take the Barlow Road around Mt. Hood?

extended.name: Columbia River
extended.info: Raft your wagon down the rapids, or pay the toll for the Barlow Road.

extended.name: Oregon City
extended.info: Journey's end. You've crossed 2,000 miles to reach the Willamette Valley.

pace_options.name: Steady
pace_options.description: Normal traveling pace, about 15 miles per day. Conservative and safe.

pace_options.name: Strenuous
pace_options.description: Push the oxen harder, 18-20 miles per day. Tiring but covers more ground.

pace_options.name: Grueling
pace_options.description: Maximum speed. 20+ miles per day. Real risk of exhaustion and illness.

ration_options.name: Filling
ration_options.description: Eat well. Strong morale and recovery, but food disappears fast.

ration_options.name: Meager
ration_options.description: Standard rations. Sustainable for the long haul.

ration_options.name: Bare Bones
ration_options.description: Survival rations. Stretches food, but the team will weaken.

turn_actions.name: Push On
turn_actions.description: Roll dice and advance down the trail. The default action.

turn_actions.name: Hunt
turn_actions.description: Spend the day hunting. Skip movement. Roll for food: gain 1d4 food normally, +3 if Hunter on team, +2 if Trapper on team.

turn_actions.name: Rest
turn_actions.description: Spend the day camped. Skip movement. Restore +2 Health and +1 Morale. +1 extra Health if Doctor on team.

turn_actions.name: Forage
turn_actions.description: Search nearby land for wild food and water. Skip movement. Gain 1d3 food, +2 if Farmer on team, +1 water if Scout on team.

fort_options.name: Buy Supplies
fort_options.description: Trade money for goods. 2 Money = 3 Supplies, or 2 Money = 4 Food, or 2 Money = 4 Water.

fort_options.name: Repair Wagon
fort_options.description: Spend 1 Supply to repair wear and tear. Restores Movement to maximum.

fort_options.name: Rest at Fort
fort_options.description: Spend 1 day resting. Gain +2 Health, +2 Morale.

fort_options.name: Get Trail News
fort_options.description: Talk to travelers and trappers. Reveals what type of event the next 3 spaces hold.

fort_options.name: Sell Goods
fort_options.description: Trade your goods for cash. Each hide sells for 3 Money. Each food unit beyond 5 sells for 1 Money. Each water unit beyond 5 sells for 1 Money. Each supplies unit beyond 3 sells for 1 Money.
fort_options.narrative: The trader looks over your goods. After some haggling, you settle on a fair price.

fort_options.name: Hire On for the Day
fort_options.description: Spend 1 day working at the fort. Each able-bodied team member earns about 1 Money. Costs 1 day of travel time and -1 Morale.
fort_options.narrative: Hard, honest day's work — but we lose ground.

modifier.effect: +2 money (skilled labor in demand at frontier forts)

fort_options.name: Move On
fort_options.description: Skip the fort and continue traveling.

river_options.name: Ford the River
river_options.description: Drive your wagon across. Free, but risky.

river_options.name: Caulk and Float
river_options.description: Seal the wagon to make it float. Safer, but uses supplies.

river_options.name: Hire a Ferry
river_options.description: Pay for safe passage. Costs money.
river_options.availability: Most rivers, not all (no ferry at Big Blue or Snake)

river_options.name: Native Guide
river_options.description: Hire a Shoshone or other Native American guide. Safest option, paid in trade goods.

modifier.effect: no cost — guide accepts your gratitude

river_options.availability: Snake River and a few western crossings
river_options.historical_note: Native guides routinely helped emigrants cross dangerous rivers, often for trade goods. This was one of many ways indigenous peoples aided the migration.

historical_facts.title: 2,000 Miles on Foot
historical_facts.fact: The Oregon Trail was about 2,000 miles long — roughly the same distance as walking from New York City to Denver. Most pioneers walked the entire journey. Only the very young, the very old, and the sick rode in the wagon. By the time families reached Oregon, many had worn through three or four pairs of shoes.
historical_facts.quiz_question: How long was the Oregon Trail?
historical_facts.correct: About 2,000 miles

historical_facts.title: Why Leave in April?
historical_facts.fact: Wagon trains had to leave Independence, Missouri by mid-April. Any later and the prairie grass wouldn't be tall enough to feed the oxen. Any later than that and you'd hit the Rocky Mountains in late summer — and the snows could close the passes by October. Timing was life and death.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why did wagon trains have to leave Independence by mid-April?
historical_facts.correct: To reach Oregon before winter snows closed the mountain passes
wrong: To avoid summer heat in the mountains
To beat the rainy season in Oregon
To meet the U.S. Army for protection

historical_facts.title: Cholera: The Trail Killer
historical_facts.fact: Cholera killed more pioneers than anything else on the Oregon Trail. It spread through dirty water at busy river crossings. The terrifying thing was its speed — a strong, healthy person could feel a stomach ache at breakfast and be dead by sundown. About 1 in 10 emigrants died on the trail. Most are buried in unmarked graves you can still find today.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What was the #1 killer on the Oregon Trail?
historical_facts.correct: Cholera

historical_facts.title: The Great Register of the Desert
historical_facts.fact: Independence Rock in Wyoming is a 130-foot granite dome the size of a football field. Pioneers carved their names into it as they passed. Reaching it by July 4th meant you were on schedule for Oregon. Today, more than 5,000 names from the 1840s and 1850s are still visible in the stone — a 175-year-old guest book of the American West.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What landmark did pioneers carve their names into?
historical_facts.correct: Independence Rock

historical_facts.title: Buffalo: The Plains' Living Pantry
historical_facts.fact: When pioneers crossed the Great Plains, vast herds of American bison stretched to the horizon — sometimes 30 million animals. A single buffalo gave 350 to 500 pounds of meat. Pioneers also burned dried buffalo dung — called 'buffalo chips' — for cooking fires when there was no wood. By 1900, hunting had reduced the herds to fewer than 1,000 animals.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Approximately how much meat did a single buffalo provide?
historical_facts.correct: 350 to 500 pounds

historical_facts.title: The Truth About Native Americans
historical_facts.fact: Movies show pioneers fighting Native American warriors at every turn. The truth is the opposite: Native peoples helped emigrants far more than they harmed them. The Pawnee, Shoshone, Cayuse, and many other tribes traded food, guided river crossings, and shared knowledge of the land. Without their help, even more pioneers would have died on the trail.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Did Native American tribes mostly help or harm pioneers?
historical_facts.correct: Mostly helped — they traded food, guided crossings, and shared land knowledge
wrong: Mostly harmed — frequent attacks were common
They charged tolls to pass through their land

historical_facts.title: South Pass: The Hidden Doorway
historical_facts.fact: South Pass crosses the Continental Divide at 7,400 feet — but the climb is so gentle and gradual that most pioneers didn't even realize they had crossed it. Yet on one side, every river flows east toward the Atlantic. On the other, every river flows west toward the Pacific. South Pass is the secret reason wagon travel to Oregon was possible at all.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What is special about South Pass?
historical_facts.correct: It crosses the Continental Divide so gently most pioneers didn't notice
wrong: It is the steepest pass on the trail
It was built by the U.S. Army
It was named after a famous explorer named South

historical_facts.title: Not the Wagons You're Thinking Of
historical_facts.fact: The big covered wagons in movies are 'Conestoga' wagons — but they were too heavy for the Oregon Trail. The real Oregon Trail wagon was the 'prairie schooner' — only about 4 feet wide and 10 feet long, with a canvas cover. It could only carry 2,000 pounds. Families had to make hard choices: take the piano or the plow? Take grandmother's china or another sack of flour? Most heirlooms ended up abandoned beside the trail.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What was a 'prairie schooner'?
historical_facts.correct: A small canvas-covered wagon used on the Oregon Trail

historical_facts.title: The Loneliest Mailbox
historical_facts.fact: Forts on the trail were the only places to mail letters home. A letter from Fort Laramie might take six months to reach a mother back in Ohio — and you might never know if it arrived. Many pioneer families never saw their relatives again after they left. Each fort visit was a chance to write what could be a final goodbye.
historical_facts.quiz_question: How long could a letter take to reach home from a fort on the trail?
historical_facts.correct: About six months

historical_facts.title: The Great Migration
historical_facts.fact: Between 1840 and 1860, somewhere between 300,000 and 500,000 people walked west on the Oregon Trail. Some went to Oregon to farm. Some peeled off to California for the Gold Rush. Some headed to Utah. It was one of the largest peaceful migrations in human history — and it permanently changed America.
historical_facts.quiz_question: About how many people walked west on the Oregon Trail between 1840 and 1860?

historical_facts.title: How Much Could You Carry?
historical_facts.fact: A prairie schooner held 2,000 pounds — and that had to feed and clothe a family for six months. A typical load: 600 pounds of flour, 200 pounds of bacon, 100 pounds of sugar, 75 pounds of coffee, 50 pounds of salt, plus rifles, ammunition, blankets, tools, and one set of nice clothes for arrival. Every extra pound slowed the oxen. Every missing pound risked starvation.
historical_facts.source: End of the Oregon Trail Interpretive Center
historical_facts.quiz_question: About how much weight could a prairie schooner wagon carry?
historical_facts.correct: About 2,000 pounds

historical_facts.title: The Donner Party Warning
historical_facts.fact: In 1846, the Donner Party left for California with 87 people. They tried a 'shortcut' that turned out to be slower. By the time they reached the Sierra Nevada mountains in late October, snow had closed the passes. Trapped for the winter without food, only 48 of them survived. Their story became the most famous warning of why timing mattered on the trail.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What happened to the Donner Party in 1846?
historical_facts.correct: They were trapped by mountain snow in the Sierra Nevada
wrong: They got lost in the desert

historical_facts.title: Why Oxen, Not Horses?
historical_facts.fact: Most Oregon Trail wagons were pulled by oxen, not horses. Oxen were stronger, ate any grass they could find, kept going on bad days, and weren't worth stealing. They moved slowly — only about 2 miles per hour — but they kept moving. Horses were faster, but they needed expensive grain, broke down on rough ground, and were prized targets for theft.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why did most Oregon Trail wagons use oxen instead of horses?
historical_facts.correct: Oxen were stronger, ate grass, didn't break down, and weren't worth stealing

historical_facts.title: Manifest Destiny
historical_facts.fact: 'Manifest Destiny' was the 1840s idea that the United States was destined to expand from coast to coast. It motivated thousands of pioneers — but it also justified taking land from Native peoples and from Mexico in the war of 1846-1848.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What was 'Manifest Destiny'?
historical_facts.correct: The 1840s belief that the U.S. was destined to expand from coast to coast
wrong: A railroad company in the West
A treaty with Native American tribes

historical_facts.title: The Oregon Treaty of 1846
historical_facts.fact: Until 1846, the Oregon Country was jointly claimed by Britain and the United States. The Oregon Treaty drew the border at the 49th parallel — the same border between the U.S. and Canada today. Pioneers leaving in 1848 had only just settled which country they were walking to.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Until 1846, which two countries jointly claimed the Oregon Country?
historical_facts.correct: The United States and Britain

historical_facts.title: What Did a Wagon Cost?
historical_facts.fact: A fully outfitted wagon for the Oregon Trail cost between $500 and $1,000 in 1848 dollars — roughly $20,000 to $40,000 today. That included the wagon, oxen, food, tools, weapons, and clothing for the whole journey. For most pioneer families, it was every dollar they had.
historical_facts.quiz_question: About how much did a fully outfitted Oregon Trail wagon cost in 1848 dollars?

historical_facts.title: Women on the Trail
historical_facts.fact: Women on the Oregon Trail did everything men did — drove wagons, hunted, made repairs, doctored — plus cooking, sewing, childcare, and laundry, often while pregnant. Their journals are some of our best historical records of the journey. Many women initially didn't want to go; their husbands had decided. By Oregon, many felt the journey had changed them.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What roles did women have on the Oregon Trail?
historical_facts.correct: They did everything men did, plus cooking, childcare, and laundry — often while pregnant
wrong: They mostly stayed in the wagons and didn't work
They led the wagon trains as official 'wagon mothers'
Most pioneer wagon trains had no women

historical_facts.title: Children of the Trail
historical_facts.fact: About 1 in 5 pioneers on the Oregon Trail was a child. Children walked, drove oxen, gathered buffalo chips for fires, hunted small game, and watched siblings. Many children died of cholera or accidents. Some pioneer families lost more than half their children before reaching Oregon.
historical_facts.quiz_question: About what fraction of Oregon Trail pioneers were children?
historical_facts.correct: About 1 in 5

historical_facts.title: Why the Mountains Have Less Air
historical_facts.fact: South Pass crosses the Rocky Mountains at 7,400 feet. At that altitude, the air contains about 25% less oxygen than at sea level. Pioneers got tired faster, oxen breathed harder, and fires burned cooler — all because of physics they couldn't have explained at the time.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why is breathing harder at higher altitudes like South Pass?
historical_facts.correct: There is less oxygen in the thinner mountain air
wrong: The air is colder, which slows breathing
The wind is stronger and blows the air away
Mountains have toxic gases from underground

historical_facts.title: The Indian Removal Act
historical_facts.fact: While pioneers were heading west, the U.S. government was forcing Native American tribes off their eastern lands under the 1830 Indian Removal Act. The Trail of Tears — when Cherokee, Creek, Choctaw, Chickasaw, and Seminole peoples were forced to walk to Oklahoma — happened just years before the Oregon Trail boom. The two trails were happening at the same time.
historical_facts.source: National Museum of the American Indian
historical_facts.quiz_question: What was the 'Trail of Tears'?
historical_facts.correct: When Native American tribes were forced to walk to Oklahoma in the 1830s
wrong: A river that pioneers had to cross
A song sung by pioneer women

historical_facts.title: Buffalo Chips for Fire
historical_facts.fact: Most of the Oregon Trail had no trees. Pioneers burned 'buffalo chips' — dried buffalo dung — for cooking fires. It was a job often given to children. The chips burned hot and clean. Pioneer women joked that biscuits cooked over buffalo chips tasted exactly the same as biscuits cooked over wood.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What did pioneers burn for fires when there were no trees?
historical_facts.correct: Buffalo chips (dried buffalo dung)
wrong: Their leftover wood from the wagon

historical_facts.title: Faith on the Trail
historical_facts.fact: Most Oregon Trail pioneers were religious — Methodist, Baptist, Catholic, Presbyterian, and others. Sunday was usually a rest day for both worship and giving the oxen a break. Wagon trains held church services in the open prairie, with hymns sung by hundreds of voices. Some pioneers described these as the most beautiful religious experiences of their lives.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What did most pioneer wagon trains do on Sundays?
historical_facts.correct: Rested and held church services
wrong: Pushed extra hard to make up time
Stayed in their wagons all day
Hunted to stock up on meat

historical_facts.title: Jumping-Off in Independence
historical_facts.fact: Independence, Missouri became the main 'jumping-off place' for the Oregon Trail starting around 1841. By 1848, the year of this game, the population swelled every spring with thousands of waiting wagons. Local merchants made fortunes selling supplies. The town smelled of livestock, fresh-cut wood, and hope.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What year was the Oregon Trail's main 'jumping-off place' fully established?
historical_facts.correct: Around 1841 (in Independence, Missouri)

historical_facts.title: The Whitman Mission
historical_facts.fact: Marcus and Narcissa Whitman established a mission in 1836 near present-day Walla Walla, Washington. Narcissa was one of the first white American women to cross the Rockies. The mission helped many emigrants but ended tragically in 1847 when measles brought by the wagon trains killed many Cayuse children, leading to a violent confrontation. The Whitmans and twelve others died.
historical_facts.source: National Park Service — Whitman Mission NHS
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why did the Whitman Mission end in tragedy in 1847?
historical_facts.correct: Measles brought by emigrants killed Cayuse children, leading to violence
wrong: A flood washed the mission buildings away
The Whitmans abandoned it for California gold
The U.S. Army closed it down

quiz_variants.question: Who was Narcissa Whitman?
quiz_variants.correct: One of the first white American women to cross the Rocky Mountains

quiz_variants.question: What disease was brought by emigrants and devastated the Cayuse near the Whitman Mission?

historical_facts.title: Fort Laramie — The Halfway Mark
historical_facts.fact: Fort Laramie in present-day Wyoming was the unofficial halfway point of the Oregon Trail. By the time wagons reached Laramie they had traveled about 650 miles from Independence and had another 1,350 to go. Pioneers rested, repaired, resupplied, and wrote letters home that mail riders would carry east. Reaching Fort Laramie meant the easy part was over and the mountains lay ahead.
historical_facts.source: National Park Service — Fort Laramie NHS
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why was Fort Laramie called the unofficial halfway point of the Oregon Trail?
historical_facts.correct: Because it sat about 650 miles from Independence — roughly half of the 2,000-mile journey
wrong: Because pioneers turned back from there half the time
Because the U.S. Army required wagons to register there at the midpoint
Because the Pony Express turned around there

quiz_variants.question: What did pioneers do at Fort Laramie?
quiz_variants.correct: Rested, repaired wagons, resupplied, and sent mail home
wrong: Voted on whether to continue or return east
Picked up army escorts to Oregon
Met California-bound emigrants and traded routes

quiz_variants.question: About how far had pioneers traveled when they reached Fort Laramie?

historical_facts.title: The Sublette Cutoff
historical_facts.fact: The Sublette Cutoff was a shortcut west of South Pass that saved roughly 70 miles by skipping Fort Bridger. The trade-off was a brutal 50-mile dry crossing called the 'Little Colorado Desert' with no reliable water. Wagon trains debated for days whether to take it. Those who guessed right gained time. Those who guessed wrong sometimes lost oxen — or people.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What was the trade-off of the Sublette Cutoff?
historical_facts.correct: Saved 70 miles but crossed 50 miles of waterless desert
wrong: Saved a week but cost double in tolls
Avoided cholera but added a tall mountain pass
Faster but required Native guide payment

quiz_variants.question: About how long was the dry crossing on the Sublette Cutoff?
quiz_variants.correct: About 50 miles with no reliable water
