wrong: About 5 miles, manageable in a half-day
About 200 miles, longer than any pioneer attempted

historical_facts.title: South Pass — The Continental Divide
historical_facts.fact: South Pass in present-day Wyoming is a wide, gentle saddle through the Rocky Mountains — so gentle that pioneers often crossed without realizing they had topped the Continental Divide. East of South Pass, water flows to the Atlantic. West of it, water flows to the Pacific. Pioneers used to dip their canteens in two creeks to taste water from both oceans.
historical_facts.source: Bureau of Land Management — South Pass National Historic Landmark
historical_facts.quiz_question: What makes South Pass important?
historical_facts.correct: It is a gentle crossing of the Continental Divide in the Rocky Mountains
wrong: It is the highest peak on the Oregon Trail
It is the longest river crossing on the trail
It is the only place where two trails meet

quiz_variants.question: What is the Continental Divide?
quiz_variants.correct: The line where rivers begin flowing east toward the Atlantic or west toward the Pacific
wrong: The U.S.-Canada border in the West
The line between free states and slave states
The boundary between Oregon and California

quiz_variants.question: Why was South Pass surprisingly easy to cross compared to other Rocky Mountain passes?
quiz_variants.correct: It is a wide, gentle saddle rather than a steep peak
wrong: The U.S. Army built a road through it
Native guides cleared a tunnel through the mountain
It was lower in elevation than the prairie

historical_facts.title: Sacagawea and Lewis & Clark
historical_facts.fact: Sacagawea, a young Lemhi Shoshone woman, traveled with Lewis and Clark from 1805 to 1806 — about 40 years before the Oregon Trail wagons rolled. She translated, identified edible plants, and her presence with a baby signaled to other tribes that the expedition came in peace. Without her, the journey might have failed. Pioneers crossing the trail walked roads she had helped open.
historical_facts.source: National Park Service — Lewis & Clark NHT
historical_facts.quiz_question: How did Sacagawea help the Lewis & Clark Expedition?
historical_facts.correct: She translated, found edible plants, and signaled peaceful intent to other tribes
wrong: She drew the first map of the entire route
She led every expedition meeting and made all decisions
She paid for the expedition with money from her tribe

quiz_variants.question: About how many years before Oregon Trail wagons did Sacagawea travel with Lewis and Clark?
What tribal nation was Sacagawea born into?

historical_facts.title: The Snake River — Treacherous Beauty
historical_facts.fact: The Snake River in present-day Idaho was one of the most beautiful and most dangerous stretches of the Oregon Trail. The river ran in deep canyons, sometimes hundreds of feet below the trail. Pioneers could see the water but couldn't reach it. Many died of thirst within sight of the river. The Native Shoshone and Nez Perce knew the safe descent points; pioneers who hired guides survived; those who didn't, sometimes did not.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why was the Snake River dangerous despite being a major water source?
historical_facts.correct: It ran through deep canyons that pioneers couldn't easily descend to reach the water
wrong: It was poisoned by alkali for most of its length
Bandits guarded every approach to the water
Pioneers were forbidden by treaty from drinking from it

quiz_variants.question: Which Native peoples helped pioneers find safe descent points on the Snake River?

historical_facts.title: Wagons Made for the Trail
historical_facts.fact: The Conestoga wagon was too heavy for the Oregon Trail. Most pioneers used a smaller 'prairie schooner' — about 10 feet long, 4 feet wide, with a canvas top stretched over hickory bows. A loaded prairie schooner weighed about 1,500 pounds and could carry another 1,500 pounds of supplies. It rode on iron-tired wooden wheels and was pulled by 4 to 6 oxen.
historical_facts.source: Smithsonian National Museum of American History
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why didn't pioneers use the famous Conestoga wagon on the Oregon Trail?
historical_facts.correct: Conestoga wagons were too heavy; pioneers used smaller 'prairie schooners' instead
wrong: Conestoga wagons were illegal west of Missouri
Conestoga wagons couldn't be repaired without a blacksmith
Conestoga wagons could only be pulled by horses

quiz_variants.question: About how much could a prairie schooner carry in supplies?
What animals usually pulled a prairie schooner?

historical_facts.title: Why Oxen, Not Horses?
historical_facts.fact: Oxen pulled most Oregon Trail wagons because they were stronger, calmer, cheaper, and could eat the prairie grass that horses found unpalatable. Oxen also held up better on the long distances and were less likely to be stolen by bandits. The disadvantage: they were slow — about 2 miles per hour. A wagon train made 12 to 20 miles on a good day.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why did most pioneers use oxen instead of horses to pull wagons?
historical_facts.correct: Oxen were stronger, calmer, cheaper, ate prairie grass, and were less likely to be stolen
wrong: Horses were illegal to take across the Mississippi River
The U.S. government required oxen for all wagon trains
Horses couldn't drink the water west of Independence

quiz_variants.question: About how fast did an ox-pulled wagon travel?
wrong: About half a mile per hour

quiz_variants.question: About how many miles did a wagon train travel on a good day?

historical_facts.title: Women on the Trail
historical_facts.fact: Pioneer women on the Oregon Trail did most of the cooking, laundry, child care, and medical care — on top of walking the same 2,000 miles as the men. Many wrote diaries that survive today and tell us most of what we know about daily trail life. Women also drove wagons, hunted, and helped repair wheels. Several gave birth on the trail. The journey was as much theirs as anyone's.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What do historians use to learn about daily life on the Oregon Trail?
historical_facts.correct: The diaries kept by pioneer women
wrong: Government surveys of every wagon train
Newspaper articles published in Oregon City

quiz_variants.question: What kinds of work did pioneer women do on the trail?
quiz_variants.correct: Cooking, laundry, child care, medical care, driving wagons, hunting, and repairs
wrong: They rode in the wagon and did not work

historical_facts.title: Children on the Trail
historical_facts.fact: About one-third of Oregon Trail emigrants were children. Many walked the entire journey alongside the wagons, picking up buffalo chips for fires, watching younger siblings, and gathering wild fruit. Some children played a game of finding three special things on the trail every day — a bird's feather, a smooth river stone, a wildflower. Childhood on the trail was hard but not joyless.
historical_facts.quiz_question: About what fraction of Oregon Trail emigrants were children?
historical_facts.correct: About one-third
wrong: Almost none — the trail was too dangerous

quiz_variants.question: What was a common chore for pioneer children on the trail?
quiz_variants.correct: Picking up buffalo chips for the cooking fires

historical_facts.title: The Cost of Outfitting
historical_facts.fact: Outfitting a family of four for the Oregon Trail cost about $700 to $1,000 in 1848 — the equivalent of more than two years' wages for a working family. Most pioneers were not the very poor; they were middling farmers selling everything they owned for a chance at land in Oregon. The Donation Land Claim Act later promised 320 acres to single settlers and 640 to married couples — vast wealth by the standards of crowded eastern farms.
historical_facts.source: Oregon Historical Society / Donation Land Claim Act records
historical_facts.quiz_question: About how much did it cost a family of four to outfit for the Oregon Trail in 1848?
historical_facts.correct: About $700 to $1,000 — more than two years' wages
wrong: About $20 — almost anyone could afford it
About $50,000 — only the wealthy could go
Free — the U.S. government paid for everything

quiz_variants.question: How much land did a married couple receive under the Donation Land Claim Act?

historical_facts.title: The Fur Trade Trails Came First
historical_facts.fact: Before pioneers came in wagons, fur trappers and traders had walked these trails for decades. Mountain men like Jim Bridger, Jedediah Smith, and Joseph Walker mapped the rivers, the passes, and the safe water sources between 1820 and 1840. Without their knowledge, the Oregon Trail as wagon emigrants knew it would not have existed. Bridger himself founded the fort that bears his name.
historical_facts.quiz_question: Who knew the Oregon Trail routes before wagon emigrants arrived?
historical_facts.correct: Fur trappers and mountain men who had explored the region in the 1820s and 1830s
wrong: U.S. Army surveyors who marked every mile
British traders from the Hudson's Bay Company who built the trail

quiz_variants.question: Which mountain man founded a fort on the Oregon Trail that still bears his name?

historical_facts.title: The Letter Home
historical_facts.fact: Pioneers sent letters home from forts along the trail. Mail riders carried letters east in saddlebags, and a letter from Fort Laramie might take three to six months to reach a family in Ohio or Pennsylvania. Some letters never arrived; some arrived after the writer had reached Oregon and written again from there. Receiving a letter was an event for the whole community back east — neighbors gathered to hear the news read aloud.
historical_facts.quiz_question: About how long did a letter from Fort Laramie take to reach the East?
historical_facts.correct: Three to six months
wrong: About one day, by Pony Express
Letters could not be sent from the trail

quiz_variants.question: Who carried letters from forts on the Oregon Trail back east?

historical_facts.title: Trade Goods at the Forts
historical_facts.fact: Fort traders charged pioneers two to ten times what supplies cost back east. A pound of flour that cost 4 cents in Independence might be 35 cents at Fort Hall. Sugar, coffee, and ammunition were similarly marked up. Pioneers learned to leave Independence well-supplied and visit forts only when desperate. A Banker on the team helped — they had cash for emergencies. A Merchant negotiated better deals.
historical_facts.source: Hudson's Bay Company records / Fort Hall trade ledgers
historical_facts.quiz_question: Why were supplies at trail forts so expensive?
historical_facts.correct: Forts were remote, so traders charged two to ten times the eastern price
wrong: The U.S. government taxed trail forts heavily
Forts only accepted gold, which raised the price
Native peoples controlled the price of all trail goods

quiz_variants.question: Which profession helped a pioneer family negotiate better prices at fort trading posts?

historical_facts.title: The Donation Land Claim Act
historical_facts.fact: The Donation Land Claim Act of 1850 gave free land in Oregon to settlers who farmed it for four years. A single white settler over age 18 received 320 acres; a married couple received 640 acres. The Act explicitly excluded Native peoples, Black Americans, and Hawaiians from owning land — a painful injustice woven into the very motivation that brought wagon trains west. Understanding the trail means understanding both the dream and the exclusion.
historical_facts.source: Oregon Historical Society — Donation Land Claim Act records
historical_facts.quiz_question: What did the Donation Land Claim Act of 1850 promise emigrants?
historical_facts.correct: Free land in Oregon if they farmed it for four years
wrong: Free passage on a steamship from Missouri
A salary from the U.S. Army for guarding the trail
Citizenship for any nationality that arrived

quiz_variants.question: Who was excluded from owning land under the Donation Land Claim Act?
quiz_variants.correct: Native peoples, Black Americans, and Hawaiians
wrong: Anyone born outside the United States
Nobody — the Act was open to all settlers

historical_facts.title: Buffalo Chips for Cooking
historical_facts.fact: On the open prairie, where trees were rare for hundreds of miles, pioneers cooked over fires made from dried buffalo droppings — called 'buffalo chips' or 'prairie coal.' Children gathered them by the bushelful. The chips burned hot, almost smokeless, and odorless once dried by the prairie sun. Many pioneer families wrote that buffalo chips kept them alive — without that fuel, they could not have cooked or boiled water for safety.
historical_facts.quiz_question: What did pioneers use for fuel when there were no trees on the prairie?
historical_facts.correct: Dried buffalo droppings (called buffalo chips)
wrong: Coal they bought at trail forts
Leftover wood scraps from the wagon

quiz_variants.question: What were 'prairie coal' or 'buffalo chips'?
quiz_variants.correct: Dried buffalo droppings used as cooking fuel
wrong: A type of coal mined in Wyoming

historical_facts.title: Trail Graves
historical_facts.fact: About 1 in 10 Oregon Trail emigrants died on the journey — roughly 30,000 people in total over the trail's main use from 1841 to 1869. Most are buried in unmarked graves along the route. Some graves can still be found today by hikers and ranchers; the Bureau of Land Management has cataloged hundreds. Each one is a story we will never fully know. The trail is, in a real sense, also a long graveyard.
historical_facts.quiz_question: About how many people died on the Oregon Trail in total?
historical_facts.correct: About 30,000 over the trail's main use from 1841 to 1869
wrong: Almost none — modern medicine prevented most deaths

quiz_variants.question: Where can Oregon Trail graves still be found today?
quiz_variants.correct: Unmarked along the route, cataloged by the Bureau of Land Management
wrong: In a single national cemetery in Oregon City
Only in churchyards along the trail
All graves were moved to museums

surprise_events.name: Aurora Borealis
surprise_events.intro: A green light swims across the northern sky. Then red. Then green again, brighter.
surprise_events.flavor: Aurora was rarely visible this far south, but it happened — pioneers wrote about it in journals as a sign from God, a wonder of wonders, a portent. Modern science calls it the same name and a different cause.
surprise_events.dyk_pairing: The aurora is caused by charged particles from the Sun striking gases high in Earth's atmosphere. Oxygen glows green and red; nitrogen, blue and purple. The same physics that lit the prairie sky for our pioneers lights the night skies of Alaska and Norway today.

narratives.default: The whole camp stands in silence. The horses do not move. The children do not whisper. The lights ripple across the dark like cloth in water. By the time it fades, no one is the same. We will tell our grandchildren about this night.

surprise_events.name: A Lone Fiddler
surprise_events.intro: A man walks into camp at dusk with a fiddle case under his arm.
surprise_events.flavor: Solo travelers walking the trail were not unheard of — fiddlers, preachers, prospectors, runaways. They asked for a meal and gave what they had.

narratives.default: He says he's headed for Oregon, same as us. Asks for a plate of beans in exchange for a song. He plays for two hours straight by firelight — old reels, slow ballads, jigs that get the children dancing. We sleep that night with music still in our ears.
narratives.Musician: {Musician} unpacks their own fiddle within minutes. The two of them play together until the embers die — old reels and new ones, harmonies and counter-harmonies. The whole camp is awake at midnight, dancing, and no one minds.

surprise_events.name: Found Gold Dust
surprise_events.intro: A small leather pouch in the dirt by an abandoned firepit.
surprise_events.flavor: Pioneer leavings sometimes hid real value — coin pouches, jewelry, a few flakes of California-bound gold dust.

narratives.default: Inside: a few flakes of gold dust and a folded note in a child's handwriting. The note just says 'for mama.' We do not know who they were. We do not know what happened to them. We pocket the gold quietly and continue west.

surprise_events.name: Eagle Feather
surprise_events.intro: The youngest finds a feather in the grass — long, dark, unmistakable.
surprise_events.flavor: Native peoples of the plains regard the eagle feather as sacred. Pioneers sometimes did too — a sign of fortune, a charm against harm.

narratives.default: The child holds it up like a sword. The whole team smiles. We tuck it into the wagon canvas where it can be seen. Something feels lighter for the rest of the week. Some call it luck. Some call it grace.

surprise_events.name: Shooting Star Wishes
surprise_events.intro: A streak across the sky. Then another. Then a hundred.
surprise_events.flavor: The Perseid meteor shower peaks in mid-August — many pioneers crossed Wyoming during the show.

narratives.default: We lay on our backs in the dust and watch. Every few seconds, a streak of fire across the dark. We make wishes — every one of us, even those who don't say it out loud. By morning we don't remember most of them. But we feel different. The trail seems shorter in the morning.

surprise_events.name: White Buffalo
surprise_events.intro: A buffalo, separate from the herd, white as winter, watching us.
surprise_events.flavor: The white buffalo is sacred to the Lakota, Cheyenne, and other Plains nations — the embodiment of the White Buffalo Calf Woman, a teacher and bringer of the sacred pipe. Pioneer journals from the 1840s and 1850s record at least three sightings. We name what we saw; we do not pretend to know what it meant.
surprise_events.dyk_pairing: The Lakota nation traces the white buffalo back to White Buffalo Calf Woman, who taught the Lakota the sacred ceremonies and pipe. To this day, a white buffalo birth is honored across many nations.

narratives.default: No one moves to raise a rifle. Even the children understand. We watch, the buffalo watches, and after a long quiet moment it turns and walks back into the herd. We do not speak of it for the rest of the day. We carry the memory of it the rest of our lives.

surprise_events.name: A Stranger's Riddle
surprise_events.intro: An old man at a creek crossing, who looked like he'd been waiting for someone.
surprise_events.flavor: The trail had its share of strange travelers — preachers, prophets, drifters, hermits.

narratives.default: He asks: "What do you carry with you that you cannot put down?" No one answers right away. He nods, as if the silence was the right answer, and walks off into the brush. We talk about it for days. None of us forgets the question.

surprise_events.name: Sudden Hailstorm
surprise_events.intro: Out of a clear blue sky, a column of dark cloud — and then ice falling like fists.
surprise_events.flavor: Plains hail could be the size of eggs and could kill livestock in minutes. Pioneer journals describe it with terror.

narratives.default: The hailstones bruise everyone before we can shelter under the wagon. One ox is hit hard and goes down. The canvas is shredded. By the time it passes, we are bleeding and stunned and counting losses.
narratives.Carpenter: {Carpenter} pulled the canvas tight and lashed extra wood across the wagon top before the worst of it. The team huddles underneath. Nothing breaks. The hail passes and we count blessings.
narratives.Cowboy: {Cowboy} rushes to the oxen, drags them under a low overhang, holds the lead by force. The hail strips the grass bare around them but the oxen stand. We come through whole.

surprise_events.name: Buffalo Stampede Distant
surprise_events.intro: A sound like thunder under the ground. The horizon turns dark with running animals.
surprise_events.flavor: A real stampede was a force of nature. Pioneer journals describe miles of running buffalo — beautiful and terrifying.

narratives.default: The herd passes a half-mile to the south — thousands of dark shapes thundering through the dust. The earth shakes underfoot. Our oxen stamp nervously but hold. When the dust settles, the prairie behind them is bare. The team is silent, awed.

surprise_events.name: Wagon Train Reunion
surprise_events.intro: A wagon train heading EAST. Faces familiar — half-remembered cousins, neighbors from another life.
surprise_events.flavor: Some pioneers turned back. Some had business to settle. Reunions on the trail were rare and emotional.

narratives.default: There are tears. There is laughter. We share a meal that lasts three hours. They tell us what's ahead — the rivers to fear, the fort that has fresh apples, the stretch where the wind never stops. They press extra food on us. We watch their wagons until they're a smudge on the horizon.

surprise_events.name: Strange Lights in the Hills
surprise_events.intro: Pinpoints of fire moving along the ridge. Not a wagon train. Not a camp. Something else.
surprise_events.flavor: Pioneer journals occasionally mention 'spirit lights' or 'fox fire' — most likely campfires, optical effects, or natural gas vents. The mystery was real to those who saw them.

narratives.default: We post extra watch. The lights move along the ridge for hours and then are gone. By morning the dust has been brushed clean of any tracks. No one slept well. We continue on, glancing over our shoulders for two days.

surprise_events.name: Coyote Sings to the Moon
surprise_events.intro: A single coyote on a low rise, silhouetted against the moon. It tilts its head back and sings.
surprise_events.flavor: Coyote song was the soundtrack of pioneer nights. Most found it eerie. Some learned to love it.

narratives.default: Then another joins. Then a chorus, far across the prairie. The song lasts twenty minutes — yips and howls weaving in and out. Then silence. The moon rolls on. We remember being small once, listening for things in the dark.

surprise_events.name: An Old Pioneer Veteran
surprise_events.intro: A bent old man at a creek crossing, smoking a clay pipe, watching the wagons go by.
surprise_events.flavor: Some pioneers crossed multiple times. Some lived along the trail. Their wisdom was free for the asking.

modifiers.effect: useful tip — extra +2 morale, route hint

narratives.default: He tells us he's crossed five times. Lost a wife on the second crossing, a child on the fourth. Still came back. We give him coffee and listen for an hour. When we leave, he raises his hat. We do not see him again.
narratives.Guide: {Guide} sits with him for two hours. He draws a map in the dirt — a route around bad ground, a creek the maps don't show, a fort that has fresh oranges in summer. {Guide} listens like a student. We walk away with knowledge no map can give.
narratives.Politician: {Politician} has the gift of getting people talking. The old man opens up — tells us where the trail bandits are this season, which fort prices are honest, where to find good water. The team is buzzing about it for days.

surprise_events.name: Children Find a Bird's Nest
surprise_events.intro: The youngest comes running with cupped hands. "Don't tell mama!"
surprise_events.flavor: Small wonders mattered on the trail. A nest of meadowlark eggs could brighten a whole week.

narratives.default: Inside: four pale-blue eggs no bigger than peas. We make her promise to put them back exactly where she found them. She does. The whole camp is gentler with each other for the rest of the day. Some things on the trail are just for the kids.

surprise_events.name: Tornado on the Horizon
surprise_events.intro: A thin black thread, twisting from a black cloud to the prairie. Several miles distant.
surprise_events.flavor: Plains tornadoes were rare for individual wagon trains to face directly, but seeing one in the distance was common — and never forgotten.

modifiers.effect: evade the worst, no morale loss

narratives.default: We watch it cross the prairie for forty minutes — slow, almost graceful, leaving a brown trail behind it. It does not turn toward us, but no one breathes easy until it dissolves back into the cloud it came from. We are quiet for the rest of the day.
narratives.Scout: {Scout} reads the wind shift early and pulls the wagon train into a draw a half-mile off the trail. The tornado passes a quarter-mile away — close enough to count rope-thicknesses in the funnel. We watch it pass and continue without a scratch. A story for the rest of our lives.

surprise_events.name: A Star Falls to Earth
surprise_events.intro: A streak of fire — green, then white — and a deep BOOM that shakes the wagon.
surprise_events.flavor: Meteorites were occasionally seen and even recovered by 19th century travelers. The experience was unforgettable.
surprise_events.dyk_pairing: The Leonid meteor shower peaks every November when Earth crosses the dust trail of comet Tempel-Tuttle. The streaks of fire are tiny grains of comet rock burning up at sixty miles above the prairie. Some of them survive to ground; pioneers occasionally found and kept them.

narratives.default: The team runs out of the wagons in the dark. Whatever it was struck not far off — somewhere over the next ridge. By dawn the air still smells faintly of ash and metal. We don't go looking. Some things are not meant to be picked up. We carry the story with us.

bandit_ambush.philosophy: The climactic encounter near journey's end. Quiz performance + profession bonuses + team state determine the outcome. Functions as the game's 'final exam' — rewarding teams who paid attention to historical content.
bandit_ambush.intro: Three riders on the ridge, faces wrapped, rifles across their saddles. They are between you and Oregon City.
bandit_ambush.flavor: Bandits and outlaws preyed on emigrants near the end of the trail, when wagons were exhausted and the temptation was greatest. Real ambushes happened — and real wagons fought them off, talked them down, or paid the price.
quiz_question_styles: What was MOST commonly used to caulk wagons crossing rivers?
Which fort was the LARGEST resupply point on the trail?
What landmark did pioneers carve their names into?
What was the #1 killer on the Oregon Trail?
What year was Independence, Missouri the main jumping-off point?

5_correct.narrative: The bandits ride closer, hesitating. {Politician_or_speaker} steps forward, calm and confident, and the team's calm bearing speaks louder than any rifle. The bandits turn their horses and ride east. We continue with everything we came with.

4_correct.narrative: The standoff lasts a long minute. The bandits demand a tribute. {team_speaker} talks them down to a single barrel of supplies. They ride off cursing, but they ride.

3_correct.narrative: The bandits open fire. We return it. {Cowboy_or_Hunter_if_present} drops one of them with a clean shot. The other two flee, but not before stealing two barrels and wounding {wounded_member}.

2_correct.narrative: It is a gunfight, brief and ugly. We hold the wagon but we lose {lost_member} the {lost_profession} in the exchange. The bandits ride off with three barrels and a horse.

1_correct.narrative: The ambush is bad. The bandits know what they're doing. We lose {lost_member_1} and {lost_member_2}. Half the supplies. We continue, but barely.

0_correct.narrative: We were not ready. The bandits take everything. {lost_member_1}, {lost_member_2}, and {lost_member_3} fall. The wagon limps on toward Oregon City carrying ghosts.

profession_protection.effect: +1 effective correct answer (their gunfight skill matters)
if 3+ correct, no member is lost (calming presence)
if 4+ correct, no supply loss (saw the ambush coming, prepared)

bandit_ambush.narrative_post_ambush: The trail ahead is clear. Oregon City is hours away. We are quieter than we were yesterday. Different. The journey has marked us.

scripted_graves.name: Sarah Keyes
scripted_graves.epitaph: Died May 29, 1846 — Aged 70 — Mother of the Donner-Reed Party
scripted_graves.fact: Sarah Keyes died of consumption near Alcove Spring on the way west. She was the oldest member of the Donner Party. Her family carved her name into a stone before continuing.

scripted_graves.name: John D. Lee
scripted_graves.fact: Cholera killed thousands of pioneers. Most graves on the trail were unmarked or marked with simple wooden boards that have long since rotted away.

scripted_graves.name: Narcissa Whitman
scripted_graves.fact: Narcissa Whitman was one of the first white women to cross the Rockies. She and her husband Marcus founded the Whitman Mission. Both were killed in 1847 in a tragic conflict with the Cayuse people during a measles epidemic. The story is more complicated than any monument can tell.

scripted_graves.name: Unknown Child
scripted_graves.fact: Many pioneer graves were marked simply 'unknown' or with just a first name. About 1 in 5 trail emigrants was a child. Many did not survive the journey.

hidden_figures.hint: An old frontiersman waving from the edge of town
hidden_figures.fact: Daniel Boone helped open the western frontier decades before the Oregon Trail.

hidden_figures.hint: A man in army blue inspecting the fort
hidden_figures.fact: Fort Kearney (later renamed Fort Kearny) was named after Stephen Watts Kearny, a U.S. Army officer of the Mexican-American War.

hidden_figures.hint: A bearded mountain man at the trading post
hidden_figures.fact: Jim Bridger was a legendary mountain man who founded Fort Bridger in 1843. By 1848 he was already famous across the West.

hidden_figures.hint: A robed priest carving his name into the rock
hidden_figures.fact: Father De Smet was a Belgian Jesuit missionary who carved his name into Independence Rock in 1840 — one of the earliest signatures still visible today.

hidden_figures.hint: A scout pointing west across the divide
hidden_figures.fact: Jedediah Smith was the first American to cross South Pass in 1824 — opening the route that became the Oregon Trail.

hidden_figures.hint: A doctor in a long coat, walking near a small white church
hidden_figures.fact: Marcus Whitman was a physician and missionary who, with his wife Narcissa, founded the Whitman Mission in 1836.

hidden_figures.hint: A tall white-haired man at the gates of Oregon City
hidden_figures.fact: John McLoughlin, the 'Father of Oregon,' was a Hudson's Bay Company chief factor who helped countless pioneers reach Oregon City. He founded the city in 1842.

leaderboard_seed.name: Joseph Meek
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Lost the Carpenter at the Snake River.

leaderboard_seed.name: Tabitha Brown
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Late but proud. Hard winter ahead.

leaderboard_seed.name: Margaret Reed
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Lost in the snow before Fort Hall.

leaderboard_seed.name: Joel Palmer
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Veteran of the trail. Knew every shortcut.

leaderboard_seed.name: Asa Lovejoy
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Founded a town when he arrived.

leaderboard_seed.name: Catherine Sager
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Orphaned on the trail. A different journey.

leaderboard_seed.name: Father De Smet
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: Faith and patience saw them through.

leaderboard_seed.name: Nancy Kelsey
leaderboard_seed.epitaph: First woman to cross by wagon.

dispatch_system.philosophy: Letters home cost money but boost morale and create personal artifacts. The app generates a one-paragraph letter from the team's perspective when sent.
dispatch_system.letter_template_seed: Generate a 4-6 sentence letter in 1840s voice from the team's wagon to family back east. Reference: current location, days elapsed, current team status (who's alive, sick, lost), pace and rations strategy, one specific recent event. Sign with the player's name. End with hope or worry, depending on team state.

teacher_mode.philosophy: Generate a printable rubric report after each game so teachers can grade engagement and learning objectively.
report_includes: Total historical facts shown to team
Quiz score on the bandit ambush (if reached)
Number of rivers crossed and crossing methods used
Pace strategy summary (% time in Steady / Strenuous / Grueling)
Members lost (with names, days, and causes)
teacher_mode.format: Printable HTML page (Letter portrait), structured for grading
teacher_mode.trigger: Toggle in setup: 'Teacher Mode' (default off). When on, the report screen replaces or supplements the regular end-game screen.

what_if_mode.philosophy: After a wagon finishes (win or lose), offer ONE counterfactual replay where the team sees an alternate outcome from their pivotal choice.
what_if_mode.trigger: End-game screen, button labeled 'What If?' below the score
logic: Identify the pivotal choice during the game (largest resource-shift decision, OR member-loss-causing decision, OR Independence Rock arrival)
Replay JUST that scene with the alternate option taken
Return to the original score screen — the original game is what counts
what_if_mode.narrative_intro: What if you had chosen differently at [event_name] on Day [day]?

patterns: Dearest family — / We are at {location}. The journey has been {tone}. {specific_recent_event}. {team_status_line}. We push on. / Yours always, / {player_name}
Mother — / It is Day {day} on the trail. We reached {location} at last. {specific_event}. The team is {tone_word}. {member_callout}. Pray for us. / {player_name}
{recipient} — / The {wagon_name} has come {distance_descriptor}. {specific_event}. {team_status_line}. The land is bigger than I imagined. / With love, / {player_name}

hunt_action.name: Hunt
hunt_action.description: Spend the day hunting for game. Yields meat and hides.

success_with_hunter.narrative: {Hunter} brings down a {animal}. The team butchers it for meat and saves the hide for trade.

success_with_trapper.narrative: {Trapper} knows the country. Two animals taken — meat and hides for the cash box.

success_with_cowboy.narrative: {Cowboy} gets a clean shot. Not a great haul, but enough.

success_no_specialist.narrative: We try our best. A small rabbit, maybe a grouse. The skin is too small to bother saving.

failure.narrative: The game vanished. We return empty-handed at dusk.

money_economy.philosophy: Money is the strategic resource. Players should constantly weigh: do I sell this hide now or hold it for a fort with better rates? Do I work a day at this fort or push on? Different professions enable different income streams. Cash-poor wagons should feel real pressure; cash-rich wagons should feel rewarded for their decisions.

income_sources.scaling: Hunter grants 1 hide per success; Trapper grants 2

income_sources.examples: Doctor, Carpenter, Seamstress, Artist, Cook, Blacksmith, Musician, Magician, Language Teacher, Trader

money_economy.balance_target: A wagon that hunts regularly, sells at every fort, and triggers 2-3 profession encounters should comfortably afford 1-2 ferries, 4-5 fort resupplies, and 2-3 letters home. A wagon that ignores income will go broke around day 60.
ui_visibility: Money displayed prominently in the resource ribbon
Hides displayed as a NEW resource icon between Supplies and Money
When money/hides change, ribbon shows brief +N/-N animation in green/red
When entering a fort, show one-line tip card: 'Your goods at this fort: N hides, M excess food. Sell them for cash.'
Profession encounter events should narrate the money source clearly: 'Three silver coins, paid in cash.'

music_system.philosophy: Variety prevents auditory fatigue in a 90-minute classroom session. Songs rotate through a pool with mood-aware selection. No single song plays back-to-back.
rotation_rules: Pick song from the pool whose mood matches current game mood
Never play the same song twice in a row
Rotate at minimum every 90 seconds even if no mood change
On mood change, fade out current song and switch within 5 seconds
Funeral songs only play on member loss / wagon elimination — outside that pool
mood_logic: hopeful: morale >=7 AND no member sick/dying
neutral: morale 4-6 AND no member dying
weary: morale <=3 OR any member sick
somber: any member dying OR resources at zero
funeral_only: triggered on death event only, returns to prior mood after
music_system.tempo_variation_rule: Within a mood pool, prefer alternating fast/slow songs (don't play 2 slow songs back-to-back). Tempo classes: fast=120+ bpm, medium=90-120, slow=below 90. Funeral hymn (Amazing Grace, 60bpm) plays once on death, then returns to prior mood. After a funeral hymn, the next song picked from the mood pool should be at least 90bpm to break the somber spell.
music_system.amazing_grace_speedup: Amazing Grace at 60bpm felt funeral-pace plodding in playtest. Bump to 75bpm for the in-game funeral version (still slow and reverent, but not soporific). Re-encode with more notes for richer melody.

death_announcement_protocol.philosophy: Member deaths must NEVER pass silently. Each death is a story beat — named, narrated, scored, and audibly marked. Without this, the simulation feels like numbers ticking down instead of a journey.
death_announcement_protocol.trigger: A member transitions from any living state to 'lost'
required_sequence: 1. PAUSE — game halts current scene processing
2. SCENE — composes a death scene: current backdrop dimmed 40%, the lost member's full-body figure shown center-canvas with a fade-to-grayscale animation over 1.5s
3. AUDIO — Amazing Grace begins playing (replacing current music) at slightly increased volume
4. NARRATIVE CALLOUT — appears with the death message:
Body: '{Member Name} the {Profession} was lost on Day {N}. Cause: {cause_phrase}.'
Period closing: 'We dig a grave by the trail and continue west.'
5. TRAIL STRIP — member's portrait transitions to grayscale + cross icon + 'Lost Day {N}' label
6. DELAY — Continue button does not appear for 2.5 seconds (give the moment weight)
7. JOURNAL — entry written to Trail Journal: '{Day N}: {Member Name} the {Profession}, lost to {cause}. We pressed on.'
8. RESUME — after Continue clicked, music returns to prior mood pool, game resumes

cause_phrase_map.starvation: starvation, after too many days without food
cause_phrase_map.dehydration: thirst — the water had run out days before
cause_phrase_map.winter_exposure: the cold of the high passes
cause_phrase_map.abandonment: they could not bear the trail any longer and turned back at first light

death_announcement_protocol.first_death_special: On the FIRST member loss in a journey, the death scene holds 1 second longer and the Continue button is replaced briefly with the text 'A grave on the trail.' before becoming clickable. This calibrates the player to the weight of subsequent losses.

difficulty_settings.philosophy: A meta-setting that scales the simulation's challenge without changing the game's structure. Selected once at setup. Default is Medium, which matches the historical 1-in-10 death rate. Easy is forgiving for first-time players or younger classes. Hard is for repeat players who want a real test.

Easy.label: Easy — A Forgiving Trail
Easy.subtitle: For first-time players. Members are hardier, money flows freely, and you have one revival.

Medium.label: Medium — The Honest Trail
Medium.subtitle: The historical experience. About one in ten wagons faces total disaster. Recommended for most classroom plays.

Hard.label: Hard — The Trail of Tears
Hard.subtitle: Cholera spreads faster. Hunters miss more often. The land is unforgiving. For repeat players who want a real test.

difficulty_settings.ui_placement: Show as Screen 2.5 in setup, between game mode and team selection. Three large cards with the labels above, the subtitles, and an icon (sun/cloud/storm).

warning_escalation_system.philosophy: Players must have agency at every escalating threat. Currently the simulation ticks silently and members die without warning — this kills the kids' sense of choice and turns the game passive. Every transition to a worse state must produce either a notification (informational) or a choice event (decision required). Death can no longer come without a window for action.

death_per_turn_cap.rule: By default, NO MORE THAN ONE member can transition to 'lost' state in a single turn. If multiple members would die simultaneously through the natural state machine progression, only the most-progressed one (or random pick if tied) actually dies — the others remain in 'dying' state for the next turn, giving the player another window to intervene.
exceptions: Catastrophic event outcomes (river drowning that fails, bandit ambush with 0-1 quiz correct, cholera epidemic event) can kill multiple members in one event because the player CHOSE to enter that risk through their action.
Day 165 winter ending kills exactly 1 member as part of the ending sequence, regardless.

transition_notifications.philosophy: When a member transitions from healthy → weakened, or weakened → sick, the player gets a brief informational popup. No choice required, just acknowledgment. This is the EARLY WARNING.

healthy_to_weakened.trigger: Member state transitions from healthy to weakened
healthy_to_weakened.ui: Small toast notification slides in from top-right of scene, auto-dismisses after 4 seconds. Yellow accent.

narratives_by_cause.exhaustion: {name} is exhausted from the hard pace.

healthy_to_weakened.audio_cue: Soft worried tone — descending sine wave, 200ms

weakened_to_sick_or_injured.trigger: Member transitions from weakened to sick OR injured
weakened_to_sick_or_injured.ui: Larger banner notification pauses scene. Player must click Acknowledge to continue.

narratives_by_cause.starvation: {name} has fallen ill from the lack of food. Without recovery they will worsen.
narratives_by_cause.dehydration: {name} has fallen ill from dehydration. Their lips crack and their head pounds.
narratives_by_cause.cholera_event: {name} has caught cholera. The next days are critical.
narratives_by_cause.injury_event: {name} is hurt. The wound needs tending.
narratives_by_cause.default: {name} has fallen ill. The next days are critical.

weakened_to_sick_or_injured.audio_cue: Coughing sample (the same loop already in audio system)

pre_death_intervention.philosophy: When a member transitions to 'dying' state, the simulation PAUSES and the player gets a major event with multiple choices. This is the player's window to save the member. Without action, the member will be lost on a subsequent turn.
pre_death_intervention.trigger: Member state transitions to 'dying' (from sick or injured) OR a member is in 'dying' state at the start of a turn
pre_death_intervention.fires_at: End of turn AFTER state transitions but BEFORE death checks. The member is in 'dying' state and at risk; the player gets to decide what to do.
pre_death_intervention.narrative_intro_template: {name} the {profession} is gravely ill. Their fever burns. They drift in and out of waking. Without intervention they will not survive much longer.

choices.label: Race to the nearest fort
choices.description: Push toward medical help. -1 day. -1 morale. +30% chance to recover.
choices.narrative_success: We pushed hard. {name} is alive but weak when we reach the fort. The doctor there continues the care.
choices.narrative_failure: We pushed hard. We were too slow. {name} did not see the fort gates.

choices.label: Use the fort doctor
choices.description: The fort's surgeon has supplies and skill we don't. No travel — we are already at the gates.
choices.narrative_success: The fort's doctor takes {name} into the infirmary. Hours pass. By sundown {name} is alive — pale, weak, but alive.
choices.narrative_failure: The fort's doctor did everything that could be done. {name} did not survive the fever.

choices.label: Stop and tend to {name}
choices.description: Halt the wagon. Spend 2 days at full rest. Doubled food and water consumption while caring. Best chance of recovery.
choices.narrative_success: Two days of bed rest, broth, cold cloths on the forehead. {name} pulls through. Weak, but alive.
choices.narrative_failure: We did everything we could. The fever was too strong. {name} did not rise on the third morning.

choices.label: Hold a prayer service and continue
choices.description: Push on at half pace. -1 day. Priest present: +20% recovery chance and +1 morale. No Priest: pure faith.
choices.narrative_success_with_priest: {Priest_name} gathers the family at the bedside. They lead them in prayer and song through the night. {name} is held, loved, kept warm, and the wagon does not move. By morning the fever has broken. Some called it grace; some called it the gift of being still and tended.
choices.narrative_success_without_priest: We prayed by the bedside as the wagon rolled. We sat with {name} in turns. By dawn the fever had broken. We continue west.
choices.narrative_failure: We sat with {name} through the night. We held their hand. The fever was too strong. They were lost before morning.

choices.label: Use a Revival (Easy mode only)
choices.description: Spend your one revival to bring {name} back to weakened state. Only available on Easy difficulty. Cannot be used twice.
choices.narrative_success: By some miracle of nature, of grace, of luck, {name} stirs at dawn. They are weak and pale, but they live. The trail goes on.

choices.label: Continue at full pace
choices.description: Push on without stopping. Hardest on the dying member. -1 morale. 10% recovery chance only.
choices.narrative_success: Against all reason, {name} held on through the bouncing wagon. By dusk they spoke their first words in three days.
choices.narrative_failure: {name} did not survive the day's travel. We buried them at sundown.

pre_death_intervention.doctor_modifier: If the team has a Doctor in healthy or weakened state, the recovery probability for 'stop_and_tend' shifts to the 'with_doctor' value. Add +0.15 to all other choices' recovery_probability when Doctor is present.
pre_death_intervention.audio_during_event: The 'oof_oof_oof' state loop continues (member is still in dying state). Music ducks to 0.04. Scene composes with the dying member as the focal figure, in a tent or on a bedroll.
pre_death_intervention.ui_pacing: This is a major event. Hold the intro for 3 seconds before showing choices. Choices are presented as four large cards (or 2-3 cards if some unavailable). After choice, narrative resolves over 4-5 seconds before the actual outcome (recovery or death) is revealed.

per_member_health_bar.philosophy: Visual feedback at-a-glance. Each portrait in the team strip carries a small horizontal health bar below the name. Color-coded by state. Players see at a glance who is in trouble.

specification.size: 48px wide × 4px tall, sits below the member name in the team strip portrait

color_by_state.lost: transparent (no bar shown — replaced by ✝ icon)

specification.background: rgba(61, 40, 23, 0.15) — faint brown trough so empty bars are still visible
specification.transitions: When state changes, bar animates over 0.6s to new color and width with ease-out timing

teacher_mode_use_cases.philosophy: Teacher Mode is for graded play, not casual play. Most class periods will have it OFF. Turning it on adds a printable rubric report at the end. Teachers should explain to students when it's on.
when_to_turn_on: End-of-unit assessment — students play in pairs/teams as their summative grade for the Westward Expansion unit
Take-home assignment (1-2 students per wagon) where the teacher needs evidence of engagement and learning
Substitute teacher day — produces tangible evidence of work without the substitute needing to grade live
Parent-teacher conferences — board + journals make a striking artifact; rubric report makes the grading defensible
Documentation for IEP, 504, or learning-objective requirements
when_to_keep_off: First exposure to the game (kids should learn the mechanics without grading pressure)
Open-ended exploration where the goal is curiosity rather than assessment

ui_setup_screen_copy.label: Teacher Mode (off by default)
ui_setup_screen_copy.explanation: Turn this on ONLY if today's play is being graded. Adds a printable rubric report at the end of the game showing which historical content the team encountered, what choices they made, and how they performed on the bandit quiz. Most plays should leave this OFF — the game is more fun without grading pressure.
ui_setup_screen_copy.placement: Last item on Setup Screen 5 (the 'Ready to roll' screen), as a small toggle switch. Default off.

teacher_mode_use_cases.report_format: Letter-portrait HTML page, printable, with: student names, wagon name, date, game length, mode, difficulty, team composition, total events encountered, total choices made, total facts shown, bandit quiz score, forts visited, rivers crossed, pace strategy, rations strategy, members lost (with names/days/causes), final outcome, score breakdown, and the full Trail Journal. Designed to be graded against a rubric the teacher provides.

historical_illustrations.philosophy: Real history makes the game educational. Each major landmark and fort gets a period-styled illustration shown alongside the Did You Know fact. Default is daguerreotype-style SVG illustration drawn by the build (sepia, oval frame, period-accurate subject matter). Optional upgrade path: Nicholas can replace any illustration with a real public-domain historical photograph by adding base64-encoded image data to a manifest constant.

daguerreotype_specifications.frame: Oval frame (300px wide × 230px tall), warm cream border 6px, then sepia border 2px, then black inner edge 1px
daguerreotype_specifications.color_palette: Sepia tones only — #5C4A36 for darks, #A89070 for midtones, #D4C19E for highs, #FBF6F0 for paper-white background
daguerreotype_specifications.texture: Subtle grain via SVG filter feTurbulence at low opacity (0.08)
daguerreotype_specifications.vignette: Dark sepia vignette at edges, fading to lighter center
daguerreotype_specifications.ornament: Small decorative flourish in corner with year (e.g., '1849') in serif script

locations.name: Fort Laramie
locations.subject: Wood-and-adobe walled fort with American flag flying. Two-story blockhouse visible. A few wagons gathered outside the gates. Mountains in distance.
locations.caption: Fort Laramie, Wyoming, c. 1849. Originally a fur trading post (Fort William, 1834), it became a U.S. Army fort in 1849. The most important resupply point on the Oregon Trail.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'Fort Laramie 1858 Albert Bierstadt' or 'Fort Laramie LC-USZ62'

locations.name: Chimney Rock
locations.subject: Tall slender spire of clay and sandstone rising hundreds of feet from the prairie. Distinct cone-and-shaft silhouette. Wagon train visible in foreground for scale.
locations.caption: Chimney Rock, Nebraska. The most-mentioned landmark in pioneer journals. Visible from over 30 miles away across the plains. The spire was already eroding by 1848.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'Chimney Rock Nebraska Jackson' or 'Chimney Rock 1866'

locations.name: Independence Rock
locations.subject: Massive rounded granite dome, like a turtle shell, rising 130 feet from the plain. Names visible carved into the stone. Sweetwater River curving past it.
locations.caption: Independence Rock, Wyoming. Pioneers tried to reach this landmark by July 4 — 'Independence Day' — to ensure they would clear the mountains before winter. Thousands of names are still carved in its surface today.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'Independence Rock Wyoming 1870' or 'W. H. Jackson Independence Rock'

locations.name: Soda Springs
locations.subject: Naturally bubbling springs in a high meadow. Small steaming pools. A few pioneers gathered around tasting the water. Surrounding hills with sparse pines.
locations.caption: Soda Springs, Idaho. Naturally carbonated mineral springs that pioneers used to make a kind of fizzy bread. A favorite rest stop. Now mostly submerged under the Soda Point Reservoir.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'Soda Springs Idaho Hayden Survey 1872'

locations.name: South Pass
locations.subject: Wide gentle saddle between two distant mountain ranges. Dry grass plains. Almost no apparent climb. A line of wagons crossing.
locations.caption: South Pass, Wyoming. The Continental Divide here is so gentle that most pioneers didn't realize they had crossed the highest point of their journey. The 'hidden doorway' to the West.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'South Pass Wyoming Jackson 1870'

locations.name: Fort Bridger
locations.subject: Modest log palisade fort with a few buildings inside. Mountain man Jim Bridger himself perhaps visible. Trading post atmosphere.
locations.caption: Fort Bridger, Wyoming. Founded 1843 by mountain man Jim Bridger as a private trading post. By 1848, a critical resupply point for emigrants on the cutoff trails.

locations.name: Whitman Mission
locations.subject: Small white-painted clapboard mission house with a wooden cross. A garden visible. Solemn empty atmosphere. (The mission was destroyed in November 1847.)
locations.caption: Whitman Mission, Washington. Founded by Marcus and Narcissa Whitman in 1836 — among the first Americans to cross the Rockies overland. The mission was destroyed in 1847 in a tragic conflict with the Cayuse during a measles epidemic.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'Whitman Mission Walla Walla' (note: only post-destruction photos exist)

locations.name: The Dalles
locations.subject: Columbia River narrowing dramatically through black basalt cliffs. Native fishermen on rocky platforms. Dangerous rapids visible.
locations.caption: The Dalles, Oregon. The narrowest point of the Columbia River, where the water funnels through volcanic rock. Native fishermen had used these falls for thousands of years. Pioneers had to choose: risk the rapids or take the brutal Barlow Road over the Cascades.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'The Dalles Oregon Columbia River Carleton Watkins'

locations.name: Oregon City
locations.subject: Small frontier town beside a great waterfall (Willamette Falls). Wooden buildings, a few church spires. Forested hills behind. Founded by John McLoughlin.
locations.caption: Oregon City, Oregon. The end of the Oregon Trail. Founded in 1842 by John McLoughlin of the Hudson's Bay Company, who became known as the 'Father of Oregon' for the help he gave countless arriving pioneers.
locations.historical_photo_search: Library of Congress: 'Oregon City 1850' or 'Willamette Falls Oregon'

locations.name: Fort Kearney
locations.caption: Fort Kearney, Nebraska. Built in 1848 to protect Oregon Trail travelers along the Platte. The fort had no walls — just buildings on the open prairie.

locations.name: Fort Hall
locations.caption: Fort Hall, Idaho. Built by Nathaniel Wyeth in 1834, sold to Hudson's Bay Company. The last real resupply before the Snake River.

locations.name: Fort Boise
locations.caption: Fort Boise, Idaho. Hudson's Bay Company trading post on the Snake River. Resupply before the Blue Mountains.

locations.name: Fort Nez Perces
locations.caption: Fort Nez Perces (Walla Walla), Washington. Hudson's Bay Company post near the Whitman Mission.

locations.name: Ash Hollow
locations.caption: Ash Hollow, Nebraska. Pioneers locked their wagon wheels and slid down California Hill into this lush spring-fed valley.

locations.name: Courthouse Rock
locations.caption: Courthouse and Jail Rocks, Nebraska. Two clay-and-sandstone formations that pioneers passed before reaching Chimney Rock.

locations.name: Scotts Bluff
locations.caption: Scotts Bluff, Nebraska. Eight hundred feet of bluff named for Hiram Scott, a fur trapper who died here in 1828. Mitchell Pass cuts through it.

locations.name: Snake River
locations.caption: The Snake River, Idaho. Cut deep canyons through black lava rock. Pioneers could see the water hundreds of feet below the trail but often could not reach it. The Shoshone and Nez Perce knew the safe descent points; pioneers who hired guides survived the dry days.

locations.name: Columbia River
locations.caption: Wagons rafted the Columbia River through the Cascades — sometimes lashed three at a time onto great log rafts. Until the Barlow Road opened in 1846, the river was the only way through. Many wagons were lost at the rapids.

locations.name: Platte River
locations.caption: The Platte River, Nebraska. 'A mile wide and an inch deep, too thick to drink and too thin to plow,' said the pioneers. The trail followed its broad valley for over six hundred miles — the longest single landmark of the Oregon Trail.

locations.name: Kansas River
locations.caption: Kansas River ferry, c.1850. Pioneers paid 50 cents to a dollar a wagon to be ferried across by Kansa or Pottawatomie ferrymen. The first ferry was started by Joseph and Louis Papin in 1846. Cheaper than caulking and floating — but only if the wagon master had cash.

locations.name: Green River
locations.caption: The Green River, Wyoming. Swift, deep, and cold from mountain snow. Three to five ferries operated here at peak season; the wait could be two days. Many drowned trying to ford instead of pay the toll.

locations.name: Register Cliff
locations.caption: Register Cliff, Wyoming. A soft sandstone bluff where pioneers carved their names just past Fort Laramie.

locations.name: Trail Ruts
locations.caption: Wagon ruts in solid sandstone, near Guernsey, Wyoming. Worn five feet deep by 250,000 pioneers over twenty-five years.

locations.name: Massacre Rocks
locations.caption: Massacre Rocks, Idaho. A narrow squeeze through black lava boulders. On August 9-10, 1862, a Shoshone party attacked four wagon trains here, killing ten emigrants. The grim nickname stuck. The Shoshone, displaced by emigrant traffic that destroyed game and grass, were among the most-tested Native nations on the trail.

locations.name: Farewell Bend
locations.caption: Farewell Bend, Oregon. Pioneers' last view of the Snake River before turning north into the Burnt River canyon.

locations.name: Willamette Falls
locations.caption: Willamette Falls at Oregon City. The forty-foot waterfall pioneers heard before they saw — the trail's end was just below.

locations.name: Blue Mountains
locations.caption: The Blue Mountains, Oregon. Steep, forested, the last hard climb before the Columbia. Snow could close them by October.

locations.name: The Great Plains
locations.caption: The Great Plains. A sea of grass stretching to the horizon. Pioneers spoke of the loneliness as much as the beauty.

locations.name: Mountain Snowstorm
locations.caption: A late-season storm in the high country. The wagon master prayed for an early frost to harden the trail, not a blizzard to bury it.

locations.name: Wagon Lashed for Descent
locations.caption: A wagon lashed with ropes and chains for a steep descent. Wheels locked, oxen straining backward to hold the load.

locations.name: Wagon at Camp
locations.caption: A pioneer family's wagon at evening camp. The cookfire, the picketed oxen, the tarp drawn over the wagon bed for sleeping.

locations.name: Covered Wagon
locations.caption: A prairie schooner — the small, light wagon most pioneers used. Ten feet long, four wide, hickory bows holding up a canvas top.

locations.name: Sunset on the Sage
locations.caption: Sunset over the sagebrush plains. The smell of warm sage carried for miles in the evening air.

locations.name: Jim Bridger
locations.caption: Jim Bridger, mountain man and trail-blazer. Founded Fort Bridger. Could not read or write, but knew every river west of the Missouri.

locations.name: Ezra Meeker
locations.caption: Ezra Meeker. Crossed the trail in 1852 at twenty-two; spent the rest of his long life preserving its memory and marking the route.

locations.name: Tabitha Brown
locations.caption: Tabitha Brown. Crossed the trail in 1846 at sixty-six. Founded a school for orphans in Forest Grove, Oregon, that became Pacific University.

locations.name: George Washington Bush
locations.caption: George Washington Bush, a free Black settler who brought his family across in 1844. Settled in what is now Washington Territory after Oregon's Black exclusion law.

locations.name: Chimney Rock (alternate view)
locations.caption: Chimney Rock, Nebraska — a second period photograph. The spire was already eroding by the time pioneers passed.

locations.name: Monument Rocks
locations.caption: Monument Rocks (the Chalk Pyramids), Kansas. Seventy-foot chalk-and-shale spires rising from the prairie south of the trail. Pioneers detoured to see them. The chalk was so soft that wagons could carve names into the base. Now the first National Natural Landmark in the United States (1968).

locations.name: City of Rocks
locations.caption: City of Rocks, Idaho. A maze of 60-foot granite spires where the California Trail branched south from the Oregon Trail. Pioneers camped in the shade of the rocks and signed their names in axle grease — many signatures are still readable today, 175 years later. About one in three emigrants chose California from this junction.

locations.name: Buffalo Stampede
locations.caption: A buffalo stampede on the Great Plains. Herds numbered in the tens of thousands; when one panicked, the ground shook for miles. Pioneers who saw a stampede coming had minutes to drive the wagons into a creek bed or be trampled. By 1880, the buffalo were nearly gone.

locations.name: Columbia River (gorge view)
locations.caption: The Columbia River Gorge cuts through the Cascade Range — the only sea-level path through the mountains in 1,000 miles. The river offered the trail's last great obstacle and its most beautiful stretch.

locations.name: Cholera Warning
locations.caption: A period broadside warning of cholera. The disease killed more pioneers than anything else on the trail — sometimes a strong, healthy person felt a stomach ache at breakfast and was dead by sundown. Spread through dirty water at busy river crossings. Boiling water for two minutes was the only sure prevention pioneers had.

locations.name: Pioneer Family
locations.caption: A pioneer family at their wagon. About a third of all emigrants were children. Mothers cooked, washed, nursed the sick, watched the little ones, and walked the same 2,000 miles as everyone else. The diaries pioneer women kept survive today and tell us most of what we know about daily trail life.

locations.name: Native Americans of the Trail
locations.caption: Native peoples of the Oregon Trail — Pawnee, Sioux, Shoshone, Nez Perce, Cayuse, Walla Walla, and many others. They guided pioneers across rivers, traded fish and horses, taught the use of camas root, and warned of bad water. Far more often than fighting, they helped — and watched their lands and game disappear under wagon wheels.

locations.name: Wagon Train at Dusk
locations.caption: A pioneer wagon train circled for the night. The wagons formed a corral that kept the oxen safe and let families cook over a single shared fire. Songs after supper, prayer at sundown. Pioneers slept on the ground, not in the wagons — the wagons were full of supplies.

locations.name: Wagons on the Plains
locations.caption: A wagon train crossing the Great Plains. On a good day a wagon train made 12 to 20 miles. The trail was so heavily used by 1850 that the ruts were already a foot deep — in some places they remain visible today.

locations.name: Period Pioneer Wagon
locations.caption: A prairie schooner — the small, light wagon most pioneers used. About 10 feet long, 4 wide, with a canvas top stretched over hickory bows. Loaded weight around 1,500 pounds, plus another 1,500 of supplies. Pulled by 4 to 6 oxen at about 2 miles per hour.

locations.name: Wagons in the Distance
locations.caption: A line of wagons stretching to the horizon. By 1849, the year of the California Gold Rush, more than 25,000 emigrants crossed in a single season — the trail looked like a mile-wide stripe of dust and canvas, visible for thirty miles.

upgrade_path_for_real_photos.instructions: To enhance the game with real public-domain historical photographs, Nicholas (or anyone) can: (1) Visit Library of Congress (loc.gov/photos) and search for the suggested terms above; (2) Download the highest-resolution version of each photo; (3) Resize to 600x460 max and save as JPG with quality 85; (4) Convert to base64 using any base64 tool (Windows PowerShell: [Convert]::ToBase64String([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes('photo.jpg'))); (5) Add the base64 string to the historicalPhotosOverride constant in pink_oregon_trail.html under the matching id key. The game checks the override first; if present, displays the photograph; if not, falls back to the daguerreotype SVG. All Library of Congress photographs from before 1928 are public domain.
upgrade_path_for_real_photos.default: Ship with daguerreotype SVG illustrations. Real photo upgrade is optional.

testing_protocol.philosophy: The wagon decomposition bug and the viewport zoom bug were both visual issues that surface immediately in testing but were not caught before delivery. This protocol prevents that class of failure. Run all tests before declaring the build complete.

1_visual_smoke_test.description: Open the game in browser. Take screenshots at 7 standard resolutions. Verify visually.
checks_per_resolution: All UI elements visible without horizontal scroll
Team strip portraits visible (may scroll horizontally on narrow screens)
Narrative callout fits within scene canvas
1_visual_smoke_test.pass_criteria: All 7 resolutions show usable layouts. Below 1024 wide, layout may degrade gracefully but must still be navigable.

2_wagon_integrity_test.description: Travel 10 spaces. Watch the wagon at each.
checks: Wagon body, ox, wheels, and yoke all move together as one unit
Ox stays in front of wagon, attached via yoke
Wheels rotate beneath wagon body, never drifting outside the wagon footprint
Wagon faces RIGHT (ox to the right of wagon body)
Wagon translates rightward when traveling, never appearing to move backward
2_wagon_integrity_test.pass_criteria: Zero visible decomposition across 10 consecutive moves.

3_responsive_test.description: Resize browser window from 1920px wide down to 800px wide. Then back up.
checks: Layout reflows smoothly without breaking
No element gets cut off or overlaps another
Text scales appropriately (not stuck at one size)
Scene canvas always fills its grid cell
3_responsive_test.pass_criteria: Smooth reflow at every width from 800 to 1920.

4_state_machine_test.description: Force resource conditions via console. Verify expected state transitions.

test_cases.expected: Roughly 3-5 of 7 healthy members should transition to weakened or sick. Print team states to console; verify.
Most members should transition to weakened or dying — water effects are stronger than food.

test_cases.setup: Set one member to 'sick' state. Confirm Doctor is on team and healthy.
test_cases.expected: 60% of the time, the sick member should recover to weakened. Run 10 times, count successes.

4_state_machine_test.pass_criteria: Each test case produces results within 20% of expected probability.

5_death_announcement_test.description: Force a member to die via console. Verify all 9 steps of the death announcement protocol fire.
checks_in_order: 2. Scene composes: backdrop dims to 40%, member figure shown center
4. Narrative callout shows name + cause + closing line
5. Team strip portrait grayscales with cross icon
6. Continue button does NOT appear for 2.5 seconds
9. After Continue clicked: music returns to mood pool, game resumes
5_death_announcement_test.pass_criteria: All 9 steps fire in order with no missing step.

6_warning_escalation_test.description: Force healthy → weakened → sick → dying transitions on a single member. Verify each transition produces the correct UI feedback.
checks: healthy → weakened: small toast notification slides in, auto-dismisses in 4s
weakened → sick: larger banner pauses scene, requires Acknowledge click
sick → dying: full intervention event with multiple choices
Choice 'race_to_fort' only available if next fort within 3 spaces
Choice 'use_revival_charge' only available on Easy with charges remaining
After choice, narrative resolves over 4-5 seconds before outcome reveal
6_warning_escalation_test.pass_criteria: All transitions produce escalating UI weight.

7_money_flow_test.description: Run a 25-space Short trail game with default Medium difficulty. Log every money change.
checks: Starting money = 30
Across the journey, hides accumulate from successful hunts
At least 2 of the 12 new money-earning encounter events trigger
Selling at any fort produces money proportional to hides + excess
Forts give clear 'tip card' showing sellable goods on arrival
7_money_flow_test.pass_criteria: End-of-game money is between 20-80 for an averagely-managed game. A spend-only game ends near 0; an income-focused game ends 60-100.

8_music_rotation_test.description: Play the game for 5 minutes at moderate morale. Log every song change.
checks: At least 3 different songs play in 5 minutes
No song plays back-to-back
Songs match mood (hopeful at high morale, weary at low)
Forced death triggers Amazing Grace, then returns to prior mood pool
8_music_rotation_test.pass_criteria: ≥3 songs heard, no immediate repeats, mood shifts produce song shifts.

9_difficulty_test.description: Start a new game on each difficulty. Verify modifiers apply.
checks_easy: Revival charge available in pre-death intervention

10_full_journey_test.description: Play one complete Short journey end-to-end on Medium difficulty. Make ordinary choices.
checks: Setup screens all work, all inputs accept keyboard correctly
Wagon stays intact across all 25 moves
At least one event fires per region transition
Sound effects play (death, money gain, dice, etc.)
Reaching Oregon: cheering and confetti loop until Continue
Trail Journal at end has 15+ paragraphs of period-voice prose
Leaderboard shows seeded entries
Player score appears on leaderboard
10_full_journey_test.pass_criteria: Game can be played from start to finish without console errors or visible bugs.

testing_protocol.delivery_gate: All 10 tests must pass before declaring the build complete. Report results test-by-test in the final build report.
testing_protocol.regression_protocol: When applying any future patch, re-run tests 1, 2, 3, and 10 (the visual + end-to-end tests). They catch the most common regression classes.

dice_system.philosophy: Players need to SEE chance happening. The current 1d4 is invisible and unsatisfying — wagon teleports forward by an inscrutable amount. Replace with a visible 2d6 roll showing both dice, faces and pips, with a brief animation. Cap movement so it doesn't break game pacing.
dice_system.philosophy_on_cap: 2d6 raw can produce 12, but advancing 12 spaces in a single turn would skip 2-3 events and break the pacing of a 25-space Short trail. We cap movement at 8 per turn (after pace and state modifiers) to preserve the chance-of-events density. The dice still SHOW their natural roll (2-12); the displayed movement number after pace/state mods is what advances the wagon.
dice_system.movement_formula: raw_2d6_total + pace_modifier + state_modifier + morale_modifier — clamped to [1, 8]

ui_specification.dice_display_during_push_on: When player clicks Push On, show both dice center-canvas, animated tumbling/shaking for 0.6 seconds, then settling on their face values. Each die shows pips (1-6 dot pattern) on a clean white face with rounded corners.
ui_specification.movement_summary_callout: After dice settle, briefly show: 'Rolled 4 + 5 = 9 — pace +1, team strong +1 = 10 (capped at 8). Travel 8 spaces.' Then resolve the new space.
ui_specification.audio: Dice rattle SFX during tumble (wood-on-table cascade, ~0.5s). Soft chime on settle.
ui_specification.mini_map_animation: After dice settle, mini-map wagon icon slides smoothly from current to new position over 0.8s before space resolution fires.

water_economy.philosophy: Water was a one-source resource (forts only, paid). This violates both common sense (rivers have water; Soda Springs is named for water) and historical reality (pioneers drank from streams, springs, and rivers constantly). Players who tried logical strategies (forage at a river) were punished by the absence of water as a logical reward. Fix this — water comes from many places.

sources.narrative: We filled the barrels from the river before crossing.
sources.note: Already implemented in v2.2 at line 8866 — keep this.

sources.source: Forage at any river or landmark space
sources.narrative: We found a clear stream and filled the barrels.
We dug a small spring and gathered seeps and dew.
We filled every barrel from the bubbling springs. The water is faintly fizzy and tastes of iron.
We let the hot water cool overnight and filled the barrels by morning.

sources.source: Any spring or watering hole encounter event

sources.narrative: The trader sells barreled water by the gallon.
We caught the rain in our pots and barrels.

water_economy.scarcity_design: Water can still be a strategic concern in the high desert region (spaces 33-42 in Extended), where forage yields are halved and there are no rivers. The strategic depth comes from PLANNING ahead at the last river before the desert, not from water being categorically unobtainable everywhere.

morale_impacts_v2_3.philosophy: Morale should be a real strategic resource. Currently it only marginally affects movement (+1 if morale ≥8) and music mood. Make morale matter mechanically across multiple systems.

high_morale_8_to_10: Members heal one state-tier on Rest (instead of by probability)
Surprise events 50% more likely to be positive
Music mood: hopeful (Oh Susanna, Buffalo Gals)
low_morale_2_to_3: Surprise events 30% more likely to be negative
crisis_morale_0_to_1: 10% chance per turn that 1 member quits the wagon (state→lost, cause='abandonment')
25% chance per turn of an Argument event (lose 1 day to camp infighting)

morale_recovery_sources: Rest action: +1 morale per turn rested
morale_drain_sources: Member sick or dying: -1 per turn

day_67_easter_egg.philosophy: Day 67 should be a memorable comedic beat. Sixth-graders find the number 67 inherently funny (in 2026 'six seven' is a popular meme). Pink Oregon Trail can wink at this without breaking the period setting.
day_67_easter_egg.trigger: First time gameState.day reaches 67 in any wagon's journey

scene.title: Day 67 — A Strange Day
scene.intro: It is Day 67. The number sixty-seven. The team looks around and laughs for no clear reason.
flavor_options: Cookie the cook holds up six fingers, then seven. The team breaks into laughter that lasts the whole afternoon.
Someone finds a wagon wheel with six and seven spokes painted in alternating colors. No one knows why.
A passing rider tips his hat and says 'sixty-seven miles to the next fort, or maybe seventy-six. Hard to tell out here.' Nobody knows whether to laugh or cry.
The Priest reads from Psalm 67 around the campfire. The team listens — but somebody chuckles, and then everyone is laughing. The Priest pretends to be offended, then laughs too.
scene.audio: Light, lively flute riff (5-6 quick notes ascending then descending) followed by a soft chime
scene.rendering_note: Pick one flavor at random. Show as a light comedic event in the standard event callout style. Don't repeat in the same journey.

hover_tooltip_system.philosophy: v2.2's transition toasts were fly-by — easy to miss while focused on the action menu. Replace with persistent hover tooltips on team-strip portraits. This way the player can check on any team member at any time, without time pressure.
hover_tooltip_system.removal: Disable .transition-toast entirely. Keep the audio cue (worry chime) when transitions occur, so the player has an auditory hint that something changed.

addition.trigger: Mouse hover over any team-strip portrait (or tap on touch device)
tooltip_content: Current state with description (e.g., 'Sick — coughing, fever. Doctor cannot heal until they recover')
Days in current state (e.g., 'Sick since Day 32 — three days')
Recovery probability if Doctor present (e.g., '60% chance to recover with Doctor today')
Cause of state if known (e.g., 'Cause: starvation')
Profession bonus contribution if active (e.g., 'Hunting: +75% success when this member is healthy')
addition.ui_styling: Soft cream background, deep rose border, drop shadow, max-width 300px. Appears with a 100ms fade-in. Stays until pointer leaves the portrait.

additional_visual_state_signals: Already implemented in v2.2: health bar color and width by state
Add a state-indicator badge under the bar with text: 'healthy' (green), 'tired' (gold), 'sick' (orange), 'hurt' (terracotta), 'dying' (red, pulsing)
On state change, briefly flash the entire portrait with a colored glow matching the new state, lasting 1.5 seconds (this replaces the toast as the immediate visual hint)

minimap_upgrade.philosophy: Currently the mini-map shows a simplified line. Players using the physical board need a screen-board that MATCHES it: the same 6-tile layout, same key landmarks visible, all wagons positioned in real time. This becomes the bridge between physical and digital play.

specification.size_px: 320 wide x 200 tall (expandable to 640x400 on click)
specification.layout: 3 tiles wide × 2 tile rows, matching the physical board exactly
specification.tile_content: Simplified version of each board tile: trail line through key spaces, named landmarks at correct positions, region color tinting (plains tan, foothills mauve, mountains purple-grey, desert beige, forest green)
specification.wagon_markers: Each wagon shown as a small colored circle with the player's initial inside (e.g., 'N' for Nicholas, 'G' for Gabby). Wagons in distinct colors (deep rose, gold, navy, forest green, plum, copper for up to 6 players).
specification.current_player_marker: Currently-active wagon's marker pulses with a soft halo and is slightly larger.
specification.expandability: Click/tap to expand to full-screen overlay (640x400 view of the full board). Click again to collapse.
specification.always_visible: Mini-map docks in bottom-left corner, always visible during gameplay (not collapsed by default).

turn_indicator_system.philosophy: In multi-wagon games (Cooperative or Competitive), players need to constantly know whose turn it is. Currently the player name is buried; players lose track. Make this prominent and persistent.

specification.turn_banner_top_of_scene: When a wagon's turn begins, a banner slides down from the top of the scene canvas reading: '[Player Name]'s turn — [Wagon Name]' in large serif italic. Holds for 2.5 seconds, then minimizes to a small persistent corner badge.
specification.persistent_corner_badge: Top-right of scene canvas, always visible during turn: 'Now playing: [Player Name]' with the wagon's color accent. Click to expand to full team strip preview.
specification.audio_cue: Soft, cheerful 2-note chime when the banner appears (different from the worry-chime used for state transitions).
specification.wagon_color_accents: Each wagon has an assigned color (deep rose, gold, navy, forest green, plum, copper). The action menu border, mini-map marker, and turn banner all use this color for the active wagon, so multiple players don't lose track of which wagon is theirs.

trail_news_upgrade.philosophy: Trail News was 'shows next 3 stops by name' which is barely useful — players already see this on the mini-map. Make it real: surface upcoming events, hazards, weather forecasts, or tips that meaningfully aid strategy.

content: Hazard hint if upcoming spaces include a tough river: 'A traveler reports the Snake River is running high.'
