Weather hint if upcoming weather event due (40% chance of being available): 'Storm clouds on the horizon. Plan accordingly.'
Profession-relevant tip: if a Doctor is on team, 'Cholera reports from the Platte River Valley.' If a Carpenter, 'A wagon train reports broken axles ahead — bring extra supplies.'
Random fact tied to next landmark: 'The next big landmark is Independence Rock — pioneers try to reach it by July 4.'
Strategic tip: 'Forts are scarce in the high desert region. Stock up here.'
specification.narrative_voice: First-person colloquial pioneer voice. 'A trader from the west says the river's still passable. Won't be in two weeks.'
specification.tip_card_format: Show as a stylized broadside / handbill — period-appropriate styling. Each tip on its own line with bullet, max 4-5 tips per news session.

simulation_logic_validation.philosophy: Build the v2.3 patch with explicit user-story / use-case tests, BEFORE writing the code. Each scenario must be reasoned through end-to-end, validating that the simulation produces a sensible narrative outcome that matches player expectations. This is how the priest/banker swap, water-but-not bug, and skinTone bugs would have been caught.

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: Player has a Priest and a Banker on the team. The Banker is in dying state. End of turn fires.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: If Banker dies: Banker name, profession, and skinTone (consistent across selection, naming, gameplay) appear in the death scene. Team strip updates with Banker grayscaled. Priest unchanged. If Banker lives: Banker remains in dying state with health bar pulsing red; intervention may fire next turn.

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: Player selects 7 professions on screen 4 (e.g., Doctor, Hunter, Carpenter, Cook, Scout, Cowboy, Priest). Continues to Naming, names them. Hits Back to return to selection. Deselects Carpenter. Selects Influencer instead. Returns to Naming. Continues. Begins journey.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: In the in-game team strip, each profession shows the name and skin tone the player gave it for THAT profession. Names and skin tones MUST stay aligned to their professions across array mutations. Specifically: if Carpenter is removed from index 2 of the professions array, name[2] and skinTone[2] (which were associated with Carpenter) must ALSO be removed. When Influencer is appended at the end, name[6] and skinTone[6] must be empty/freshly-randomized so the player can name the new member from scratch.

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: A wagon lands on a river space. Player chooses Ford. Player succeeds. The next time their turn comes around (in Competitive mode), they look at the screen.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: The screen shows them at the NEXT space (or at least the previously-resolved river space without re-firing the river-crossing scene). The turn-intro callout says something appropriate to their current position, NOT the river that has been crossed already.

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: Player chooses Forage. Their team has no Scout, no Farmer.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: They get +1-2 food AND +1-2 water (logically — foraging includes finding water sources). With Farmer: more food. With Scout: more water. Without either: still get reasonable amounts of both.
They get the historical fact (existing) AND they refill water (because Soda Springs is literally named for its water). Morale +1 (the team rests at this famous landmark).

Two dice appear center-canvas, tumble for 0.6s, land on values (e.g. 4 and 5). A summary appears: 'Rolled 4+5 = 9, pace +1, capped at 8 — travel 8 spaces.' Then the wagon advances visibly along the mini-map. Then the new space resolves.
A special light comedic event fires. Morale +2. A flute riff plays. The team laughs about the number 67. (Sixth-grade kids will laugh too.)

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: Player hovers over a team-strip portrait.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: A tooltip appears showing member name, profession, current state with description, days in state, recovery probability if a Doctor is present, cause of state, and profession bonus contribution. The toast notifications (which were fly-by) are gone — hover gives persistent info instead.

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: The team's morale drops to 1 (crisis level). Two more turns pass.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: Push On movement is reduced by 2. There's a 10% chance per turn one member quits (lost with abandonment narrative). Music shifts to somber (Shenandoah only). Hunt success drops 25%. The player feels the desperation and is motivated to find a fort or successful event.

required_user_stories_pre_implementation.story: Multiple players (4) play in Competitive mode.
required_user_stories_pre_implementation.expected: A turn banner appears at the top of scene at start of each wagon's turn showing the player name. A persistent corner badge shows 'Now playing: [Name]'. Each wagon has its own color accent on action menu, mini-map marker, and turn banner. The mini-map shows all 4 wagons in their colors at their respective positions.

simulation_logic_validation.test_protocol: Before writing code for any v2.3 feature, write a brief paragraph describing how the user story plays out in the patched code. If anything is unclear or contradictory, surface it as an open question BEFORE writing the implementation. After writing the code, manually walk through the user story step-by-step verifying each expected outcome holds.

team_alignment_fix.philosophy: Names and skin tones are PER-PROFESSION metadata. They must stay aligned to their profession across all array mutations. The bug in v2.2 is that .splice() on professions doesn't propagate to names/skinTones, leaving members at higher indices with the wrong metadata.
team_alignment_fix.the_actual_bug_observed: A player picks Doctor/Hunter/Carpenter/Cook/Scout/Cowboy/Priest, names them all, hits Back, deselects Carpenter, adds Influencer. The professions array becomes [Doctor, Hunter, Cook, Scout, Cowboy, Priest, Influencer]. But the names array is still [DrBill, Bob, Carla(was-Carpenter), Cookie, ScoutSam, CowboyJoe, FatherPat]. Result: in-game, Cook is named 'Carla', Scout is named 'Cookie', Cowboy is named 'ScoutSam', Priest is named 'CowboyJoe', and Influencer is named 'FatherPat'. The diligent player who picked specific roles sees their work erased.
team_alignment_fix.required_invariant: At ALL times when w.stage === 'naming' or beyond, w.names.length === w.professions.length AND w.skinTones.length === w.professions.length, and the i-th element of names/skinTones describes the i-th profession.
implementation_pattern: When deselecting a profession (selection stage onclick): perform the splice on professions[idx], names[idx], skinTones[idx] in lock-step.
When appending a profession (selection stage onclick): push '' to names and a fresh random skin tone to skinTones at the same time.
When entering naming stage: defensively reconcile — truncate names/skinTones to professions.length, pad with empty/random as needed.
Apply the same lock-step principle to any future code that mutates professions.
team_alignment_fix.non_destructive_alternative_to_consider: An alternative architecture is to make team members objects from the start: w.team = [{profession, name, skinTone}, ...]. Splice operates on whole objects atomically — alignment becomes structural, not procedural. This is cleaner but a bigger refactor. The minimal fix is the lock-step splice approach above. Use your judgment.

fort_visit_system.philosophy: v2.3 made fort visits mathematically rare (Monte Carlo: avg 0.52 forts of 4 visited per Short journey, 54% of journeys visited zero). The 2d6-capped-at-8 dice produces 42% of moves at exactly 8 spaces, causing systematic overshoot. Fix: three paths to a fort, each with different cost/agency. Forts (and only forts) become decision points instead of probabilistic landing zones.

three_paths.trigger: 2d6 roll lands wagon EXACTLY on a fort space
three_paths.ui: Wagon advances normally, fort menu opens automatically (existing v2.2 behavior)

three_paths.trigger: 2d6 roll WOULD CARRY the wagon past a fort it has not visited yet on this journey
three_paths.cost: Forfeit unused movement (e.g., rolled 8, fort is 5 spaces ahead, stopping forfeits 3 spaces)
three_paths.ui: After dice settle but before wagon moves, popup callout: 'You see [Fort Name] ahead at space [N]. Your team is [pace_descriptor]. Stop here, or push past?' Two large buttons: 'Stop at the Fort' (forfeits N-D spaces of movement) / 'Push Past' (continues to original destination)
three_paths.implementation_note: Insert this prompt between the dice settle animation and the wagon position update in doActionPushOn. Once the player chooses, set wagon position accordingly and call resolveSpace.
three_paths.fort_filter: Only forts the wagon has not yet visited this journey. Re-visiting an already-visited fort doesn't trigger the prompt (keeps pacing brisk).
three_paths.audio: Soft chime when the prompt appears; click on either choice continues the turn

three_paths.trigger: Player clicks the new 'Make for the Fort' action button BEFORE rolling Push On dice. Available when an unvisited fort is within 5 spaces.
three_paths.cost: 1 extra day per 3 spaces traveled (rounded up). Wagon advances directly to the fort regardless of dice. Skips the Push On dice roll entirely for this turn.
three_paths.ui: Fifth action button on the action bar (alongside Push On / Hunt / Rest / Forage), only shown when the trigger condition is met. Button label: 'Make for the Fort' with subtitle showing the cost: 'Push hard for [Fort Name] — [N] spaces, +[D] day(s)'.
three_paths.members_dying_bonus: If any member is in dying state, the day cost is reduced by 1 (minimum 1). Frame as 'urgency drives the team'.
three_paths.narrative: The wagon master sets a hard pace. We do not stop, even at dusk. We push for [Fort Name].
three_paths.audio: Louder dice rattle replaced by a steady drumbeat of hooves; wagon icon moves more aggressively across the mini-map

fort_visit_system.rivers_landmarks_unaffected: Voluntary-stop applies ONLY to forts. Rivers and landmarks are story beats — if your roll carries the wagon across a river or past a landmark, it resolves as normal. No 'skip past' option. Forts are the only decision-point space type because they are the only space type where the player gains discretionary mechanical benefits (buy/sell/repair/rest/letter).
fort_visit_system.expected_visit_rate: Combined three paths should bring average fort visits per Short journey from 0.52 to ~2.5-3.0 of 4. Players with sick/dying members should reliably reach a fort via forced march. Average wagons on a quiet journey should still visit 2-3 forts via voluntary stops alone.

race_to_fort_fix.philosophy: The intervention's race_to_fort choice currently adds 1 day and a +30% recovery bonus but never moves the wagon. Players who choose 'race to fort' for a dying member end up still at the same trail position with a member who recovered (or died) by some unexplained mechanism. The narrative lies about what happened. Fix: the choice actually moves the wagon to the fort.
implementation: 1. Find the next unvisited fort via distanceToNextFort(w)
2. Move wagon position directly: w.position = position_of_next_fort

5. After the recovery narrative resolves and the player clicks Continue, the next renderActionMenu naturally fires resolveSpace which triggers resolveFort because the wagon is now standing on a fort space
6. The player gets full fort benefits (buy medicine, repair, rest, etc.) automatically
race_to_fort_fix.narrative_hook: Append a line to the recovery narrative: 'We pull into the gates of [Fort Name] at sundown.' On failure: 'We pull into the gates of [Fort Name]. {name} did not see them.'
race_to_fort_fix.interaction_with_path_3: Forced march and race_to_fort use the same underlying movement — race_to_fort is essentially a forced march triggered by the intervention flow. Consider extracting a moveWagonToFort(w) helper that both code paths call.

historical_photo_system_v2_4.philosophy: v2.3's URL-based fetcher hard-failed on zero successful URLs and exited without writing the override block. URLs to LoC images are also unstable — what worked when I wrote the spec may 404 a month later. Switch to a folder-based system as the primary mechanism.
historical_photo_system_v2_4.primary_mechanism: Local folder. Nicholas places JPG files in historical_photos/ at the project root. The fetcher script reads any files matching the 9 landmark IDs (fort_laramie.jpg, chimney_rock.jpg, etc.) and writes them as base64 into the override block. Files he doesn't include simply fall back to the daguerreotype SVG.
historical_photo_system_v2_4.secondary_mechanism: URL fallback. If a landmark has no local file, the script attempts to fetch a curated URL. If that fails, it skips silently and the SVG fallback is used.
key_behavioral_changes: Script writes the override block ALWAYS, even if zero photos were obtained (so the consumer code reliably finds window.HISTORICAL_PHOTOS_OVERRIDE).
Verbose logging shows EXACTLY what was found, what was skipped, and where to download missing photos.
Idempotent: re-running with new files in the folder updates the block; removing the folder reverts to all-SVG.
Per-image diagnostics: dimensions, file size, base64 size.
recommended_sources_for_nicholas: Library of Congress Photos (loc.gov/photos) — search the location name, click an image, click 'Download' → choose 'JPEG'. Public domain.
Wikimedia Commons (commons.wikimedia.org) — search the location name + 'historical', filter by 'public domain'. Right-click image → 'Save Image As'.
Oregon-California Trails Association (octa-trails.org) — has historical photo archives.
If no period photo is available for a landmark (e.g., Whitman Mission was destroyed in 1847 and no daguerreotypes survived), use any historically-themed illustration or leave the SVG fallback.

bandit_overshoot_fix.philosophy: v2.3 set the bandit ambush to fire at exact equality on space 47 (Extended) or 23 (Short). Any wagon that overshoots that space due to dice never encounters the bandits — and never gets the climactic test of the historical content they were shown along the trail. This makes the educational climax of the game silently optional. Fix: when a Push On roll would carry the wagon past the unvisited bandit space, the bandits stop the wagon at their ambush space (forfeiting unused movement). The bandits are FORCED, not voluntary — they're literally blocking the trail.
implementation: In doActionPushOn, after dice settle but before setting w.position:
2. Check: would this carry the wagon past the unvisited bandit space?
3. If yes: set w.position = banditSpace, log 'The bandits stop the wagon at [name]', fire triggerBanditAmbush(w)
4. If the wagon LANDS exactly on the bandit space (existing behavior): same flow, bandits fire normally
5. The bandit ambush check should happen BEFORE the voluntary-fort-stop check (bandits are involuntary, take precedence)
bandit_overshoot_fix.narrative_when_intercepted: The bandits saw us coming. They have us cornered at [N] miles short of where we meant to camp. There is no choice but to face them.
edge_cases: If the bandit space falls exactly on a fort or river: the bandit ambush fires first. Fort/river resolve afterward on next turn.
If a wagon is forced-marched (Path 3) past the bandit space: same overshoot logic applies. The bandits intercept.
If race_to_fort moves the wagon to a fort beyond the bandit space: bandits intercept. The dying member's intervention is paused; bandits fight first; intervention resolves after.

death_path_unification.philosophy: v2.3 introduced the 9-step death announcement protocol with the figure overlay, funeral hymn, and proper hold time. But it only fires for natural-progression deaths (state machine starvation/illness). Catastrophic deaths from rivers, bandits, and winter still use their old showOutcomeCallout flow with no figure overlay. Player sees those deaths only as text, not as a moment. Unify them.
scope: River drowning failures (in resolveRiver doRiver function)
Catastrophic event deaths (any event with 'lost_member' in narrative effects)
Morale-driven abandonment ('crisis morale' tier in v2.3)
implementation_pattern: Each catastrophic-death code path currently does: transitionMember(...) + showOutcomeCallout(...) + onContinue=>endTurn

Change to: 1) show the OUTCOME callout with the event narrative (river took them / bandits killed them) → 2) on Continue click, route to processDeathAnnouncements(w, [deceased], () => endTurn) → 3) protocol fires the figure overlay, funeral hymn, 4-second hold → 4) on protocol Continue click, endTurn fires
Result: every member loss in the game produces the same visual beat: outcome text → figure overlay → 'X has died' → continue

overlay_prominence_upgrades.figure_size: Change from clamp(120px, 22%, 240px) wide to clamp(180px, 40%, 360px) wide — roughly double
overlay_prominence_upgrades.death_stamp_text: Add a prominent 'X has died' or 'X is lost' centered overlay above the figure with weight 700, deep rose color, drop shadow

death_path_unification.audio_unification: Funeral hymn already plays once on first death (audioController.switchToFuneral). Subsequent deaths in the same journey reuse the same fade-to-funeral. Don't double-trigger.

post_death_calendar_drift_fix.philosophy: Playtest leaderboard showed Daddy's Wagon ending at '0 of 7 alive, day 137' and Matthew's at 'day 154' — wagons that lost all members but kept the calendar advancing for many days afterward. Suspected cause: catastrophic deaths (river/bandit/winter) call transitionMember directly and route through showOutcomeCallout to endTurn, but the elimination check in completeEndTurn happens AFTER w.daysTraveled += gameState.daysPerTurn. So a wagon whose last member dies in a river drowning gets one more turn of calendar advance before being marked eliminated. In Competitive mode with rotation across multiple wagons, an eliminated-but-not-yet-flagged wagon may also get a few extra days while waiting for its turn to come back around.
fix: Move the elimination check to BEFORE the w.daysTraveled increment in completeEndTurn

Also check elimination in the catastrophic-death code paths (river, bandit, winter) immediately after transitionMember — if livingMembers(w).length === 0, set w.eliminated = true synchronously, before any callout fires
The elimination scene callout still shows; it just shows AFTER the death announcement, with the days-traveled stamped at the actual day of last death — not days later
post_death_calendar_drift_fix.expected_behavior: A wagon whose last member dies on day N should show day N as the journey end day, not day N+4 or N+8. Across the full journey, eliminated wagons should never advance the calendar past the day the last member died.

graphics_overhaul_v2_6.philosophy: Players described v2.5 graphics as 'pre-1980' — vector silhouettes with flat colors. The fix is dramatically richer SVG: layered parallax scenery, gradient atmospheres, animated weather, detailed character figures with state-dependent expressions, real day/night cycle, animated dice/wheels/water/fire. Stays within the single-file offline constraint. File grows from ~500KB to ~1.2-1.5MB. The aim is the visual register of Reigns / 80 Days / Florence — published indie game polish, not AAA realism.
scope_summary: 5 region backdrops (Plains, Foothills, Mountains, High Desert, Forest) with 3-layer parallax, gradient atmospheres, drifting clouds, time-of-day variation
23 detailed character SVGs with profession-specific accessories, clothing folds, face features that vary by health state
Animated dice with 3D-feeling settling rotation, pip patterns
Wagon wheel rotation that actually rolls forward (not just spins in place)
Particle systems: rain, snow, dust devils, campfire embers, river flow
Day/night ambient cycle: sky color and lighting shift with the calendar

region_backdrop_specification.implementation: Each region backdrop is an SVG with 3 layered groups: .bg-far, .bg-mid, .bg-near. The CSS animates them at different rates when the wagon advances (slower for far, faster for near = parallax). Atmospheric overlay sits on top with a linear gradient that shifts with day count.

regions.far_layer: Distant horizon line with two soft hill silhouettes, almost flat. Gradient from #C8B59A to #A89070
regions.mid_layer: Rolling prairie hills, more defined, with scattered sage clumps
regions.near_layer: Foreground tall grass blades (10-15 of them, slightly different heights), wagon ruts in the dirt
ambient_elements: 1-2 buffalo silhouettes grazing at random positions on the horizon, animated to slowly drift
3-5 birds in a loose V formation crossing the sky every ~30 seconds
Cloud layer: 4-6 soft clouds drifting at 0.3 of wagon advance speed
Wind effect: tall grass sways in a sine-wave loop

regions.far_layer: Soft brown hills, three layers, increasing height
regions.mid_layer: Closer hills with sparse trees (5-7 conifers in silhouette)
regions.near_layer: Rocky outcrops, sage brush, small pebbles
ambient_elements: Hawk circling on thermals, wide slow loops
Occasional deer silhouette appearing on a hilltop, lingering for 8 seconds, then exiting

regions.far_layer: Snow-capped peaks, jagged silhouette, multiple parallax layers giving depth
regions.mid_layer: Closer mountainsides with rocky bands and treeline
ambient_elements: Snowflakes when day > 130 (winter trigger): 30-50 particles falling at varying speeds and angles
Occasional falling rock animation (small rock tumbles down a slope)

regions.far_layer: Mesa silhouettes, flat-topped buttes, distant haze
regions.mid_layer: Sage flats, occasional Joshua tree silhouette, dry creek beds
regions.near_layer: Cracked earth texture, small rocks, sun-bleached bones (faint)
ambient_elements: Heat shimmer at the horizon (subtle wavy distortion)
Dust devil that occasionally appears, spirals across the screen, dissipates (every ~45 seconds)
Vulture circling slowly at high altitude
Tumbleweed rolling across foreground every ~20 seconds

regions.far_layer: Distant green ridges, layered, hint of mountains beyond
regions.mid_layer: Dense conifer forest in silhouette, varying tree heights
regions.near_layer: Detailed foreground pines with visible needles, undergrowth, mushrooms, fallen logs
ambient_elements: Light filtering through tree canopy (god rays effect via SVG gradient)
Distant water stream visible through trees with salmon occasionally jumping

character_detail_specification.implementation: Each profession's full-body SVG is rebuilt with these elements (in order from back to front layering): boots, pants, torso, arms, hands, neck, head, hair, hat, profession accessory. Each layer has gradient fills (not flat colors) for shading. Face features include: eyes (with eye color and lash detail), nose (small triangular shadow), mouth (simple curve, varies by state), eyebrows (small lines, expressive). Hair has 3-4 strand groups for variation.
character_detail_specification.shading_pattern: Use linearGradient with 2-3 stops per major shape. Light source from upper-left (so right side of body is shaded darker). 15-20% opacity black overlays for cast shadows.

healthy.face: neutral mouth curve, eyes open, normal eyebrows

sick.face: closed eyes (blinking), small frown, paler skin tone (desaturate)
sick.addition: small sweat drop on forehead, occasional cough animation

dying.addition: deep desaturation overlay, slight sway animation

state_variation.lost: Full grayscale + cross icon overlay (existing v2.3)

profession_accessories_detailed.Doctor: White medical cap with red cross detail. Stethoscope draped around neck (curved tubing visible). Small black bag at side. White coat over blue shirt — gradient cream to white.
profession_accessories_detailed.Hunter: Brown wide-brim leather hat with crease detail. Leather bandolier across chest. Quiver of feathered arrows over shoulder. Compound bow held in one hand. Hunting knife at belt.
profession_accessories_detailed.Carpenter: Brown work cap. Hammer on belt loop. Tool belt with visible chisels and saws. Rolled-up sleeves on blue work shirt. Leather apron with pockets.
profession_accessories_detailed.Cook: Tall white chef hat with creases. Apron over dark gray clothing, possibly with flour stains. Wooden spoon in one hand, small cooking pot at feet (when standing).
profession_accessories_detailed.Scout: Coonskin cap with tail visible. Buckskin jacket with fringe. Spyglass in one hand. Leather pouch at hip.
profession_accessories_detailed.Cowboy: Tan cowboy hat with rolled brim. Bandana around neck. Vest over shirt. Boot spurs visible. Six-shooter holster at hip.
profession_accessories_detailed.Priest: Black robe with white collar visible. Cross necklace. Bible held in one hand. Short, neat hair.
profession_accessories_detailed.Blacksmith: Leather apron over heavy work clothes. Hammer in one hand. Soot smudges on face and arms (subtle dark patches).
profession_accessories_detailed.Trapper: Fur hat (probably beaver). Heavy fur-trim coat. Snare ropes coiled at side. Knife at belt.
profession_accessories_detailed.Merchant: Black bowler hat. Vest with watch chain. Coin pouch at side. Slightly fancier clothing than the workers.
profession_accessories_detailed.Guide: Wide-brim hat with feather. Long leather coat. Compass in hand. Sturdy boots.
profession_accessories_detailed.Farmer: Straw hat. Faded blue overalls over work shirt. Wheat sheaf in hand or hoe at side. Sunburned skin tone.
profession_accessories_detailed.Seamstress: Hair in a bun under bonnet. Long pink dress with apron. Sewing needle and thread visible. Small basket of fabric.
profession_accessories_detailed.Musician: Small jaunty cap. Maroon vest over white shirt. Fiddle held in one hand, bow in the other.
profession_accessories_detailed.Artist: Beret. Cream smock over regular clothes (with paint smudges). Paintbrush in hand, palette in other hand.
profession_accessories_detailed.Language Teacher: Wire-frame spectacles. Dark green coat. Open book in one hand.
profession_accessories_detailed.Politician: Tall top hat. Black formal suit with white shirt and bow tie. Speech scroll in hand. Walking cane.
profession_accessories_detailed.Banker: Top hat. Gray formal suit. Small money bag in hand. Pocket watch chain visible.
profession_accessories_detailed.Actor: Dramatic flowing scarf. Red velvet jacket. Theater mask held in one hand. Posed slightly theatrically.
profession_accessories_detailed.Fashion Designer: Stylish wide-brim hat with feathers. Bright pink dress with elaborate trim. Measuring tape around neck. Pin cushion at wrist.
profession_accessories_detailed.Magician: Black top hat with star detail. Long black cape. Wand in hand, possibly with small spark effect. Pencil mustache.
profession_accessories_detailed.Influencer: Stylish hat with bandana band. Bright colorful bandana shirt. Holds something the period kids would understand as 'their voice' — maybe a small handheld megaphone or hat-pass collection cup.
profession_accessories_detailed.Food Critic: Bowler hat. Dark suit. Fork in one hand, spoon in the other (crossed). Notebook tucked under arm.

dice_settling.behavior: Two dice tumble through 3D-rotating animation (rotateX + rotateY at varying speeds), settling into final faces. CSS transform-style: preserve-3d allows actual cube-like rotation. Each die has 6 face SVGs positioned in 3D space.
dice_settling.audio: Wood-on-table cascade sample (existing 'dice' SFX), settling chime on stop

wagon_wheel_rotation.current_bug: Wheels spin in place but wagon translates as one unit, so wheels don't ROLL forward — they spin while the wagon slides over them
wagon_wheel_rotation.fix: Synchronize wheel rotation with wagon translation. For every X pixels of horizontal movement, rotate wheels by (X / circumference) * 360 degrees. Implement via JavaScript animationframe sync, or via CSS keyframes that compose translate and rotate together.

rain_particle_system.trigger: Storm event, also rain weather variant of any region
rain_particle_system.implementation: 30-50 small SVG line elements, each with its own CSS keyframe falling diagonally. Stagger animation-delay so they don't all start at the same time. Re-spawn at top when reaching bottom.
rain_particle_system.perf_note: Use transform-only animations (translateY) — no width/height or top/left changes — so the browser can compose on GPU

snow_particle_system.trigger: day > 130 in mountains region, also winter ending
snow_particle_system.implementation: 40-60 small white circles, falling slower than rain, with gentle horizontal drift via sine wave. Some are larger and slower (foreground), others smaller and faster (background) for depth

dust_devil.implementation: Animated SVG group: 5-7 small particle elements rotating around a central point that translates across the screen. Lifetime ~3 seconds, then dissipates.

campfire_embers.implementation: 8-12 small orange circles drifting upward from the fire, fading out. Each with a slightly different starting position and velocity. Looped.

river_flow.implementation: Multiple horizontal wave-shaped paths animated via stroke-dashoffset to create the illusion of moving water. Use 2-3 layers of waves at different speeds and opacities for depth.

day_night_cycle.philosophy: Real journeys feel different at dawn than at noon. Vary the atmospheric tint based on calendar day to subtly mark passage of time.
day_night_cycle.implementation: Compute time-of-day from (daysTraveled % 4): 0=morning, 1=midday, 2=afternoon, 3=dusk. Apply corresponding atmospheric gradient via CSS variable update. Transition smoothly over 0.8s when changing.
subtle_variations: Midday: bright blues and high contrast
Afternoon: golden warm light, longer shadows
Dusk: oranges, reds, deep purples in distant horizons

responsive_scaling_v2_7.philosophy: Playtest screenshots from Nicholas show the game looks broken at 25% zoom (everything tiny), cramped at 100% zoom on a high-DPI laptop, and likely won't look right at all on the 4K projector the teacher will use for class. The root cause is the layout has fixed pixel sizes for UI chrome and oversized scenery elements (fort silhouette dominates the scene). Fix: responsive design from 1024x768 (Chromebook) to 3840x2160 (projector).

layout_density_variable.name: --ui-density
layout_density_variable.computed_at_runtime: On window load and on resize, JavaScript reads window.innerWidth and sets --ui-density to one of: 'compact' (< 1280px wide), 'standard' (1280-1919px), 'large' (1920-2559px), 'jumbo' (>= 2560px).
layout_density_variable.css_consumes_via: Each density tier has its own size scaling for resource ribbon items, team strip portraits, action button heights, and font sizes. Standard css clamp() functions then handle within-tier scaling smoothly.
layout_density_variable.rationale: A 4K TV at 3840 wide is not just 'a bigger laptop' — kids viewing from 6 feet away need bigger fonts and chunkier buttons. A Chromebook at 1366 wide has no room for big chrome. A small handful of explicit tiers handles this better than continuous scaling alone.

fort_sizing_fix.current_problem: Fort silhouette in resolveFort uses translate(160 240) with base width 180, height 100, plus crenellations and flag adding 30 viewBox units up. Total fort visual occupies ~30% of viewBox vertical space, which becomes ~40-50% of canvas vertical when sliced. Visually dominates.
fort_sizing_fix.fix: Reduce the fort visual to occupy ~15-18% of viewBox vertical space. Specific changes:

new_dimensions.crenellations: polygon points scaled by 130/180 ratio, clamped to y=20-30

fort_sizing_fix.result: Fort base now 130x70 viewBox units = 16% width, 14% height. Sky and surroundings remain visible above and around it. Wagon and characters in scene have proportional space.

scene_canvas_viewbox_change.rationale: Most modern displays are 16:9 native. Matching the SVG viewBox aspect ratio to the canvas aspect ratio means slice mode performs minimal cropping — all authored content stays visible at typical resolutions. On 4:3 Chromebook (1024x768), some side-cropping happens but content remains coherent.
scene_canvas_viewbox_change.migration_note: All existing draws at coordinates within 0-800 x 0-500 must be REMAPPED to the new viewBox. Quick approach: wrap existing scenery group in '<g transform="scale(2 1.8) translate(0 0)">' which expands old 800x500 content to fit new 1600x900. Then recalibrate proportions of oversized elements like the fort.
scene_canvas_viewbox_change.alternative: Keep existing 800x500 scenery and add letterbox via 'preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid meet"' so empty bands appear as part of the regional color. Empty bands fill with same gradient as the sky/ground.

preserve_aspect_ratio_change.current: All scenery uses 'xMidYMax slice' — fills canvas, may crop top of viewBox
preserve_aspect_ratio_change.recommendation: Switch to 'xMidYMid meet' for the SCENE-CHARACTERS layer (so fort/wagon/characters are never cropped at the edges). KEEP slice for the SCENE-BACKDROP layer so the sky/ground always fills the canvas (no black bars).
preserve_aspect_ratio_change.result: Backdrop always fills canvas (slice). Characters/wagon/fort always fully visible (meet). When the canvas aspect ratio differs from viewBox aspect ratio, the characters layer shows letterboxed bands which are TRANSPARENT — backdrop shows through underneath. No crops, no voids.

typography_clamp.philosophy: All UI text uses clamp(min, vw-relative, max) so it scales smoothly with viewport width within a density tier.

jumbo.audio_note: If played on a TV, sound effects should also be louder. Consider boosting all audioController gains by 1.3x at jumbo density. Music can stay at current level.

responsive_scaling_v2_7.deployment_note: When this v2.7 is built, IF Nicholas has not yet committed the v2.6 game-code changes, applying v2.7 should be done on top of the v2.6 working tree (NOT on the v2.5 base). Verify before starting: read meta.version from oregon_trail_game_data.json and confirm it says '2.7'; check pink_oregon_trail.html for v2.6 markers (parallax CSS variable --wagon-progress, region backdrop classes .bg-far .bg-mid .bg-near) before applying v2.7 changes. If v2.6 is missing, apply v2.6 first per its amendment, then v2.7.

callout_repositioning_v2_8.philosophy: v2.7 fixed UI chrome scaling but missed that the narrative callout overlay is positioned dead-center, occupying the exact horizontal range where the wagon is drawn (x=540 in 800-wide viewBox = ~67% from left). At any zoom level on any display, the callout text overlaps and visually erases the wagon. Fix: callout becomes a left-anchored panel; right portion of scene stays visible.

current_positioning.css: position: absolute; left: 50%; bottom: 16px; transform: translateX(-50%); max-width: var(--callout-max-w, min(640px, 70vw))
current_positioning.effective_horizontal_range_at_standard_d: From ~22% to ~78% of canvas width (centered ~640px wide on a typical canvas)
current_positioning.wagon_position_at_x540_in_viewBox: ~67.5% of canvas width — solidly inside the callout's range
current_positioning.result: Wagon is visually covered by the callout in every fort/river/event scene

new_positioning.css: position: absolute; left: 16px; bottom: 16px; transform: none; max-width: var(--callout-max-w, min(580px, 42vw)); max-height: 88%
new_positioning.effective_horizontal_range_at_standard_d: From 16px (left edge) to ~42% of canvas width
new_positioning.wagon_remains_visible_at: x=540 in viewBox = ~67% canvas width = right of the callout
new_positioning.right_zone_for_scenery: Roughly 45-95% of canvas width is now visible scenery + wagon area

compact_tier_special_case.philosophy: On phones (< 740px wide), there's not enough horizontal room to split the canvas. Fall back to bottom-anchored, full-width callout — better to obscure the wagon than to truncate the text.
compact_tier_special_case.media_query: @media (max-width: 740px) — restore callout to centered/full-width, OR keep left-anchored but allow up to 86vw width

wagon_position_tweak.philosophy: If the callout panel takes left ~42% of canvas, wagon at viewBox x=540 is comfortably to its right. But to add a little breathing room and avoid edge cases, push the default wagonX from 540 to 580 — moves wagon further right, ensuring it's never close to the callout's right edge.
wagon_position_tweak.explicit_wagonX_call_sites: All call sites currently passing wagonX: 540 should become wagonX: 580. Call sites passing wagonX: 360 (smaller scenes like buffalo encounter) can stay — those are already left-of-callout-zone but the callout will sit beneath them naturally.
wagon_position_tweak.wagon_x_380_case: renderSceneCharacters({wagonX: 380}) at line ~11224 — this is the bandit scene. With callout on left taking ~42%, x=380 ≈ 47% from left would be at the right edge of the callout. Push to x=560 for bandit scene too.

edge_cases: If a callout is shown for a 'left-side' event (e.g., Pawnee family encounter on the LEFT of canvas), the callout would overlap the figure. Mitigation: such scenes already use heroes array with x positions 80, 200, 320 — yes they overlap. Acceptable trade-off — text covers the supporting characters but the wagon (the primary visual anchor) stays visible.
On compact (mobile) viewports, the left-anchored callout becomes wide enough to block the whole scene anyway. The compact tier override falls back to centered-bottom positioning for mobile.

When the callout is closed (action menu visible), the scene fills 100%. The wagonX=580 default might look slightly off-center to the right. Acceptable — the wagon was already off-center (at x=540 = 67%) before this change.

v2_9_visual_overhaul.philosophy: Multiple visual issues stacked: scenery is too dark across all times of day, the team is invisible during travel, the wagon is undersized, the bottom UI takes 3 rows when it could take 1, the now-playing badge truncates names, and the trail's geographic shape is generic. Fix all together so playtest produces a coherently-improved game, not a collection of partial fixes.

stage_1_scenery_brightness.issue: v2.6 added day/night cycle but the gradients are uniformly dark. The Oregon City finish scene (ironically a triumphant moment) renders with a near-black sky.
stage_1_scenery_brightness.fix_atmosphere_gradients: Rewrite all 5 regions x 4 times-of-day = 20 gradients. Lower bound for sky brightness (top stop): hex value lightness >= 70% during morning/midday, >= 50% during afternoon, >= 30% during dusk. NEVER pure black or near-black for any time except a true winter scene at 165+ days.
specific_brightening_targets: Plains midday: top stop #B6D4E8 (sky blue), bottom #FBF6F0 — was acceptable, leave
Plains morning: brighten top stop from current #FFE4C4 to #FDD89E (warmer dawn)
Plains afternoon: top stop #F4D2A8, bottom #FBF6F0 (golden hour) — NEW
Plains dusk: top stop #E8957B, bottom #C8A088 (still red but readable)
Foothills: similar treatment with mauve/dusty rose tints
Mountains: cooler tones but still bright at midday — top stop #B8C8D8 minimum
Mountains winter (day > 130): pale cool gray top #E0E8F0, white bottom — NOT dark
High desert midday: HOT bright top #F4D2A8 with a sun-glare radial gradient overlay (faint white dot upper-right that fades out) — sun should feel oppressive
Forest: greener and lighter than current — top #C8E0C0 morning, #98C088 midday
Oregon City finish scene specifically: override to FORCE midday-style bright atmosphere regardless of daysTraveled. This is the win moment; it should feel like dawn.
stage_1_scenery_brightness.verification: Playtest screenshots at noon and dusk in each region. The dusk scenes should be moody (sunset reds and purples) but never so dark that figures and foreground objects are silhouetted into invisibility.

stage_2_weather_and_motion.philosophy: Each region needs visible motion that's specific to its place. Plains have grass swaying and birds. Desert has tumbleweeds and heat shimmer. Mountains have drifting snow. This wasn't fully landed in v2.6.

elements: Tall grass blades sway in sine-wave loop (already specced, verify it's running)
1-2 buffalo silhouettes drift slowly across the horizon over 30s, restart at offscreen-left
3-5 birds in V formation cross the sky every ~25 seconds (existing, verify)
Cloud layer drifts at 0.3x wagon speed (existing, verify)
**Tumbleweeds**: 1-2 at any time, rolling across foreground (left-to-right or right-to-left random). 8-12 second crossing time. Re-spawn 5-15 seconds after exit.
**Sun glare overlay** at midday: faint white radial gradient in upper-right corner, fades out around the edges. Subtle but oppressive.
Heat shimmer at horizon (existing SVG filter, verify)
Dust devil 16% per turn (existing, verify)
Vulture circling slowly (existing, verify)
**Fog drift** during morning (time-of-day = 0): 2-3 horizontal soft white bands drift slowly across mid-layer, fade in/out
Hawk circling on thermals (existing)
Trees sway in wind (existing)
Occasional deer silhouette appearing on hilltop (existing)
Snowflakes (existing, day > 130, verify)
Eagle soaring (existing, verify)
**Falling rocks**: small rock tumbles down a distant slope every ~45s, brief animation
Cloud wisps cling to peaks (existing)
**Falling leaves** during dusk (time-of-day = 3): orange/yellow leaves drift down from tree canopy, slow rotation, similar to snow but warmer color and slower
God-rays through canopy (existing)
Salmon jumping (existing)
Bird silhouettes between trees (existing)

stage_2_weather_and_motion.performance_note: All ambient motion uses CSS keyframe animations on transform-only properties (translate, rotate, scale) so the browser GPU-composes them. No layout-triggering animations. Target: stays at 60fps with all weather active.

stage_3_wagon_size_up.scope: All renderSceneCharacters() call sites. Where wagonScale is currently 0.85, change to 1.10. Where it's 0.7 (small/distant scenes), change to 0.85. Where it's 1.0, keep.
stage_3_wagon_size_up.rationale: Wagon should be visually substantial — it's the player's anchor. Currently it reads as small relative to the canvas.

stage_4_callout_typography_smaller.new_clamps: Title clamp(17px, 1.7vw, 30px); body clamp(13px, 1vw, 19px); choice button label clamp(13px, 1vw, 18px)
stage_4_callout_typography_smaller.rationale: Callout-on-left occupies 42vw at standard density. Slightly smaller text fits more callout in the same horizontal space and gives more visual breathing room for the right-side scene.

stage_5_single_row_bottom_bar.philosophy: Currently the bottom of the screen has 3 horizontal bands stacked (action buttons, team strip, mini-map). At standard+ density, collapse to ONE row: team strip on left, mini-map middle, action buttons right.

layout_per_density.compact: KEEP stacked layout (3 rows). Not enough horizontal room for single-row.
layout_per_density.standard: Single row. Team strip ~38%, mini-map ~22%, action buttons ~36%, gaps ~4%. Total height: 100px (was ~280px stacked).
layout_per_density.large: Single row, more generous spacing. Total height: 110px.
layout_per_density.jumbo: Single row, large elements. Total height: 130px.

stage_5_single_row_bottom_bar.css_pattern: Use CSS Grid with grid-template-columns: auto 1fr auto and align-items: center. Each child gets its natural width; mini-map flexes to fill the middle.
stage_5_single_row_bottom_bar.minimap_behavior: Stays click-to-expand. In single-row layout, default size is smaller (~280x80px). Click expands to fullscreen overlay (existing v2.7 behavior preserved).
stage_5_single_row_bottom_bar.action_button_behavior: When choices fire (event/intervention), choice options STILL appear in the callout panel (per Nicholas's confirmation in item 1). Action bar stays as 4-5 buttons (Push On / Hunt / Rest / Forage / optional Make for Fort). When dice are rolling, action bar is briefly disabled with a 'Rolling...' overlay.
stage_5_single_row_bottom_bar.vertical_space_freed: Going from ~280px stacked to ~100px single row at standard density frees ~180px for scene canvas. Significant scenery breathing room.

stage_6_team_walking_alongside.design_philosophy: During Push On (the dice-roll-and-travel turn), the team is visible alongside the wagon, animated with a walk cycle. State of each member determines their position and animation. Team becomes part of the moving scene, not just abstract icons in a strip.

lead_position.members: Up to 2. Scout if present, otherwise the healthiest available adult.
lead_position.behavior: Walking briskly, looking ahead, pointing occasionally

flank_position.behavior: Walking alongside the wagon, sometimes turning toward each other (chatting)

rear_position.members: Up to 1 (often the slowest or oldest)
rear_position.behavior: Walking with slight lag, sometimes catching up

wagon_interior_silhouettes.behavior: Children (if any team member is a child — TBD slot) and sick/injured/dying members render as dimmer silhouettes visible through the canvas top of the wagon. Use a slightly transparent shape inside the wagon SVG.
wagon_interior_silhouettes.implementation_hint: Add a <g class='wagon-passengers'> inside the wagon SVG that holds 0-3 simplified silhouette figures. Show/hide based on which members are riding.

ground_grave_markers.behavior: For each lost team member, a small grave marker (cross or stone) renders on the ground BEHIND the wagon (lower x range, ~50-150 viewBox), with the member's name on it. Persistent across turns. Grave markers do NOT move with parallax — they're attached to the trail behind, which the wagon has passed.
ground_grave_markers.rationale: Visual memory of the team's losses without being morbid. Kids will point at them. Same emotional weight as the persistent localStorage graves spec'd in v2.0.

state_to_position_assignment.logic_summary: When composing the Push On scene, walk the team[] array and assign each living member to a position based on state and profession.
rules_in_priority_order: 1. Lost members: render grave marker on ground at trail-rear; do not assign to walking position
2. Dying members: ride in wagon (silhouette in wagon-passengers group)
a. Up to 2 to lead position. Prefer Scout, then healthiest by profession-priority
b. Up to 2 to flank position
c. Up to 1 to rear position. Prefer weakened over healthy (slowest brings up the rear)
d. Any remaining: ride in wagon (silhouette) — represents children/extra hands
state_to_position_assignment.edge_case_all_riding: If all 7 members are sick/injured/dying, only the wagon shows with all silhouettes inside. The ox still pulls. The wagon master is implied (not shown). Bleak but legible.
state_to_position_assignment.edge_case_team_smaller_than_7: If members are lost, the team has fewer walking figures. Lead/flank/rear positions render with whoever's available. Empty positions just don't render.

walk_cycle_animation.frames: Use 2-state CSS animation: stride-left and stride-right alternating. 0.7s per cycle.
walk_cycle_animation.css_pattern: @keyframes walk { 0% { transform: translateY(0); } 50% { transform: translateY(-2px); } 100% { transform: translateY(0); } } — adds a slight bounce to each figure
walk_cycle_animation.leg_animation: Inside the character SVG, the two leg <rect> elements get separate animations: left-leg shifts forward 4px viewBox units while right-leg shifts back, then swap. Roughly 0.7s cycle.
walk_cycle_animation.head_bob: Slight head transform: translateY(-1px to 1px) at ~1Hz
state_modifiers: Healthy: full bounce (-2px), full leg swing (4px)
Weakened: half bounce (-1px), 80% leg swing, slight forward lean
Lead-position-Scout: faster cycle (0.6s), slight forward lean (eager)

horizontal_motion_during_dice_roll.behavior: While the dice are tumbling and settling (~1.2s), all walking team members + wagon translate rightward at a rate proportional to the rolled movement. After settle, freeze in final positions.
horizontal_motion_during_dice_roll.wagon_wheel_sync_note: v2.6 noted wagon-wheel-translation sync as deferred to v2.7 (then v2.8, still pending). Implement it now alongside the team walking system since both use the same translate-with-time approach. JavaScript drives the position; CSS-attached wheels rotate proportionally to translateX delta.

between_turns.behavior: When NOT in a Push On animation (callout open, action menu showing, etc.), team figures stay at their final walking positions but the walk-cycle animation pauses. They're frozen in place. Wagon also static.
between_turns.rationale: Performance — 7 figures animating at all times is wasteful. They only need to walk during the dice roll's ~1.5 seconds.

stage_7_historical_map_shape.philosophy: The on-screen mini-map currently renders a generic curve. Replace with a stylized historical map of the Oregon Trail's actual route across the western territories.

map_design.background: Parchment color (#F4E8D8 base) with subtle aged texture (SVG filter feTurbulence at very low opacity for paper grain)
map_design.edges: Slightly browned/curled edges via SVG filter or radial gradient overlay
map_design.state_outlines: Faint sepia outlines of: Missouri (eastern start), Kansas Territory, Nebraska Territory, Wyoming Territory, Idaho Territory, Oregon Territory. State names labeled in small italic serif. Border lines are 0.5px thin, sepia (#A89070).
rivers: Missouri River (eastern edge, faint blue-gray line)
Platte River (across Nebraska, parallel to trail)
Columbia River (Oregon, near The Dalles and final stretch)
map_design.trail_line: Thick rose-pink line (#B85770) following the historical route. ~3px stroke. Slight texture via stroke-dasharray for hand-drawn feel.
trail_shape_landmarks: Independence MO at coordinates (15, 45) of viewBox
Slight southern dip through Nebraska/Wyoming border (~around x=40-50)
South Pass is the inflection point — trail bends northwest from there
Through Idaho along Snake River oxbow
Columbia River gorge near final approach
Oregon City at coordinates (12, 38) of viewBox (Willamette Valley, slightly south of Columbia)
stop_markers: Forts: filled brown circles, 5px diameter, with small text label (e.g., 'Fort Laramie')
Rivers: small wave icons at crossing points
Current wagon position: tiny wagon SVG (10px wide), positioned by linear-interpolating between trail keypoints based on w.position / trail.length
map_design.all_wagons_view: In Competitive mode, render each wagon as a small colored circle with player initial. Currently active wagon's marker pulses gently.

stage_7_historical_map_shape.viewBox: viewBox='0 0 100 60' (5:3 aspect, fits the bottom-row mini-map slot)
stage_7_historical_map_shape.expand_to_fullscreen: Click the mini-map to overlay a 90vw x 80vh version of the same map at higher detail. Click anywhere outside to dismiss. Existing v2.7 expand mechanism preserved.
stage_7_historical_map_shape.interaction_with_physical_board: The on-screen map's geographic shape should INFORM v3.1's physical board redesign. Same trail route, same landmarks at same relative positions. v3.1 will redo the printable tiles to match this digital map.

stage_8_photo_override_population.philosophy: Nicholas provided a zip with 29 historical photos. 11 of them map cleanly to landmarks in the trail. Pre-populate the override block during this build so photos appear immediately on first launch.
stage_8_photo_override_population.photo_block_source: A separate file 'historical_photos_override_block.html' has been generated containing the full <script id='historical-photos-override'> block with all 11 photos base64-encoded inside. Total size ~620KB.
implementation: Read historical_photos_override_block.html from the project folder
Find the closing </body> tag in pink_oregon_trail.html
If a <script id='historical-photos-override'> block already exists in the HTML, replace it with the new block (same logic as fetch_historical_photos.py)
Otherwise insert the new block immediately before </body>
Save and verify the file size increased by ~620KB
stage_8_photo_override_population.expected_HTML_size_after: v2.8 was ~600KB, v2.9 with photos embedded will be ~1.2-1.3MB. Still well within reasonable limits, still single-file offline.
stage_8_photo_override_population.consumer_code_changes_needed: The existing landmarkIllustrationFor() function in v2.5+ already checks window.HISTORICAL_PHOTOS_OVERRIDE first. If present, render ; otherwise render daguerreotype SVG. Verify this function handles the new bonus landmark IDs (fort_kearny, fort_hall, fort_boise, ash_hollow, courthouse_rock, scotts_bluff). If those IDs aren't currently mapped to a landmark name, add the mapping in resolveLandmark / resolveFort.

stage_9_now_playing_badge_fix.issue: Screenshot shows 'g' in the now-playing badge instead of full player name 'Gabby'.
stage_9_now_playing_badge_fix.root_cause_hypothesis: Either the badge renders only first character (e.g., name.charAt(0)), OR the player name isn't being passed correctly and the code falls back to a single-char default.
stage_9_now_playing_badge_fix.fix: Locate the now-playing badge render code (likely in renderTurnBanner or showNowPlaying). Ensure it renders the full w.playerName, not a truncated version.
stage_9_now_playing_badge_fix.acceptance: Win screen, leaderboard, all in-game badges show 'Gabby' (or whatever name was entered), not 'g'.

v2_9_dependencies_on_earlier_versions: Requires v2.8 callout-on-left (for the new wider scene canvas to be useful)
Requires v2.7 density tier system (for stage 5 single-row bottom bar)
Requires v2.6 region backdrops (for stage 1 brightness fix and stage 2 weather)
Build report should verify all earlier mechanics still work after this overhaul
scope_explicitly_excluded: Audio system overhaul — deferred to v3.0
Physical board PDF regeneration — deferred to v3.1
Pioneer Voices Did You Know cards — deferred to v3.0 (paired with audio)
The static-noise-every-30-seconds bug — deferred to v3.0 audio overhaul
Real instrument samples for music — deferred to v3.0

v3_0_mobile_and_visibility.philosophy: The game was designed primarily for desktop classroom use but Gabby plays at home on multiple devices including phones. Mobile screens currently can't display the scene, callouts, action menu, team strip, AND mini-map all at once — something is always cut off. Plus, in multi-wagon Competitive games, wagon markers stack into a blob that hides individual positions. Fix both with one focused amendment.

scope.mobile_ui_overhaul: Drawer-based layout (Approach C) for viewports < 740px wide AND for any narrow-portrait orientation
scope.pin_needles: Smart-stacking pin needle wagon markers — affects desktop mini-map AND mobile drawer-map equally

stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.philosophy: On mobile, the scene canvas takes the top ~60% of viewport. Below it, a fixed-position interaction panel takes ~40% — shows the active callout when an event is firing, otherwise shows the action buttons. Team strip and mini-map collapse into bottom-edge drawer tabs that slide up over the scene when tapped.
stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.viewport_detection: Add to existing density updater: if window.innerWidth < 740 OR (window.innerWidth < 1024 AND innerHeight > innerWidth), set density to 'mobile'. This treats narrow-portrait tablets as mobile too.

layout_when_mobile.scene_canvas_zone: top 60% of viewport. Region backdrop, characters, wagon, parallax — all rendered here, stretched to fill available space
layout_when_mobile.interaction_panel: fixed bottom 40%. Shows narrative callout when one is active; falls back to action buttons (Push On / Hunt / Rest / Forage) when not
layout_when_mobile.resource_ribbon: compressed to 32px tall, scrollable horizontally if needed, sticks to top
layout_when_mobile.drawer_tabs: Two slim tabs visible along the bottom edge: 'Team' (left) and 'Map' (right). Both ~80px wide × 28px tall, tap to slide up the corresponding drawer over the scene
layout_when_mobile.drawer_team: slides up from bottom, takes ~70% of viewport height, shows full team strip with state info inline (no hover needed — tap a portrait for tooltip; tap outside drawer to dismiss)
layout_when_mobile.drawer_map: slides up similarly, shows the mini-map at near-fullscreen size with all wagons visible via pin needles

stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.css_pattern: Use position: fixed for the bottom interaction panel and drawer tabs. Drawers use transform: translateY(100%) -> translateY(0) with 0.3s cubic-bezier transition for slide animation. Drawers have backdrop-filter: blur(2px) so scene shows through subtly. Tap-outside (overlay div behind drawer) dismisses.
stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.scene_canvas_aspect_lock: On mobile in portrait: encourage landscape via a one-time gentle prompt 'Rotate your phone for the best experience'. Don't HARD lock — kids might be sitting on a couch holding the phone in portrait. Just suggest. Don't show the prompt twice in the same session (use sessionStorage flag).
stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.audio_gate: First user gesture (tap on Begin Journey button or any setup screen interaction) triggers audioController.init() if not already initialized. Mobile browsers (especially iOS Safari) require this. Add a 'tap to enable sound' hint on first mobile load if no gesture detected after 3 seconds.
stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.save_state_notice: On first mobile launch, show a one-time toast: 'Your progress saves on this device. Different devices keep separate journeys.' Auto-dismiss after 6 seconds. Use localStorage flag to never show again.
stage_1_mobile_drawer_layout.tap_vs_hover: Replace hover-tooltip behavior on team portraits with tap-to-show. On desktop, hover still works. On mobile, tap a portrait opens a small popover with the same info; tap elsewhere dismisses. Detect via @media (hover: none) — no DOM-time detection needed.

stage_2_pin_needle_wagons.philosophy: Current wagon markers on the mini-map are 10px colored circles. When 4-6 wagons are at or near the same trail position, they overlap into a single colored blob. Replace with pin-needle markers that stack vertically when wagons share a space.

marker_design.needle: Thin vertical line (2px wide) extending up from the trail line. Color matches the wagon's assigned color (rose, gold, navy, forest, plum, copper).
marker_design.head: Circle at the top of the needle, 8-10px diameter, same color as needle, with player's first initial in white inside (centered).
marker_design.needle_length_per_wagon: Each wagon at the same space gets a different needle length so heads don't overlap. Order: leftmost wagon (lowest player index) gets shortest needle (16px), next gets 24px, then 32px, 40px, 48px, 56px. Up to 6 wagons stacked at one space without head overlap.
marker_design.needle_origin_offset: Each needle's base attaches at slightly different x within the space (5-pixel range), so even at the same space they're not coplanar — easier to read which is whose.

smart_stacking_logic.step_1: Group all wagons by their current trail position
smart_stacking_logic.step_2: For each group sharing a space, sort by player index (or wagon ID)
smart_stacking_logic.step_3: Assign each wagon in the group a 'stack rank' from 0 to N-1
smart_stacking_logic.step_5: Render needle x-offset = (stackRank - (groupSize-1)/2) * 5 px from space center
smart_stacking_logic.result: Six wagons stacked at one space appear as a fan of six pin needles of increasing height, slightly spread horizontally. Six wagons spread across the trail look like six individual flags. Player visually identifies their own wagon by color and initial.

stage_2_pin_needle_wagons.active_wagon_marker: Currently-active wagon's needle pulses gently (CSS keyframe scale 1.0 -> 1.15 -> 1.0 over 1.5s). Other wagons stay static.
applies_to: Desktop mini-map in the bottom-bar (single-row at standard+ density)
Mobile drawer-map (drawer slides up from bottom)
stage_2_pin_needle_wagons.performance: Six pin needles per wagon = 12 SVG elements per wagon (line + head circle + text). For 6 wagons = 72 elements. Negligible.

stage_3_setup_screen_mobile_reflow.philosophy: Setup screens currently use multi-column grids that overflow narrow viewports. Reflow to single-column, full-width on mobile.
specific_changes: Profession selection grid: 5 columns desktop -> 2 columns mobile -> scrollable list. Each card 100% width on mobile.
Name inputs: stack vertically, full-width, 50px tall touch targets
Difficulty cards (Easy/Medium/Hard): stack vertically full-width on mobile (currently side-by-side)
Wagon count selector: large tap targets, stack vertically
Begin Journey button: 60px tall on mobile, full-width-minus-margin

v3_1_physical_board_redesign.philosophy: The physical board is the keepsake. Currently 6 letter-size tiles taped together — functional but not beautiful. Redesign with historically accurate geography, period-style watercolor aesthetic, AND add a single-sheet 36x24-inch poster format for Staples-quality wall printing. Decorated and paintable versions of both. Designed so Gabby and her mom can paint either format into something extraordinary.

tile_format.files: board_short_tile_A_decorated.html through board_short_tile_F_paintable.html (6 tiles x 2 versions)
tile_format.assembly: 3 wide x 2 tall = 25.5 x 17 inches assembled board
tile_format.audience: print at home on a regular printer, glue to foam board

single_sheet_format.audience: print at Staples or similar, ready to use as a finished poster
single_sheet_format.cost_estimate: $15-30 at Staples on satin photo paper or 100lb cardstock

design_principles.historical_geography: Trail follows the actual Oregon Trail route — east-to-west across plains (Missouri/Kansas/Nebraska), slight southern dip through Wyoming, South Pass crossing, northwest through Idaho along Snake River, ending in the Willamette Valley. Trail shape is an L-shape rotated: long east-west horizontal with a northwest leg in the final third.
design_principles.aesthetic: Period-style watercolor map, like 1840s emigrant guides or Lewis & Clark expedition maps. Sepia browns, muted greens, dusty roses, parchment cream. Pink as the trail color (period-appropriate cochineal red was a real period dye).
design_principles.scenery_per_region: Each section of the trail shows region-appropriate scenery: prairie grass and buffalo on the plains, Chimney Rock and Independence Rock as drawn landmarks in Wyoming, mountain ranges around South Pass, Snake River with oxbow shape across Idaho, Cascade Mountains and Pacific Northwest forest at the finish.
key_landmarks_visible: Chimney Rock (drawn as actual recognizable shape)
design_principles.spaces_marked: 25 spaces for Short Journey or 50 for Extended, drawn as small numbered circles along the trail line. Each space has its place name in italic serif text below.
decorative_elements: Compass rose in upper-left corner (period style, 8-point with fleur-de-lis north)
Cartouche/title block in upper-right with 'Pink Oregon Trail' title and 'Designed by Gabby Kasdaglis, 1848' attribution
Scale bar showing 'one inch = X miles' at bottom
Faint state/territory borders (Missouri, Kansas Territory, Nebraska Territory, Wyoming Territory, Idaho Territory, Oregon Territory)
River systems drawn faintly (Missouri, Platte, Sweetwater, Snake, Columbia)
Mountain ranges shaded as soft brown forms (Rockies, Wasatch, Blue Mountains, Cascades)
Decorative border edge — narrow patterned frame in sepia

stage_1_redesigned_tiles.philosophy: Replace the existing 12 tile HTML files with historically accurate, beautifully decorated versions. Each tile is one section of the trail; tiles assemble 3-wide-by-2-tall into a 25.5x17 inch board.

tile_F.region: Wyoming high country / South Pass / Idaho

stage_1_redesigned_tiles.trail_flow: A -> B -> C across top. C drops down to F (continues vertically at right edge). F -> E -> D across bottom (right to left). D contains finish at Oregon City in lower-left.

tile_A: Independence MO start: small drawn town with red-brick storefronts, smoke from chimney
Wagon train silhouettes leaving town heading west
First few river crossings (Kansas River, Big Blue River)
Compass rose in upper-left corner of this tile only (north pointer)
State label MISSOURI in faint italic
tile_B: Platte River drawn as wide blue-gray ribbon across tile
Fort Kearny as small palisade fort silhouette
Buffalo herd on the horizon (small dark shapes)
Chimney Rock drawn as actual recognizable conical spire with its distinct silhouette
Scotts Bluff as broad rocky escarpment
Ash Hollow as small valley with trees
State label NEBRASKA in faint italic
tile_C: Fort Laramie as larger fort with American flag
Independence Rock drawn as turtle-shell granite dome with names visible faintly carved
Devil's Gate drawn as narrow river canyon
Trail bending to drop downward at tile's lower-right (toward tile F)
tile_F: South Pass drawn as gentle saddle between mountain ranges (Rocky Mountains visible in background as soft brown forms)
Fort Bridger as smaller log palisade
Soda Springs drawn as small bubbling springs with steam
Fort Hall as British-style trading post
Trail entering from top (continuing from tile C) and exiting to the left (continuing to tile E)
tile_E: Snake River drawn with its actual oxbow curve shape — the dramatic bends Idaho is known for
Fort Boise as small palisade near a river bend
Three Island Crossing as a section where the trail crosses through a river
Massacre Rocks (note: respectfully drawn — small rocky outcrop, not dramatized)
Trail flowing right-to-left across this tile (continues from F, heads to D)
tile_D: Cascade Mountains rising in background (snow-capped peaks)
Whitman Mission as small white-painted clapboard building (treated solemnly)
The Dalles drawn as Columbia River narrowing through black basalt cliffs (Native fishermen on rocky platforms — respectful representation)
Columbia River widening as it flows toward the Pacific
Oregon City at finish — small Willamette Valley town beside Willamette Falls
Pacific Ocean hinted at as soft blue at the western edge

tile_construction.border: Decorative sepia border around each tile, ~24px wide, with period pattern (small repeated motif)
tile_construction.tile_label: Each tile labeled in lower-right with 'Tile A of 6' style indicator and assembly hint arrow
tile_construction.alignment_marks: Small pink registration dots at corners help kids align tiles when taping together
tile_construction.paintable_version: Same composition but with most fills removed, leaving only outlines in light sepia. Trail line stays light pink. Numbered space circles stay outlined. Region name and key landmark labels stay in pencil-stroke gray. Text spaces large enough to paint within. Light gray dot grid faintly visible to suggest 'paint within these regions'.

stage_2_single_sheet_poster.philosophy: A separate, more elaborate design optimized for the 36x24 inch landscape poster format that Staples can print. Different from the tiles — single continuous artwork without seam lines, more decorative, more space for elaborate scenery.

construction.preserve_aspect_ratio: xMidYMid meet (in case browser rendering is for preview)
construction.print_optimization: All vector — no raster images. Scales to any size cleanly. Staples will print at native 36x24 inches without quality loss.

compositional_choices.trail_layout: More elaborate than tile version. Trail is a sweeping S-curve across the landscape, with Independence in lower-left and Oregon City in upper-left (echoing the historical L-shape but more dramatic for visual impact at poster size).
compositional_choices.space_distribution: All 50 Extended-trail spaces marked. Short Journey players use only the first 25 (or play with all 50 if they want — both modes work on the same poster).
compositional_choices.scenery_zones: Each region has a dedicated zone of the poster with elaborated scenery — Missouri starts with a frontier town vignette, plains have a buffalo herd in detail, Wyoming features Independence Rock and Chimney Rock as foreground subjects, mountains have snow-capped peaks with detailed shading, desert has mesa formations, Pacific Northwest has Mount Hood and Cascade range visible.
compositional_choices.compass_rose: Large ornate compass rose, ~8 inches diameter, in lower-right corner. Eight-point with fleur-de-lis north and decorative scrollwork.
compositional_choices.title_cartouche: Upper-center: large cartouche scroll-style banner reading 'PINK OREGON TRAIL — 1848' in period serif. Below it: 'A History Game Designed by Gabby Kasdaglis'. Decorated with quill-and-inkwell vignette, small drawing of a covered wagon.
compositional_choices.scale_bar: Bottom-center: 'One inch = approximately 70 miles' with hash marks.
compositional_choices.decorative_borders: 1.5-inch decorative border around the whole poster with repeated period motifs (small wagons, oxen, fleurs, stars).
compositional_choices.corner_vignettes: Each of the four corners has a small period-style vignette: NW corner shows a settler family, NE shows oxen yoked to wagon, SW shows a Native American family in respectful representation (a Pawnee guide), SE shows a campfire scene.

paintable_version.philosophy: Designed specifically for a mother-daughter painting project. Outlines clear and clean. Large open regions for color filling. Reference colors hinted as soft watermark labels (e.g., 'sky blue here', 'prairie green here') in faint pencil.
paintable_version.what_stays_printed: Trail line in light pink. Space circles outlined and numbered. Landmark labels in italic serif. State borders in pencil stroke. Compass rose outlined (not filled). Title cartouche outlined. Border pattern outlined.
paintable_version.what_to_paint: Region scenery (sky, ground, mountains, rivers, trees, buffalo). Decorative borders. Compass rose interior. Cartouche backing. Corner vignette interiors.
paintable_version.paint_guide_overlay: Optional: light gray text labels in each major region suggesting paint colors ('sage green for plains', 'sepia for mountains', 'soft yellow for desert'). Toggle-able when generating the file.

stage_3_assembly_and_painting_guide.philosophy: A document Gabby and her mom can use to plan the painting project. Lists all the supplies needed, suggests color palette, provides painting tutorial for the trickier elements (mountains, rivers, watercolor washes).
sections: Print options (tile version OR poster version, with cost estimates)
Staples printing instructions (paper recommendations, finish recommendations)
Hobby Lobby supply list (paints, brushes, palette, sealant)
Color palette card with hex values and physical paint matches
Painting techniques (watercolor wash for sky, layered green for grass, soft graduated mountains, river highlights)
Tips for painting around the printed text (don't paint over the trail line, the space numbers, the labels)
Sealing instructions (Mod Podge gloss after fully dry to make the board last a school year)
Optional: laminating service at Staples ($5-10) for ultra-durability

v3_1_1_fixes.philosophy: Three small targeted bugs surfaced in playtest. Each is contained and validated in isolation. Bundle them as one quick amendment so Nicholas gets all three fixes in one push.

fix_1_day_night_cycle_stuck.issue: Sky stays dark across all regions and times of day. The Oregon City win scene renders with near-black sky despite v2.6 day/night cycle being specced and v2.9 brightness fix being applied.
diagnosis_steps: Step 1: Open pink_oregon_trail.html. Search for 'data-time' and 'time-of-day' attribute usage. Confirm the attribute IS being SET on document root or scene container at game start AND on every turn end.

Step 2: If not being set: ADD a setTimeOfDay(daysTraveled) function that sets document.documentElement.dataset.time to the appropriate value (morning, midday, afternoon, dusk) based on (daysTraveled % 4 === 0 ? 'morning' : daysTraveled % 4 === 1 ? 'midday' : daysTraveled % 4 === 2 ? 'afternoon' : 'dusk'). Call it from completeEndTurn AND from beginJourney AND from advanceWagon.

Step 3: Verify the CSS rules :root[data-time='midday'] .atmosphere etc. are actually defined for each region. If gradients are defined but selector is wrong (e.g., .region-backdrop[data-time] instead of :root[data-time]), fix the selector.

Step 4: Inspect the actual gradient values for 'midday' across all 5 regions. If they're still dark (lightness <70% on top stop), apply the v2.9 brightening that was specced but possibly didn't land. JSON v2_9 stage_1 has the specific brightening targets.
fix_1_day_night_cycle_stuck.additional_oregon_city_fix: When the wagon reaches the finish space (Oregon City), force the atmosphere to bright midday regardless of daysTraveled. Add this in resolveFinish or wherever the win scene composes: temporarily override document.documentElement.dataset.time = 'midday' for the win scene, restore prior value if game continues for some reason (it doesn't usually).
fix_1_day_night_cycle_stuck.verification: Walk through journey from day 1 to day 60+, hitting all four time-of-day values. Each should show a visibly different sky tint. Reach Oregon City — sky should be bright (not dark) at the win scene.

fix_2_race_to_fort_when_at_fort.issue: When a wagon is currently sitting on a fort space (e.g., just used Make for the Fort, or landed on a fort lucky-roll, or arrived via the v2.4 race_to_fort that was fixed) AND a member is in dying state, the intervention event still shows 'Race to the nearest fort' as a choice. Player picks it, the game advances them to... the SAME fort they're already at, which is nonsensical.
fix_2_race_to_fort_when_at_fort.root_cause: The availability check for race_to_fort is likely 'distanceToNextFort(w) <= 3' which returns 0 (or a small number) when the wagon is at or near a fort. Treating 0 as 'valid distance' is wrong — 0 means you're ALREADY there, not 'fort is conveniently close.'
fix_2_race_to_fort_when_at_fort.fix: In resolveInterventionChoice or wherever choice availability is evaluated:
fix_logic: Calculate distanceToNextFort(w) — but exclude the wagon's CURRENT space if it's a fort.
Specifically: only consider forts AHEAD of the wagon (position > w.position) AND not yet visited (not in w.fortsVisited).
If no such fort exists within 5 spaces, race_to_fort is unavailable.
If wagon is currently AT a fort space: race_to_fort is also unavailable (the player should use 'Stop and tend' or 'Pray and continue' instead — they're already at fort medical resources).
When race_to_fort is unavailable: do not include it in the choice list shown to the player.
fix_2_race_to_fort_when_at_fort.improvement_for_at_fort_case: When a member is dying AND the wagon is at a fort, the intervention event should add a NEW choice option: 'Use the fort doctor' — which uses the fort's existing medical resources for a +30% recovery chance bonus AND costs 0 days (you're already at the fort). This makes being-at-a-fort meaningfully different from being-at-a-trail-space when intervening.
fix_2_race_to_fort_when_at_fort.narrative_when_using_fort_doctor: The fort's doctor takes {name} into the infirmary. Hours pass. The fort's surgeon has supplies and skill our wagon does not. {name} {recovers/does not survive} by sundown.
fix_2_race_to_fort_when_at_fort.verification: Force a wagon to a fort space. Force a member to dying state. Trigger end-of-turn. Intervention event should NOT show race_to_fort. Should show stop_and_tend, pray_and_continue, continue_full_pace, AND the new 'Use the fort doctor' option (with +30% bonus and 0-day cost).

fix_3_pdf_page_fill.issue: The two paintable PDFs (board_short_paintable.pdf and board_extended_paintable.pdf) render each tile in the top-left corner of the letter-portrait page instead of filling the page. About 60-70% of each page is blank.
fix_3_pdf_page_fill.root_cause_hypothesis: The .page CSS rule has 'width: 8.5in; height: 11in' but during print rendering, wkhtmltopdf may be sizing the inner SVG to its viewBox dimensions (612x792 pixels at default 72 DPI = 8.5x11 inches) without applying the .page wrapper sizing. Or the SVG width/height attributes are explicitly set to small px values overriding the CSS.
fix_steps: Step 1: In the combined-tile HTML files, ensure each .page element has explicit width: 8.5in; height: 11in AND that width/height are NOT overridden by any media query or print rule.

Step 2: Each <svg> inside .page must have NO explicit width/height attributes (or have width='100%' height='100%'). The viewBox handles the coordinate system; the CSS handles the actual rendered size.
Step 3: Add 'width: 100%; height: 100%; display: block;' as inline style or class to every <svg> in the combined HTMLs.
Step 4: The .page rule should also have 'overflow: hidden' to prevent any tiny offset from causing a scrollbar.
Step 5: For the @page CSS, ensure 'margin: 0' is set so the printable area is the full 8.5x11.
fix_3_pdf_page_fill.rebuild_required: After fixing the HTML, regenerate both PDFs using wkhtmltopdf with the same flags as before (--page-size Letter --orientation Portrait --margin-top 0 etc.). Verify visually that each page fills with the tile artwork.
fix_3_pdf_page_fill.verification: Open the new PDFs. Each of the 6 pages should show the tile filling the entire 8.5x11 sheet — not just the top-left corner. The decorative outline border should run along all four edges of the page.

scoring_v3_3.philosophy: Replace the single-number score with four orthogonal tracks. A team finishing slow with everyone alive at full pantries should score higher than a team finishing fast with empty pantries and three lost. The slow careful team beats the fast careless team by more than 2x. That's the lesson the trail teaches.

steward.narrative: Your team arrived alive and well.
steward.narrative_low: Few of your people made it to the valley.
steward.narrative_mid: Some of your people came through. Others did not.

provider.narrative: Your wagon held its provisions to the end.
provider.narrative_low: You arrived at Oregon City with empty barrels and no coin.
provider.narrative_mid: You stretched what you had. The wagon was lean by the time you saw the valley.

scholar.narrative_low: There was much to see along the way. You did not stop to see it.
scholar.narrative_mid: You stopped at landmarks. You read what travelers had written before you.

pathfinder.narrative_low: Snow fell on the Cascades before you reached them.
pathfinder.narrative_mid: You arrived before winter. The mountains let you through.

scoring_v3_3.legacy_score: Retained for backward compatibility with seeded leaderboard. computeScore() still runs and stores w.score; computeV33Score(w) populates w.scoreV33 = { steward, provider, scholar, pathfinder, total }.

dyk_quotas.philosophy: DYK card exposure was probabilistic in v3.2. Some games gave 5 cards, some gave 18. There was no guaranteed dose for the educational objective. v3.3 enforces a per-trail minimum and a category floor so every kid who plays sees a representative slice of the historical content.
dyk_quotas.implementation: Per-wagon dyk_quota_remaining counter, initialized from trail length. Every space that can fire a DYK card checks the counter. If dyk_quota_remaining > spaces_remaining, the DYK card fires deterministically (no probability roll). Otherwise existing probability gates apply.
dyk_quotas.category_enforcement: Maintain a dyk_categories_seen Set. If fewer than 4 turns remain and an unseen required category is still owed, the next eligible DYK card forces a card from that category.
