dyk_quotas.v33_0_note: Category enforcement is wired but the historical_facts pool was not retagged with all 8 categories this round (deferred with item 5 content expansion to v3.3.x). Until retagging completes, only the 'minimum count' floor is binding; the 'category floor' is best-effort against existing tags.

residual_probabilities.philosophy: Every protective skill has a residual failure rate. Every calamity has a residual escape rate. Both honest. Both narratively framed. Kids should understand that on the real trail, good preparation made you 80% safe and bad preparation made you 20% lucky.

exhausted_protection_narratives.hunter_failed: The Hunter walked the timberline at dusk and came back empty-handed. Game was scarce; the team was tired. Tonight we eat what we have.
exhausted_protection_narratives.trapper_failed: Snares all sprung clean. The Trapper checked every line twice, but the woods were quiet.
exhausted_protection_narratives.carpenter_failed: The Carpenter laid out tools and lashings, but the wagon's bones were too tired. The repair held only partly.
exhausted_protection_narratives.scout_thirst: The Scout rode out at first light and rode back at dusk. No spring. No creek. Tomorrow we ride further.

push_on_tax.philosophy: Grueling pace for 3+ consecutive turns triggered a 30% exhaustion roll in v3.2. Steady pace had no consecutive-Push-On consequence at all. Kids who never stopped were not punished. v3.3 introduces a hidden counter that increments every Push On regardless of pace and resets on Hunt, Rest, or Forage. Narrative cues do the teaching — no on-screen meter.

narratives: The lead ox planted his feet at dawn and would not move. We rested.
The off-side ox lay down in the trace before sunrise. The wagon master spoke to him quietly. We waited.
Both lead oxen turned away from the yoke. They had walked enough. The trail master called the day.

thresholds.effect_summary: Random healthy member transitions to weakened (deterministic, no probability roll)
narratives: Sarah collapsed by the campfire. The trail demands rest.
The youngest in the train sat down on the wagon tongue at dusk and could not be roused. Fever rose with the moon.
Half the camp slept where they fell. One did not rise without help.

thresholds.effect_summary: wagon_hp -= 10, triggers Broken Wheel event with -1 backwards space
narratives: The wagon groans under endless miles. The rear axle cracks.
A spoke split as we pulled out of camp. The wheel went over and we lost half a day backing up.
The kingpin bent. We had to drop the load and walk the wagon backwards to a level patch.

thresholds.effect_summary: wagon_hp -= 18, all members morale -2, forced 2-day stop
narratives: The wagon, the team, the trail itself rebels. We are stopped.
Wagon, team, road — none of it would carry us another mile. Two days passed before we moved.
The wheels would not turn. The oxen would not pull. The morning passed and the morning after that. We slept in the dust.

push_on_tax.visibility: Hidden meter — no on-screen indicator. Narrative cues do the teaching. Kids should feel the strain, not stare at a meter.

bypass_penalties.philosophy: There is no mechanical incentive to stop at a fort or river crossing if the player is in a hurry. Bypassing is invisible. Per Nicholas: no mandatory stops — strategy must be preserved. Players choose to stop because logic makes it rational.

fort_bypass.condition: any team member at health < 7 (or in weakened/sick/injured/dying state)
fort_bypass.narrative: We saw the fort gates and pressed on. Sarah's cough deepened that night. We should have rested.

fort_bypass.condition: food below 7 days' worth of consumption
fort_bypass.narrative: We needed to resupply. We pushed on instead.
The wagon was tired. We should have stopped to rest it.
(pioneers without money skipped forts; this is honest)

river_bypass.trigger: Player chooses 'ford without choosing method' option
river_bypass.effect: auto-rolls fording outcome at +0.10 drowning probability
river_bypass.narrative: We did not pick a place to cross. We took the bank we found.

landmark_bypass.trigger: Wagon passes a landmark space without landing on it
landmark_bypass.effect: Loses that landmark's DYK card AND -1 morale
landmark_bypass.narrative: The landmark fell behind us before we even knew we were past it.

event_shadows_v3_3.philosophy: Item 10. Systems feel alive when one event raises probability of related ones. Real wagon trains experienced this — wagon damaged at river was more likely to break down on the next rough stretch; crew with one cholera case had others incubating. Per-wagon event_shadows array; each shadow is a 2-turn-window probability boost applied to a related event class. Full effect turn 1, half turn 2, gone turn 3. Multiple shadows of the same class stack additively up to a cap of x4.

shadows.narrative: The wagon was tougher than we thought, but it shows the wear.
The new axle is greener than the old. We listen for trouble.
Cholera spreads through dirty water — and we drink the same barrel.
The wagon is wet through; the harness is fouled.
Word travels among the road agents.
The wagon held but its bones are looser tonight.
The land we crossed is black ash. The trail markers are gone.

event_shadows_v3_3.decay: Full effect turn 1, half effect turn 2, gone turn 3

narrative_variants_v3_3.philosophy: Item 9. Each major event gets 3 random narrative variants for the default case. Mechanical effect identical, story different. Replay shows different prose. Profession-modifier overrides keep their existing narratives plus 2 variants where authored.

broken_wagon_wheel: The right rear wheel splinters as we cross a stretch of rocky ground. The wagon jerks to a halt at an angle. Without a fix, we go nowhere.
An iron tire band splits with a CRACK that startles the oxen. The wheel binds and locks. The wagon stops.
We feel the wagon lurch — a spoke has shattered crossing a creek bed. The wheel wobbles and we have to stop before it falls apart entirely.
river_crossing_disaster: The current was higher than it looked. Halfway across, the wagon tipped; a barrel of pork floated downstream and a man went under to his shoulders before we got him.
A submerged log caught the front axle and spun the wagon broadside. We lost half a day getting the wagon out and a quarter of our supplies were soaked through.
The ox at the off side stumbled in the deep water and the team lurched. We rescued the wagon but two pack mules were swept fifty yards down the bank before we could lasso them.
oxen_collapse: The lead ox went down in the trace at noon. We unhitched the team and stood over him an hour. He did not rise. We left his bones at the edge of the trail.
The off ox had been laboring all morning. He stopped and lay down at midafternoon. By dusk the wagon master had drawn his rifle. The team is one ox lighter tonight.
Both lead oxen refused to move at dawn. We stayed in camp until past noon, watered them, rubbed them down. The off ox never stood again.
cholera_outbreak: Sarah was well at breakfast. By noon she was vomiting. By dusk she could not lift her head. By morning the camp had dug a grave.
It came on the wind, the doctors used to say. Three of us were down by sundown. The wagon master sealed the water barrels and burned the bedding of the dead.
Cholera moves fast. The first sign was a child's stomach ache after the river crossing. By dawn three were dead. By the next sundown, two more.
food_spoilage: Weevils in the flour barrel. Maggots in the bacon. The whole side of pork had to go in the fire. We ate hardtack for three days.
A leak in the wagon canvas had let the rain in. By the time we noticed, the flour was paste and the sugar a brown ruin.
A skunk got into the food chest while we slept. We salvaged what we could. The smell traveled with the wagon for a week.
failed_hunt: The Hunter came back at dusk with an empty game bag. The deer had vanished from the country we were crossing. We ate beans tonight.
Two days of stalking and no shot. The Hunter spent the third day in camp, sharpening knives and saying nothing.
The buffalo herd we had spotted moved off in the night. The Hunter came back with one rabbit and a long apology.
severe_storm: The clouds had been building since noon. The first drops were the size of pebbles. By dark the wagon canvas was leaking in five places and the children were crying.
Thunder so loud the iron pots rang on their hooks. Lightning every few seconds. The horses panicked and broke their picket line.
Rain for thirty hours straight. The trail became a river. We sat in the wagon and watched the water rise to the floorboards.
snake_bite_classic: He was gathering firewood at dusk and reached blindly into a brush pile. The snake struck him on the wrist. The fang marks were deep.
She stepped over a fallen log and didn't see what was coiled on the other side. The strike came at her ankle. We carried her back to camp at a run.
The boy had been told to look before he reached. He had not. The rattlesnake's strike caught him on the back of the hand. The doctor worked over him through the night.
thief_in_camp: The strongbox had been nailed to the wagon bed. By dawn, the nails were still there but the box was empty. Someone in this train.
Two horses missing from the picket line. The picket pins were undisturbed. Someone had walked them out, slow and quiet.
Coffee, salt, and a side of bacon vanished from the wagon between dusk and dawn. No tracks, no witnesses. The trail brings desperate people.
contaminated_water: The spring tasted clean. By midday three of us were doubled over with the gripes. We boiled all our water for the rest of the week.
The creek where we filled our barrels at evening had a dead deer in it upstream. We did not see it until the morning.
The water at the seep was warm and faintly sweet. By moonrise, half the camp was sick. The Doctor said it was probably alkali.
lost_on_trail: The trail forked at a rocky outcrop. We chose left. By noon we were in a country we did not recognize. We turned back.
Heavy rain the night before had washed out the wagon ruts. We followed what we thought was the trail; it ended at a box canyon.
The wagon master had grown overconfident. He led us by what he called a shortcut. By the third day we admitted we were lost.

calamities_v3_3.philosophy: v3.2 had 11 daily shock types. After a handful of plays, kids saw the bad luck. v3.3 brings the catalog to 25 with 14 new entries and 3 narrative variants each. Cumulative daily shock probability across all 25 entries ~0.65-0.80. Most days have something; quiet days exist. Each new shock has a profession protection where appropriate — pioneers who prepared had advantages, but residual luck still mattered.

lightning_strike.effect: 1 member dying, oxen panic (-2 movement next turn)
narratives: A blue-white flash. The thunder is the same instant. The lead ox screams and bolts; the team scatters. We find one of our own face-down in the wet grass.
The strike came so close the air smelled of copper and burned hair. The wagon canvas glowed for an instant. We did not all rise from where we had thrown ourselves down.
Lightning split the cottonwood at the edge of camp clean down the trunk. One of our number had been sitting beneath it. The bark was still smoking when we reached them.

saleratus_poisoning.effect: If team forages without Cook/Doctor, 1 member sick
narratives: The white powder we had taken for baking soda was something else entirely. By midday, three of us were doubled over in the brush.
The crusted patches at the spring tasted clean enough. We were wrong. The cramps came before the moon was up.
Without anyone to read the soil, we used what we found. The fever followed at dawn.
Hailstones the size of hen's eggs hammered the canvas to ribbons in five minutes. Flour bags split open on the wagon bed. The oxen ran themselves bloody at the picket line.
We heard it on the wind before we saw it — a hissing roar that became a wall of white ice. By the time it passed, the wagon top was shredded and the spare flour was paste.
The first stones drove us under the wagon. The big ones came after, denting the iron tires and breaking a lantern. Sarah crawled out with a welt on her cheek the size of a coin.
The rattler was coiled in the long grass beside the wagon wheel. By the time we heard the buzz, the strike had landed.
He thought it was a stick. The fangs left two neat punctures above his ankle. The leg swelled within the hour.
She bent to draw water from the seep and the snake came out of the rocks like a whip. We carried her back to the wagon at a dead run.

sunstroke.effect: If water < 10 gal, 1 random member to weakened
narratives: The boy had been walking beside the wagon all morning without his hat. By noon he could not say his own name.
She fell where she stood, in the alkali dust, with the sun straight overhead. We poured the last cup of water on her face and prayed.
The heat was something we had no word for back east. By midday a man's footsteps would weave; by afternoon he would crumple.
The eldest had been told to stay clear of the rifle by the wagon seat. He did not. The shot went through the canvas and his own shoulder.
She was cleaning the muzzle of the rifle when it discharged. The ball cut a furrow across her forearm. The flash burned her sleeve to ash.
A dropped rifle on a rocky descent will sometimes fire. This one did. The ball passed through the wagon bonnet and grazed a man's neck on the other side.
The water at the seep was clear and cold. By morning the off-side ox lay dead in the trace, his belly swollen, his tongue black.
We did not know to look for the white crust at the spring's edge. The lead ox drank his fill at dusk; by sunrise he was past saving.
Alkali water kills slow and certain. The ox we lost at the seep had walked from Missouri without complaint. He died sixty miles from green grass.

prairie_fire.effect: If region is plains, food -15, wagon_hp -20
narratives: We saw the smoke at the horizon at noon. By dusk the line of fire was a mile wide and closing on us with the wind. We threw our gear into the wagon and drove the team straight through a creek bed.
The grass was so dry it caught from a single ember. By the time we smelled smoke the wagon's south side was already blistering.
A burnt-over country is what they call it after. The fire passed in less than an hour and left us standing in ash up to our boot tops.
We felt them in the ground before we saw them — a pulse like distant thunder. The herd came over the ridge in a brown river and ran straight through camp.
The buffalo did not see us until they were on us. The wagon tipped on its side; one of the children was knocked clean off her feet.
Five thousand head of cattle, panicked, in a single wave. The wagon held. The wagon tongue did not. We were two days mending.
The funnel came from a green sky. We had time to drag the children into a creek bed and lie flat. The wagon was three hundred yards away when the tornado found it.
We watched it cross the prairie like a black finger. We watched it turn toward us. We watched it pick up the canvas like a kerchief and tear it in two.
A wind that took the breath out of you. The wagon was on its side when we crawled out from under the wash bank. Half our supplies were in the tall grass a quarter-mile downwind.
The river bottom looked firm. The lead ox went down to his belly in three steps. We spent the day with ropes and planks and prayer.
The sand at the ford was wet but quiet. The wagon was halfway across when the off wheel sank to the hub.
We lost two crates of flour and a barrel of pork before we got the wagon free. The day was gone before the wheels were dry.
She was beside the wagon at the noon halt. She was not at the dusk camp. We searched until the moon set, calling her name across the swale.
He had wandered ahead chasing a prairie dog. The trail forked twice between him and us. We found him at sunrise, asleep in a cottonwood hollow, scratched but whole.
The youngest had a habit of falling behind. This time she did not catch up. We found her two miles back, sitting in the trail with a doll in her lap, weeping in silence.

ferry_rope_snap.effect: If on river crossing via ferry, 1 member possibly drowning
narratives: The hawser parted with a crack like a rifle shot. The ferry swung downstream broadside; one of our number went over the rail before we could grab him.
The frayed line gave at the worst moment — mid-crossing, the current at full pace. The ferry struck a sandbar and tilted. A man went under and did not surface for a long time.
The ropes had been showing wear since Fort Laramie. We thought one more crossing. We were wrong.
A spark from the cookfire found the keg. The blast threw the wagon onto its side and sent fire across the canvas in two seconds.
We had told the boy to stay away from the lantern by the powder. He did not. The flash blew out the wagon's tail panel and burned three of us before we cleared the bed.
The keg had been wedged near the iron stove for warmth in the cold nights. Warmth and powder do not keep company well. The blast was heard a half-mile down the trail.
Her jaw had ached for three days. By the fourth morning the swelling closed her left eye and the fever started.
He could not chew. He could not sleep. The pain was worse than the cholera he had survived at Fort Kearny.
A bad tooth on the trail is a bad tooth indeed. By the time the swelling went down he had lost nine pounds and three days of travel.

fortunes_v3_3.philosophy: v3.2 had 16 surprise events, mostly atmospheric. Real Oregon Trail pioneers had luck too. v3.3 brings the catalog to 35-40 with 24 new entries spanning four categories: help_from_people, nature_gives_back, timing_milestones, atmospheric_superstition. Calibrated to ~1 surprise per 2.5 turns; at least 3 fortune events per Short trail and 6 per Extended.

fortunes.name: Native Trade for Salmon
narratives: Three Cayuse riders met us at the river bend with strings of fresh salmon over their saddles. We traded a brass kettle for ten fine fish. They smiled at the children and rode on.
An elder of the Walla Walla people held up four salmon and pointed at our spare bridle. The trade was made by gestures and goodwill. We ate well that night.
The Yakama family at the salmon falls would not take coin. They wanted iron — a knife, a needle, a length of wire. We gave what we had and carried away food for a week.

fortunes.name: Native Trade for Horses
narratives: The Shoshone band at the green river had three pony horses to spare. The wagon master traded our two best buffalo hides for one. The new pony pulled steadier than any ox.
A Nez Perce trader walked the line of our wagons with a paint mare on a lead. She was worth twice what he asked. We paid in coin and shook hands twice.
We met the Crow at the headwaters. Their horses are the finest on the plains. We left them three dollars lighter and a horse heavier.

fortunes.name: Friendly Mountain Man with Map
narratives: He had been trapping these mountains for twenty years. He drew us a map on the back of a hide with a piece of charcoal. The shortcut he showed us saved us a day at Burnt River.
An old trapper rode into camp at dusk on a sorrel mule. He shared our supper. He left at dawn with a sketch of the trail ahead pressed into our hands.
He said his name was Smith but everyone here knew him by another. He marked the safe ford with a circle and the bad water with a cross. We followed his map and never lost the trail.

fortunes.name: Fellow Emigrants Share Tools
narratives: The Missourians camped beside us at Soda Springs were carrying spare iron tires. They lent us one for a half day's labor splitting their firewood. We rolled out with a stronger wagon than we came in.
An Ohio family at the next ford had a full blacksmith's kit. The father spent an afternoon tightening every bolt in our wagon. He would take no payment.
Three wagons from Illinois traveled with us for a week. We shared tools, food, and watch duties. The wagon was steadier for the whole stretch.

fortunes.name: Fort Doctor Cheap Visit
narratives: The fort surgeon at Laramie took one silver dollar for the visit. He bled the fever out of Sarah by morning and asked nothing more. A rare kindness.
The young doctor at Fort Hall had been an army medic. He worked for hours over the boy and charged us only what we could spare.
An old Whitman-trained physician at Fort Boise saw to two of our weakened in the same afternoon. Two coins for two souls. The trail can yet be kind.

fortunes.name: Fort Blacksmith Barters
fortunes.effect: -1 wagon damage, -2 hides instead of -3 money
narratives: The smith at Fort Bridger took two buffalo hides instead of cash for the wheel repair. Hides he could send east; coin he could not.
The Fort Hall blacksmith preferred trade. We left him a winter coat and a length of canvas. He left us a wheel as good as new.
An honest smith at Fort Boise looked over our hide stack, picked the two best, and re-rimmed both rear wheels for the price.

fortunes.name: Missionary Holds Healing Service
fortunes.effect: With Priest +0.10 recovery on next dying intervention; without, morale +1
narratives: A Methodist circuit rider had set up tent camp at the river. He held service at sundown. The whole camp came. The singing carried half a mile in the still air.
The Whitman station mission held a prayer service for the trail-weary. Sarah, who had been failing, sat upright through the whole hour and asked for water afterward.
Father Demers traveled west with us for two days. He prayed over the sick at every stop. By the time he turned back, two who had been failing were standing again.

fortunes.name: Eastbound Traveler with News
narratives: A westbound rider who had turned back rode into camp with a sack of mail and a bundle of newspapers. We sat by firelight reading news from St. Louis four months stale and felt nearly home.
The mail rider from Oregon City passed at noon. He had a letter for nobody we knew but he carried news of the trail ahead, fresh as morning bread.
Two trappers headed back east shared their fire and their stories. Both had known a man who had crossed before us. We learned more in one evening than we had in a week of guessing.

fortunes.name: Discarded Cache from Earlier Train
narratives: Behind a rock outcrop near the trail: a cache covered with a blanket. Salt pork, flour, a tin of ammunition, three good cookpots. Whoever left them had lightened their load. We did not waste their leavings.
A pile of stores beneath a juniper tree, marked with a stick driven upright. Earlier travelers had decided they could not carry it all. We carried what we could and blessed them.
A hidden cache near Independence Rock — flour, supplies, a fine Bowie knife. The note tucked under the lid said only 'God speed.' We kept the note and took what we needed.

fortunes.name: Wagon Master Joins Train
fortunes.effect: +0.05 to all profession protection rolls for 5 turns
narratives: An experienced wagon master who had crossed twice before fell in with our train at Fort Kearny. His advice on water and grass saved us a dozen errors over the next week.
A grizzled trail veteran in a black coat asked to join our train. He had led three crossings and lost no one. We were better for his counsel every day he rode with us.
The wagon master we had hired at Independence had quit at the Big Blue. The replacement we picked up at Fort Laramie knew the trail well enough to find every spring and avoid every alkali bog.

fortunes.name: Letter from Home Waiting at Fort
narratives: Two letters waiting at the fort post — one in mother's hand, one in sister Anne's. We read them both aloud at the fire. Half the camp wept. Half the camp wrote replies that night.
A letter at Fort Hall, four months old, from family back in Ohio. The news was old; the comfort was fresh. We tucked it into the family Bible and pressed on.
The fort sutler had been holding our mail for six weeks. Three letters and a small packet from grandmother. We did not open the packet until Christmas, six months on the trail.

fortunes.name: Bumper Buffalo Herd
narratives: The herd we struck was so large we counted by the acre, not the head. Our hunters dropped two cows in the first hour. Meat for two weeks, hides for a winter.
A buffalo herd a mile wide moved past our camp at evening. The hunters were waiting at first light. We dressed two carcasses by sundown and salted what we could.
We had not seen buffalo in six days. The herd that crossed our path on the seventh was uncountable. We took what we needed and let the rest go on.

fortunes.name: Soda Springs Carbonation
narratives: The water at the springs bubbled like cider straight from the rock. The children would not stop drinking it. Even the oxen tossed their heads at the strange tingle on their tongues.

fortunes.name: Snake River Salmon Run
narratives: The salmon were so thick at the falls a man could have walked across on their backs. We borrowed nets from the Nez Perce and pulled out enough for a week.
Salmon running so dense the river itself looked silver-flecked. We speared what we could, smoked the rest over green willow.
The Snake River salmon run was at its peak the day we arrived. The fish were as long as a man's arm. We ate well and tucked away salt-cured fillets for the days to come.

fortunes.name: Plum and Berry Season
narratives: The wild plum thickets along the Platte were heavy with fruit. The children picked baskets full. We ate plum pie that night for the first time since Independence.
A grove of chokecherry, gooseberry, and wild plum at the river bend. The whole camp took an hour with baskets and pails. We had pie filling for a month.
The June berry was just at peak. We picked four buckets in an afternoon. Flapjacks with berries that night — the children's faces purple at the corners.

fortunes.name: Camas Root Foraging
narratives: The camas meadow was bright blue with bloom. A Cayuse woman showed us how to dig the bulbs and bake them in a pit. They tasted like sweet chestnut and lasted three days.

fortunes.name: Wild Horses Worth Trading
narratives: We came on a small band of mustangs at a watering hole. The cowboy among us roped a young colt without trouble. The colt would fetch a price at any fort west of here.

fortunes.name: Antelope Hunt Success
narratives: The antelope crossed our line of sight at full speed. The Hunter took the lead doe with a single shot at three hundred yards. The whole camp ate fresh meat that night.
Pronghorn at the prairie's edge — a herd of forty. The Hunter laid down his bedroll, set up the rifle on a low rock, and took two before they scattered.
An antelope hunt at first light. The Hunter's dog flushed them from the sage and we picked up a yearling at fifty yards. Tender as veal.

fortunes.name: Calm Low-Water River Crossing
narratives: The river was running so low we crossed at a walk. The wagon hardly wet its axles. We reached the far bank dry and laughing.
We arrived at the ford on the lowest day of the season. The current was a hand's breadth deep at midstream. The crossing took twenty minutes and not a barrel was lost.
A drought year in the high country meant the river was nearly a creek. We rolled across without unhitching.

fortunes.name: Independence Rock by July 4th
narratives: We reached Independence Rock on the Fourth of July, just as the trail wisdom promised. The whole train carved their names into the granite that afternoon. We were on time.

fortunes.name: Mild Weather All Summer
narratives: The summer of '48 was kind. No prairie thunderstorm worth mentioning, no hail, no killing heat. The oxen kept their flesh; the children kept their spirits.

fortunes.name: Reunion with Old Friend
narratives: An old neighbor from back east had crossed two years before. He was at the fort, prosperous now, and stood drinks for the whole train. We talked into the night.

fortunes.name: Baby Born on the Trail
narratives: Mrs. Hawkins' baby came at three in the morning, beside the wagon, by lamplight. A girl, healthy and loud. They named her after the river: Platte. She rode the rest of the way in a sling on her mother's chest.

fortunes.name: Rainbow over Independence Rock
narratives: A rainbow arched clean over the granite dome at sunrise. Half the camp stopped what they were doing to look. The other half kept loading the wagon, but they all looked too.

personality_biases.philosophy: Lightweight bridge toward agent-based architecture without rebuilding the engine. Each member gets 5 hidden traits at character creation. Traits modify that member's behavior in stochastic rolls. Players see traits as flavor descriptors on the character card.

boldness.effect: Modifies choice-card outcome probabilities. Bold members succeed more on risky choices, less on safe ones.

frailty.effect: Multiplies that member's state-transition probabilities for degrade rules. Frail member catches cholera more easily.

faith.effect: Slows that member's morale decay. In dying-intervention, +0.05 recovery boost per Faith point above 5.

steady.effect: Modifies the success rate of that member's profession protection. Steady Hunter feeds the team more reliably.

lucky.effect: Small +/-0.02 bias on every stochastic roll involving that specific member.

personality_biases.steady_protection_multiplier: 1 + (steady - 5) * 0.04; range ~0.84 to ~1.20 on profession protection rolls
personality_biases.lucky_bias: +(lucky - 5) * 0.004; range -0.016 to +0.020 added to any stochastic roll involving this member
